SECONDARY (integrated): AAO Basic and Clinical Science Course (BCSC) Section 9, Uveitis and Ocular Inflammation, 2025-2026 edition - currently Chapters 5 (Diagnostic Considerations) and 6 (Therapy) integrated; remaining chapters (7,8,9,10,11,12,13,14,15) pending per agreed batch plan.
meta.purpose: Structured knowledge base for a clinical decision-support tool (differential diagnosis + treatment pathway). Not a substitute for clinical judgment or specialist referral.
meta.multi_source_policy: Where sources agree, content is merged with combined citation. Where sources genuinely differ, see top-level source_conflict_log - both positions are preserved, never silently blended.

input_schema.description: Suggested fields to collect from user in the tool before running matching logic
input_schema.revision_note: REVISED per user feedback: anatomic_class changed from single-select to MULTI-SELECT CHECKBOX (a case can be e.g. 'intermediate + anterior spill-over' simultaneously, not just one bucket). age_group added with clinically-defined buckets (user-confirmed).
fields: anatomic_class (MULTI-SELECT CHECKBOX, not dropdown - one or more of: anterior / intermediate / posterior / panuveitis; see anatomic_class_reference for scoring rules)
age_group (checkbox or single-select: anak/remaja/dewasa/lansia/unknown - see age_group_reference for bucket definitions)
systemic_symptoms (list of free text or checkboxes)

disease_category (uveitis/scleritis - added to distinguish true uveitis from the mechanistically-distinct scleritis category, which shares many systemic associations but different anatomy/pathophysiology)

age_group_reference.gap_resolution_note: User originally specified 'Dewasa 18-60, Lansia >65', leaving 61-64 undefined. Resolved by setting Lansia to start at 61 to keep buckets contiguous with no gap - adjust if a different resolution is preferred.
age_group_reference.data_policy: STRICT: age_group is only populated where Kanski Ch.12 gives an EXPLICIT age statement for that specific uveitis entity (not inferred from epidemiology, general clinical knowledge, or from an associated systemic disease's age unless Kanski states it applies to the uveitis itself). Where Kanski does not state it, age_group = ['unknown'] - per explicit user instruction, do NOT guess.
age_group_reference.scoring_implication: Because most entities are 'unknown' (18 of 27), age_group should be a LOW-WEIGHT bonus signal in scoring (not a hard filter/exclusion) - a case in an age bucket outside a disease's stated range should be soft-penalized, not eliminated, since age data here is genuinely sparse and Kanski itself doesn't treat age as a primary diagnostic axis for most entities.

dewasa: sarcoidosis_uveitis (juvenile form explicitly noted as rare)
entities_with_known_age_group.unknown_18_entities: aau_idiopathic_hlab27, sympathetic_ophthalmia, cmv_retinitis, progressive_retinal_necrosis, acute_retinal_necrosis, poh_syndrome, tb_uveitis, syphilitic_uveitis, lyme_uveitis, endogenous_endophthalmitis, herpes_simplex_anterior_uveitis, vzv_anterior_uveitis, cmv_anterior_uveitis, lens_induced_uveitis - Kanski does not give an explicit age range for these in Ch.12

anatomic_class_reference.revision_note: REVISED: anatomic_class is now an ARRAY per disease (multi-select checkbox in UI), not a single value. This allows representing real clinical patterns like 'intermediate uveitis with anterior spill-over' (e.g. toxoplasmosis: posterior + anterior) instead of forcing one bucket.
anatomic_class_reference.scoring_rule: If the DISEASE's anatomic_class array includes 'panuveitis', treat it as an implicit match against ANY single anatomic checkbox the user selects (anterior, intermediate, OR posterior) IN ADDITION to an exact 'panuveitis' checkbox match - because panuveitis by definition involves all compartments. If the disease array does NOT include 'panuveitis' but includes multiple specific compartments (e.g. toxoplasmosis = ['posterior','anterior']), score a match only for checkboxes the user actually selected among those listed - do not treat it as matching intermediate just because it's 'close' to posterior.
anatomic_class_reference.primary_vs_secondary_distinction: Where a compartment is explicitly described in Kanski as 'usually mild/minimal/absent' for a given disease (e.g. PRN's minimal anterior uveitis/vitritis, POHS's absent vitritis, PIC's usually-absent anterior uveitis), that compartment is DELIBERATELY EXCLUDED from the disease's anatomic_class array even though technically mentioned in the text - because its absence/mildness is itself a distinguishing diagnostic feature, not a compartment to score a positive match on. See each disease's anatomic_pattern_note for the specific reasoning.

examples.spill_over_pattern: toxoplasmosis_retinitis: ['posterior','anterior'] - primary posterior retinochoroiditis with COMMON anterior spill-over uveitis (Kanski's own term). This is the canonical example the user referenced.
examples.true_panuveitis_pattern: vkh_syndrome: ['anterior','intermediate','posterior','panuveitis'] - all compartments explicitly and prominently involved.
examples.deliberately_narrow_pattern: progressive_retinal_necrosis: ['posterior'] only - anterior/intermediate explicitly stated as MINIMAL, which is itself a key differentiator from CMV retinitis and ARN, so must NOT be checkbox-matched as a false positive.

kp_distribution_reference.purpose: Quick-reference on keratic precipitate (KP) distribution pattern as a differential-diagnosis clue, supplementing IOP. Included because slit-lamp exam is universally available (zero additional cost), making KP distribution a very high-value discriminative sign in resource-limited settings.
kp_distribution_reference.source_upgrade_note: CONFIDENCE UPGRADED: originally sourced from cross-referenced non-Kanski literature (Arlt's triangle concept + AAO EyeNet 'Viral Anterior Uveitis' + Ocular Immunology & Inflammation review). Now INDEPENDENTLY CONFIRMED by AAO BCSC Section 9 (2025-2026) Ch.5 itself: 'Small stellate KPs distributed diffusely on the cornea are usually associated with Fuchs uveitis syndrome or herpetic anterior uveitis' and 'distribution: diffusely over entire corneal endothelium, settled in a gravity-dependent triangular pattern in the inferior cornea (Arlt triangle), or focal and associated with corneal inflammation.' Two independent authoritative textbooks (Kanski's passing mention + AAO's explicit description) now support this pattern - confidence raised from 'literature-supported' to 'textbook-confirmed by 2 sources'.

arlt_triangle.description: Classic KP distribution: inverted/base-down triangle concentrated on the CENTRAL-INFERIOR cornea, apex pointing up. Caused by gravity + aqueous convection currents (rising along warm iris, falling along cooler cornea).
arlt_triangle.clinical_meaning: Suggestive of a granulomatous inflammatory process, but NOT pathognomonic and NOT etiology-specific on its own - can occur in both non-infectious (sarcoidosis, VKH) AND infectious (TB, syphilis) granulomatous disease.
typical_entities: aau_idiopathic_hlab27 (non-granulomatous, smaller KP but same gravity-driven inferior tendency)

diffuse_beyond_midline_pattern.description: KP distributed DIFFUSELY across the ENTIRE corneal endothelium, extending beyond the horizontal midline/equator (i.e. beyond the classic inferior Arlt's-triangle confinement), typically FINE/STELLATE in morphology rather than large mutton-fat.
diffuse_beyond_midline_pattern.clinical_meaning: This is the KEY EXCEPTION to the Arlt's-triangle rule and a discriminative clue toward a VIRAL or Fuchs etiology. Not pathognomonic alone, but a strong contributory signal, especially combined with elevated IOP, sectoral/patchy iris atrophy, and (for Fuchs specifically) absence of posterior synechiae. AAO BCSC Ch.5 explicitly confirms: fine stellate diffuse KP = Fuchs uveitis syndrome OR herpetic anterior uveitis.
diffuse_beyond_midline_pattern.supporting_note: Additional literature clue: fresh PIGMENTED KP (rather than the classic non-pigmented fine KP) further supports a viral cause when combined with diffuse/beyond-midline distribution.

linear_turks_line_pattern.description: KP deposited in a linear vertical formation on the inferior cornea (as opposed to the triangular Arlt pattern).
linear_turks_line_pattern.clinical_meaning: Recognized alternative distribution pattern, less specific/less commonly emphasized than Arlt's triangle or the diffuse viral pattern - low scoring weight, mainly useful to avoid mis-classifying it as 'diffuse beyond midline'.

aao_kp_descriptor_system.note: AAO BCSC Ch.5 formalizes KP description along 3 independent axes - useful as a more granular input schema than our current single kp_morphology + kp_distribution fields, worth considering for a future input_schema refinement:
aao_kp_descriptor_system.chronologic_evolution: Newly formed KPs are white and smoothly rounded; with age they become crenated (shrunken), pigmented, or glassy - so KP AGE/APPEARANCE over time is itself a clue (fresh vs old KP), distinct from size/morphology/distribution.

kp_distribution_reference.combination_rule_for_scoring: KP distribution should NEVER be used as a stand-alone diagnostic feature. It should be combined with IOP pattern, iris atrophy pattern (diffuse vs sectoral vs absent), presence/absence of posterior synechiae, and granulomatous morphology (fine vs mutton-fat) - both Kanski and AAO agree no single KP feature is diagnostic in isolation.

diseases.name: Acute Anterior Uveitis (Idiopathic / HLA-B27-associated)
key_features: Rapid onset pain, photophobia, redness, watery discharge
Ciliary injection, miosis
Fibrinous exudate common, hypopyon common (immobile, fibrin-rich)
Small non-granulomatous KP
HLA-B27 positive in ~50% otherwise-well AAU, ~90% if associated spondyloarthropathy

kp_distribution.detail: Small non-granulomatous KP, gravity-driven inferior concentration typical of standard AAU - contrast with the diffuse/beyond-midline pattern seen in viral anterior uveitis and Fuchs.
kp_distribution.source_note: General Arlt's-triangle principle applied to non-granulomatous AAU (cross-referenced literature, not Kanski-specific)

investigations: Sacroiliac joint X-ray if spondyloarthropathy suspected

treatment.first_line: Topical steroid (prednisolone acetate 1% or dexamethasone 0.1%) tapered over 5-6 weeks
treatment.severe: Intracameral tPA if dense fibrin; regional steroid if non-compliant
treatment.systemic: Rarely required for isolated anterior disease
treatment.imt_line_detail: Antimetabolites are usually FIRST-LINE IMT for anterior uveitis WITHOUT associated systemic inflammatory disease. TNF inhibitor may be considered SECOND-LINE, particularly in HLA-B27-positive patients. IMPORTANT FDA NUANCE: none of the TNF inhibitors are FDA-approved specifically for undifferentiated/isolated anterior uveitis - adalimumab's FDA indication covers noninfectious intermediate, posterior, and panuveitis only (not isolated anterior).
treatment.topical_frequency: Administration every 1-2 hours may be necessary for severe cases; periocular corticosteroids should NOT be used unless infectious etiologies have been thoroughly investigated/treated first

diseases.prognosis: Generally good if managed adequately; more variable if underlying systemic/ocular disorder present
diseases.age_group_note: AAO BCSC Ch.8 explicit: 'age at onset of HLA-B27-associated anterior uveitis ranges from 20 to 40 years' - firmly dewasa bucket. Overall AAU incidence rises further with age though (7/100k in <=14yo up to 220/100k in >=65yo per Ch.8) - i.e. AAU broadly is MORE common in older patients overall, but the HLA-B27-SPECIFIC subtype peaks younger (20-40).

epidemiology_aao.overall_aau_share: Anterior uveitis = >90% of uveitis cases in community-based practice (AAO Ch.8) - higher than the 70-80% figure from AAO Ch.5's broader epidemiology reference; both AAO figures, not conflicting with Kanski, just different study populations (community vs referral).
epidemiology_aao.hlab27_prevalence_in_aau: ~40-50% of AAU patients are HLA-B27 positive in some populations (general population HLA-B27 rate ~8% in US)
epidemiology_aao.penetrance: An HLA-B27-positive individual has only a 1-in-4 chance of developing spondyloarthritis or eye disease - most HLA-B27+ people never develop either.

diseases.critical_note_hlab27_testing: IMPORTANT: HLA-B27 positivity alone does NOT mean the uveitis IS HLA-B27-associated - the clinical picture must be CONSISTENT with the HLA-B27 pattern (unilateral/alternating, non-granulomatous, acute, high-grade with possible hypopyon/fibrin/PS). Example given by AAO: an HLA-B27+ patient with multifocal CHOROIDAL lesions does NOT have HLA-B27-associated uveitis, because choroidal lesions are not characteristic of this entity - the anatomic/clinical pattern rules it out regardless of the lab result.

ankylosing_spondylitis.treatment_note: NSAIDs first-line systemic; sulfasalazine second-line for joint disease (may reduce uveitis recurrence too); TNF inhibitors gaining favor as second-line for rapid effect

reactive_arthritis.aau_occurs_in: up to 10% of affected patients, may become bilateral/chronic
reactive_arthritis.trigger: diarrhea/dysentery (Shigella, Salmonella, Yersinia) OR urethritis (Ureaplasma urealyticum, Chlamydia) - arthritis begins within 30 days of infection in 80% of patients

ibd_related_arthritis.hlab27_note: IBD+AAU patients more likely HLA-B27+ with sacroiliitis; HLA-B27-NEGATIVE IBD patients more likely to develop INTERMEDIATE uveitis instead
ibd_related_arthritis.note: bowel disease can be asymptomatic and follow uveitis onset, not precede it

psoriatic_arthritis.pattern: tends to be INSIDIOUS and BILATERAL (contrast with classic HLA-B27 alternating-unilateral-acute pattern), more likely CHRONIC than other spondyloarthritis-associated uveitis, risk highest with psoriatic spondylitis specifically
psoriatic_arthritis.psoriasis_without_arthritis_note: AAU in psoriasis patients WITHOUT arthritis has distinct features: older age at onset than idiopathic/HLA-B27 uveitis, more likely bilateral and longer duration, POSTERIOR segment involvement can occur (unlike typical HLA-B27 AAU which is purely anterior)

diseases.name: Behçet Disease
key_features: Ocular BD is TYPICALLY ACUTE AND EXPLOSIVE, spontaneously resolving in a FEW WEEKS (not chronic like most other panuveitis entities) - potentially with substantial vision loss from retinal ischemia during the acute attack, not from persistent chronic inflammation
Most common ocular BD forms: posterior uveitis and panuveitis (50-80% of cases) - MORE severe in men
Characteristic: occlusive retinal vasculitis affecting BOTH arteries and veins -> multifocal CHALKY WHITE retinitis with surrounding hemorrhage
Optic nerve affected in 25% of ocular BD patients

Classic hypopyon: sudden-onset, SHIFTS with head position or DISPERSES with head shaking - eye may look relatively QUIET despite the hypopyon (unlike HLA-B27 hypopyon which is usually accompanied by more visible injection/pain)
Anterior uveitis MAY be the ONLY ocular manifestation in a minority of cases - don't require posterior involvement to consider BD
source_conflicts: See top-level source_conflict_log for: (1) retinal vasculitis type (arteritis+phlebitis per Kanski vs primarily-phlebitis per AAO Table 5-4), (2) TNF-inhibitor treatment line - NOW CONFIRMED via AAO Ch.10 as an explicit US-panel vs EULAR-panel disagreement, not just a temporal guideline evolution (see updated source_conflict_log entry).
associations: Turkey/Middle East/Far East ('Silk Road') higher prevalence
investigations: HLA-B51 (AAO note: 'rarely obtained and of limited value... not required for diagnosis' - diagnosis is primarily CLINICAL per ISGBD criteria)

treatment.immediate_indication: Behçet with retinal vasculitis = IMMEDIATE treatment, not early/delayed (both Kanski and AAO agree on this urgency)
treatment.kanski_eular_2008_position: Systemic steroid + azathioprine (2.5 mg/kg/day) as INITIAL management per EULAR 2008 guideline; infliximab/adalimumab as 'early' escalation per American Uveitis Society
treatment.aao_2025_2026_position: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab or infliximab) should be considered FIRST-LINE treatment for Behçet disease, per AAO expert consensus - not positioned as escalation after azathioprine failure
treatment.conflict_note: These two are NOT merged into one recommendation - see source_conflict_log. The AAO position is ~15+ years more recent and may reflect evolved consensus, but both are shown here rather than silently picking one.

aao_dosing_detail.adalimumab: SC 80mg day 1, 40mg day 8, then 40mg every other week (may increase to weekly)
aao_dosing_detail.infliximab: IV 5-10mg/kg at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 4-8 weeks (up to 20mg/kg in children); low-dose methotrexate 5-7.5mg/week co-administration reduces infliximab-induced lupus risk

treatment.severe: Cyclosporine (2-5mg/kg/day) or infliximab + azathioprine + steroids for >2-line VA loss or macular/vasculitis involvement (Kanski/EULAR)
treatment.alternative: Interferon-alfa (6 million IU/day SC, Kanski) - AAO cross-check: reports ~90% inflammation control in Behçet uveitis specifically (vs ~60% in non-Behçet uveitis), flu-like syndrome common (reduce with prophylactic acetaminophen), leukopenia/thrombocytopenia and DEPRESSION are notable adverse effects
treatment.avoid: Anticoagulants not recommended; azathioprine + IFN-alfa combo risk myelosuppression
treatment.us_vs_eular_confirmed: AAO Ch.10 EXPLICITLY confirms this is a genuine cross-organizational disagreement (not just outdated-vs-current guidance): 'An AMERICAN expert panel recommended TNF inhibitor therapy with infliximab or adalimumab as FIRST- or SECOND-line IMT for ocular BD. In contrast, a EUROPEAN LEAGUE AGAINST RHEUMATISM panel recommended using azathioprine (with corticosteroids) as FIRST-line IMT and cyclosporine or infliximab as SECOND-line treatment.' This directly matches and CONFIRMS what Kanski's EULAR-based recommendation already told us - so the 'conflict' is precisely: US expert consensus (AAO-aligned) vs European EULAR consensus (Kanski-aligned), both current and both legitimate, not one superseding the other.
treatment.colchicine_note: Colchicine is used for orogenital disease and ANTERIOR uveitis specifically, but is explicitly NOT effective for BD patients with POSTERIOR uveitis - so its utility is anatomically limited, not a general BD treatment.
treatment.interferon_practical_note: Interferon alfa-2a's LOW COST and COMPATIBILITY WITH CONCOMITANT LATENT TB (doesn't require TB clearance like TNF inhibitors do) make it commonly used as FIRST-LINE treatment OUTSIDE THE UNITED STATES specifically - a practical/access-driven geographic treatment pattern worth knowing, especially relevant for a clinician in Indonesia where interferon's TB-compatibility could be a meaningful practical advantage given TB endemicity.
treatment.corticosteroid_monotherapy_warning: AAO explicit: corticosteroid MONOTHERAPY should be AVOIDED in sight-threatening ocular BD because of risk of SEVERE REBOUND attacks during tapering - IMT should accompany steroids from early on, not be reserved only for steroid failure.

diseases.prognosis: Visual prognosis GUARDED worldwide. ~25% of chronic ocular BD patients have vision loss (from macular edema, occlusive vasculitis, optic atrophy, glaucoma). Adult males WORSE outcomes than females. Predictive factors for vision loss: posterior synechiae, persistent inflammation, elevated IOP, hypotony. Early aggressive systemic IMT improves visual prognosis.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski explicitly calls this 'panuveitis' pattern: AAU (often bilateral) + universal vitritis with active posterior disease + retinitis + retinal vasculitis. Per AAO's official SUN rule (see sun_official_reference), confirm true panuveitis requires no single predominant site AND choroidal/retinal lesions present - Behçet's presentation (AAU + universal vitritis + retinitis) satisfies this.
diseases.age_group_note: Kanski: 'The peak age of onset is the third decade.'

epidemiology_aao.prevalence: 20-421 per 100,000 in Turkey (highest); 13.5-30 per 100,000 in Asia; ~5.2 per 100,000 in the US
epidemiology_aao.age: Onset typically 3rd-4th decade, but can present in childhood or after age 50
epidemiology_aao.sex: Both sexes affected equally, but course tends to be MORE SEVERE in males
epidemiology_aao.high_risk_subgroup: Uveitis may be ESPECIALLY SEVERE in young males aged 15-25 years - AND diagnosis is often DELAYED in this specific demographic, a dangerous combination worth flagging

nonocular_manifestations_detail.oral_ulcers: White with red rims, 2-15mm, heal in 7-10 days with minimal scarring
nonocular_manifestations_detail.genital_ulcers: Deeper/larger than oral, heal WITH MORE SCARRING - scrotum/penis (men), vulva/vaginal mucosa (women)
nonocular_manifestations_detail.pathergy: ~40% of BD patients show cutaneous pathergy (sterile pustule at venipuncture/injection site) - NOT pathognomonic though
nonocular_manifestations_detail.systemic_vasculitis: Up to 25% - any vessel size, arterial occlusions/aneurysms, venous thrombosis/varices
nonocular_manifestations_detail.gi: GI ulcers (esophagus/stomach/intestines) in >50% of JAPANESE BD patients specifically - major morbidity source in that population
nonocular_manifestations_detail.neuro_bd: 30% of ocular BD patients have neuro involvement; conversely 10% of neuro-BD patients have ocular disease - the two can occur somewhat independently

diseases.fa_findings_aao: Islands of hyperfluorescence corresponding to CAPILLARITIS in posterior/midzone retina - these may or may not correspond to CLINICALLY VISIBLE retinitis, and are important indicators of active OR IMPENDING inflammation (i.e. FA can catch disease activity before it's clinically apparent - treating at this early FA-detected stage may prevent a full explosive attack).
diseases.pathogenesis_note: Necrotizing, neutrophilic, OBLITERATIVE perivasculitis (+/- fibrinoid necrosis) in capillaries/arteries/veins of ALL sizes - neutrophils, T lymphocytes, macrophages, plasma cells accumulate around vasa vasorum; local B-lymphocyte/plasma cell aggregates contribute to immune complex deposition; vascular endothelium shows upregulated adhesion molecule expression.

diagnostic_criteria_official.criteria: Recurrent oral aphthous ulcers (>=3 times/year) PLUS 2 of the following: (1) recurrent genital ulcers, (2) ocular inflammation, (3) skin lesions, (4) positive cutaneous pathergy test
diagnostic_criteria_official.hla_b51_caveat: HLA-B51 is STRONGLY associated with BD but is explicitly NOT one of the diagnostic criteria - this association is not reproducible across all populations and testing lacks sufficient sensitivity/specificity for routine diagnostic use (cross-reference our existing note on HLA-B51 being 'of limited value')

diseases.name: Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome (Fuchs Heterochromic Iridocyclitis)
key_features: Heterochromia iridis (affected eye often hypochromic)
Diffuse STELLATE KP over entire endothelium (pathognomonic pattern)
Diffuse iris atrophy with loss of crypts
POSTERIOR SYNECHIAE ABSENT (except rarely post-cataract surgery)
Eye virtually always WHITE even during exacerbation
Posterior subcapsular cataract extremely common
Glaucoma develops in up to 60%
Vitritis with anterior gel opacities can be dense
diseases.critical_note: Do NOT treat with long-term topical steroid for chronic low-grade inflammation - not helpful in this specific entity (exception to usual CAU management)

iop_pattern.detail: Glaucoma develops in up to 60% of involved eyes; typically a LATE manifestation but occasionally present at diagnosis. Several mechanisms suspected (fine radial angle vessels/small irregular peripheral anterior synechiae seen on gonioscopy - source of Amsler sign haemorrhage on AC incision).
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome section

kp_distribution.detail: Diffuse STELLATE KP over the ENTIRE corneal endothelium is one of the most classic/recognizable examples of KP distribution extending beyond Arlt's triangle - this is the reference entity most textbooks use to illustrate the 'exception to Arlt's triangle' pattern.
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (diffuse stellate KP) + cross-referenced literature explicitly using Fuchs as the classic example of diffuse/beyond-midline KP (non-Kanski)

investigations: Usually clinical diagnosis; investigate only to exclude alternatives

treatment.approach: Long-term monitoring for glaucoma/cataract; topical steroid short-term only for moderate/severe exacerbations
treatment.cataract_surgery: Increased complication risk (poor mydriasis, hyphema, zonular dehiscence)
treatment.glaucoma: May need surgical (drainage device/trabeculectomy with mitomycin-C)
treatment.cataract_surgery_exception: AAO explicit: cataract surgery/IOL implantation MAY be performed even if the eye has NOT been quiet for at least 3 months, because the anterior chamber cellular reaction in Fuchs may never completely subside with treatment anyway - this is a specific, named EXCEPTION to the general 3-month quiescence rule (cross-reference surgical_management_notes_aao_ch16_partial). Abnormal angle vessels may bleed during surgery causing postop hyphema, but outcomes are generally good.
treatment.topical_steroid_nuance: Topical steroids LESSEN inflammation but typically do NOT resolve it completely - aggressive treatment to eradicate the cellular reaction is explicitly NOT indicated (don't chase a cell count to zero in this entity). Cycloplegia usually unnecessary (consistent with near-absent PS risk already in our KB).

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Classified by Kanski under anterior uveitis; vitritis with anterior gel opacities can occur but entity remains structurally 'anterior' per SUN/Kanski chapter placement.
diseases.age_group_note: Kanski: 'a chronic non-granulomatous condition diagnosed at an average of 40 years of age.'
diseases.epidemiology_aao: Accounts for 2-3% of patient referrals to uveitis clinics (AAO BCSC Ch.8)
diseases.associations_note: AAO explicitly lists THREE infectious associations (rubella, CMV, Toxoplasma) - Kanski emphasized rubella most strongly with Toxoplasma as a secondary consideration; AAO gives CMV equal billing. Not a conflict, just AAO reflecting more recent/broader evidence.
diseases.diagnosis_basis: Diagnosis made on COMBINATION of: KP distribution pattern + presence of heterochromia + LACK of synechiae + minimal/no symptoms. Heterochromia may be SUBTLE in brown eyes (look carefully for iris stromal atrophy); in blue eyes the affected eye may actually become DARKER as atrophy progresses and posterior pigment epithelium shows through (opposite of the 'classic' lighter-eye teaching).

diseases.name: Uveitis in Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA)
key_features: INVARIABLY ASYMPTOMATIC - detected by slit lamp screening, NOT by complaint
Injection usually ABSENT even with severe uveitis
PS common in longstanding undetected cases
Band keratopathy and cataract common in severe cases
Oligoarticular JIA (ANA+ 75%) has highest uveitis risk (~20%)

screening_schedule.source: AAO BCSC Ch.8, citing 2019 American College of Rheumatology/Arthritis Foundation Guideline (SUPERSEDES/updates the older schedule previously in our KB from Kanski)
every_3_months_if_any_of: JIA diagnosed for 4 years or less
screening_schedule.every_6_to_12_months: Patients WITHOUT any of the above high-risk characteristics
screening_schedule.note: This 2019 ACR/Arthritis Foundation schedule is simpler/more current than the more granular but older schedule in Kanski Ch.12 (which had 2-monthly x6mo then 3-4mo intervals, etc.) - present the AAO 2019 version as the primary recommendation, Kanski's as a more conservative/detailed alternative.

treatment.principle: Goal: eliminate active inflammation AND prevent new complications. Even LOW-GRADE inflammation, if prolonged, causes unacceptable ocular morbidity - low threshold for escalation.
treatment.topical_steroid_threshold: If topical corticosteroids cannot be tapered to <=2x/day, OR steroid-induced ocular hypertension develops, OR inflammation is severe -> LOW THRESHOLD to begin systemic treatment
treatment.systemic_steroid_caution: Children uniquely at risk for GROWTH RETARDATION from premature epiphyseal closure with systemic corticosteroids - corticosteroid-sparing systemic IMT is usually necessary rather than prolonged systemic steroid
treatment.first_line_imt: LOW THRESHOLD to start methotrexate (antimetabolite) - well-tolerated, effectively controls uveitis, spares long-term corticosteroid complications
treatment.second_line: If uveitis inadequately controlled with MTX alone, ADD adalimumab (TNF inhibitor) - SYCAMORE trial (cross-reference immunomodulatory_drug_reference_aao) showed significant improvement with MTX+adalimumab combination
treatment.avoid: Etanercept - effective for JOINT inflammation in JIA but usually NOT effective for JIA-associated uveitis (matches Kanski's caution, cross-validated)
treatment.adjuncts: Short-acting mydriatics to keep pupil mobile/prevent PS in chronic flare; systemic NSAIDs may permit lower steroid dose
treatment.monitoring_intervals: Inactive uveitis (or <1+ AC cells): monitor every 3 months (unless tapering/discontinuing). See patient >=1 month after each topical medication change, within 2 months of each systemic treatment change.

cataract_surgery_protocol.source: AAO BCSC Ch.8 - specific to JIA-associated uveitis, more detailed than general uveitis cataract-surgery guidance
steps: Ensure uveitis well controlled >=3 months before surgery WITHOUT needing frequent topical or high-dose systemic steroids
Use systemic IMT preoperatively AND postoperatively, not just perioperatively
Place acrylic lens in capsular bag whenever possible
Monitor frequently postop, treat postoperative inflammation aggressively
Low threshold for IOL EXPLANTATION if chronic postoperative inflammation/recurrent cyclitic membranes develop
Advise patient on need for LIFELONG follow-up for late complications
cataract_surgery_protocol.band_keratopathy_first: Treat band keratopathy with EDTA chelation and ensure cornea well-healed BEFORE attempting cataract surgery
cataract_surgery_protocol.alternative_technique: Pars plana lensectomy with removal of lens complex + anterior vitreous can be used to avoid posterior capsule opacification

treatment.glaucoma_management: Medical therapy initially; surgical intervention often needed in severe cases. Standard filtering procedures usually UNSUCCESSFUL in this population - antifibrotic drugs or aqueous drainage devices usually required.

iop_pattern.detail: Glaucoma listed as a common serious complication (along with amblyopia, maculopathy, cyclitic membrane, phthisis). Not typically an early/acute diagnostic sign since JIA uveitis itself is asymptomatic.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Uveitis in JIA section

diseases.age_group_note: JIA is defined by Kanski as arthritis beginning before age 16. Oligoarticular JIA (most common form, highest uveitis risk) has 'a peak age of onset around 2 years' (anak). AAO BCSC Ch.8 adds: average age at onset of the UVEITIS specifically (not just JIA) is 6 years; uveitis generally develops within 5-7 years of joint disease onset but can occur up to 28 years after arthritis onset.

ilar_subtype_breakdown_aao.source: AAO BCSC Ch.8, International League of Associations for Rheumatology (ILAR) classification

oligoarticular.detail: <=4 joints in first 6 months, may have NO joint symptoms; uveitis develops in 25-35% of these patients, mostly within first 4 years after JIA diagnosis. Girls <5yo who are ANA+ = highest uveitis risk subgroup.

polyarticular_rf_positive.detail: RF-positive individuals may NOT develop uveitis - lower risk subtype

enthesitis_related_arthritis.detail: IMPORTANT PATTERN DIFFERENCE: unlike other JIA subtypes (which cause CHRONIC often-asymptomatic uveitis), ERA-associated uveitis is more likely ACUTE and RECURRENT with pain/redness/photophobia - resembles HLA-B27 AAU pattern more than typical JIA-uveitis pattern. Enthesitis, axial disease, HLA-B27 positivity common in this subtype.

systemic_arthritis_stills_disease.detail: fever, evanescent rash, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly; joint involvement may be minimal/absent initially; UVEITIS IS RARE in this subtype - contrast with oligoarticular which has the highest uveitis risk

undifferentiated.detail: does not meet criteria for one category, or meets criteria for more than one

diseases.hla_associations_aao: HLA-DRB1*11 and *13 may be associated with INCREASED risk of uveitis among JIA patients (in addition to ANA positivity as the main established risk marker)
diseases.prognosis: Because uveitis is frequently asymptomatic, profound ocular damage can occur before detection - this is why the screening schedule (not symptom-triggered exams) is critical. Long-term prognosis depends heavily on extent of structural complications AT TIME OF DIAGNOSIS. Frequent/severe sequelae: band keratopathy, cataract, glaucoma, macular edema, chronic hypotony, phthisis bulbi.

diseases.name: Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) Syndrome
diseases.course: phasic: prodromal -> acute uveitic -> convalescent -> chronic recurrent
key_features: Prodromal: meningitis-like symptoms, tinnitus/vertigo/deafness
Acute: bilateral granulomatous anterior + multifocal posterior uveitis, Dalen-Fuchs nodules, exudative retinal detachment
Convalescent: alopecia, poliosis, vitiligo, 'sunset glow' fundus, Sugiura sign
OCT: subretinal septae; height of subretinal fluid correlates with disease activity
diseases.diagnostic_criteria_table: Complete VKH = criteria 1-5; Incomplete = 1-3 + (4 or 5); Probable (isolated ocular) = 1-3 only

kp_distribution.detail: As a granulomatous panuveitis, VKH's anterior component classically shows mutton-fat KP with the typical gravity-driven inferior/Arlt's-triangle concentration - unlike the diffuse pattern seen in viral entities.
kp_distribution.source_note: General granulomatous-KP principle applied to VKH's granulomatous anterior uveitis component (cross-referenced literature, not Kanski-specific)

investigations: Lumbar puncture if uncertain (transient lymphocytic pleocytosis, melanin-laden macrophages)

treatment.initial: High-dose systemic corticosteroids followed by taper. Within 3 months, taper to <=10mg/day, then VERY SLOWLY (often 1mg/month) over the following 6-12 months - the SLOW taper specifically aims to PREVENT progression to late-stage VKH.
treatment.local_steroid_caution: Use local (periocular/intravitreal) steroid injections with CAUTION - VKH patients have an increased risk of developing elevated IOP
treatment.imt: Most practitioners initiate systemic IMT AT ONSET (not after steroid failure) to reduce risk of chronic inflammation -> pigment loss, chorioretinal atrophy, vision loss. Treatment reduces risk of CNV and subretinal fibrosis too.
treatment.window_of_opportunity: AAO cites literature on 'catching the therapeutic window of opportunity in early initial-onset VKH' - suggesting AGGRESSIVE EARLY treatment specifically (not just eventual treatment) can potentially CURE the disease, i.e. prevent the chronic/recurrent late-stage pattern entirely.

diseases.urgency_note: Early treatment strongly recommended (listed among 'early treatment' indications)
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Acute uveitic phase: bilateral granulomatous anterior + multifocal posterior uveitis + vitritis + papillitis + exudative retinal detachment.
diseases.age_group_note: Kanski: 'usually in the 2nd and 5th decade of life.'

epidemiology_aao.tertiary_referral_prevalence: 6-8% of uveitis cases in Asia, 1.2% in Middle East, 1-4% in North America, 2-4% in Brazil
epidemiology_aao.hla: HLA-DR4 strongly associated in Japanese patients; HLA-DR1 or HLA-DR4 in Hispanic patients (Southern California)

diseases.prodrome_detail: Headache, nausea, meningismus, dysacusia, tinnitus, fever, orbital pain, photophobia, skin touch hypersensitivity. Focal neuro signs RARE but possible (cranial neuropathies, hemiparesis, aphasia, transverse myelitis, ganglionitis). CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose in >80% of patients. Central dysacusia (high frequencies) in ~30% early, usually improves in 2-3 months.
diseases.staging_note: AAO Ch.10 clarifies: the traditional '4 stages of VKH' description is now understood as a CONTINUUM from early- to late-stage disease (not discrete sequential stages) - multimodal imaging supports this continuum model.
early_stage_features: Bilateral granulomatous anterior uveitis (rare unilateral cases exist)
Multiple serous retinal detachments, often small/shallow forming a CLOVERLEAF pattern around posterior pole, may coalesce into bullous exudative detachments
IOP may be ELEVATED (ciliary body edema/annular choroidal detachment causing AC shallowing) OR LOW (ciliary body shutdown)
late_stage_features: Smoldering panuveitis: AC inflammation, vitritis, choroiditis/thickening
Recurrent serous RD UNCOMMON in late stage (contrast with early stage)
Sequelae: posterior subcapsular cataract, glaucoma, CNV, subretinal fibrosis
Perilimbal vitiligo (Sugiura sign): up to 85% of JAPANESE patients, RARELY seen in White patients - significant ethnic variation
Integumentary changes (vitiligo, alopecia, poliosis) in up to 30% - LOWER incidence in Hispanic patients specifically

sun_2021_classification_criteria.source: SUN Working Group. Classification criteria for Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease. Am J Ophthalmol. 2021;228:205-211. (SUPERSEDES the older 2001 International Committee 'complete/incomplete/probable' criteria we previously referenced from Kanski)

early_stage_criteria.option_1: Evidence of VKH (posterior uveitis with serous detachment) AND no history of penetrating trauma/vitreoretinal surgery before onset: (a) serous/exudative retinal detachment AND (b) multiloculated appearance on FA or septa on OCT
early_stage_criteria.option_2: Panuveitis (with evidence of choroidal involvement on exam/FA/ICGA/OCT) with >=2 of the following within 4 weeks, and no trauma/surgery history: headache, tinnitus, dysacusis, meningismus, OR CSF pleocytosis
exclusions: Evidence for sarcoidosis (bilateral hilar adenopathy on imaging OR biopsy showing noncaseating granulomas)

late_stage_criteria.requires: History of early-stage VKH PLUS #1 or #2 below
late_stage_criteria.option_2: Uveitis (chronic anterior, OR anterior+intermediate, OR panuveitis with multifocal choroiditis/Dalen-Fuchs-like nodules) AND at least 1 of: vitiligo, poliosis, or alopecia

diseases.name: Sympathetic Ophthalmia (SO)
diseases.course: onset 2 weeks to 3 months post-injury in 65%
key_features: Exciting eye (injured/postsurgical) typically shows MORE SEVERE inflammation than sympathizing eye, at least initially - but prior enucleation/phthisis/corneal opacity may preclude observing this
Early symptoms: near vision loss (from ciliary body edema), photosensitivity, pain, floaters, photopsia, metamorphopsia
RARE VKH-like extraocular signs possible: CSF pleocytosis, sensorineural hearing disturbance, alopecia, poliosis, vitiligo
IOP may be ELEVATED (trabeculitis) OR LOW (ciliary body hyposecretion) - can go either direction
Choriocapillaris CLASSICALLY SPARED (at least early stage) on histology - distinguishing histologic feature
diseases.epidemiology: Incidence ~0.2-0.5% after penetrating injury; 0.19% meta-analysis; 0.01% after intraocular surgery
diseases.prevention: Enucleation of severely injured eye within 10-14 days considered historically effective if hopeless visual prognosis

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (suspect in ANY bilateral uveitis after ocular trauma/surgery)
B-scan ultrasonography / EDI-OCT for choroidal thickening
investigations.note: Key differentiator from phacoantigenic uveitis

treatment.preventive: For primary penetrating trauma with a grossly disorganized globe and NO visual potential, enucleation WITHIN 2 WEEKS of injury may reduce SO risk. Enucleation preferred over evisceration (controversial) since it removes more residual uveal tissue.
treatment.once_so_developed: Enucleation of the exciting eye does NOT reduce inflammation in the sympathizing eye once SO has developed - and the exciting eye may eventually become the BETTER-SEEING eye, so don't rush to enucleate after SO onset assuming it helps the fellow eye.
treatment.medical: Local and/or systemic corticosteroids + systemic IMT; LONG-TERM therapy needed in most patients; ALKYLATING AGENTS if severe/vision-threatening
treatment.prognosis: With prompt aggressive systemic therapy: 60% achieve final VA of 20/40; up to 25% decline to 20/200 or worse in the sympathizing eye

diseases.prognosis: 75% retain VA better than 6/60 with aggressive therapy; relapse in 50% (may be delayed years) - long-term follow-up mandatory
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski explicitly describes SO as 'bilateral granulomatous panuveitis'; anterior uveitis in both eyes + multifocal choroidal infiltrates (posterior).
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski; onset is defined relative to time since trauma (2 weeks-3 months in 65% of cases), not patient age.

epidemiology_aao.timing: 1/3 of patients develop SO within 3 MONTHS of ocular injury/surgery; <1/2 within 1 YEAR
epidemiology_aao.trend_shift: Historically more common after ACCIDENTAL trauma than surgery; but due to improved emergency surgical care (esp. combat medicine), SO is now MORE commonly associated with ocular SURGERY, particularly pars plana vitrectomy
epidemiology_aao.rare_triggers: Can rarely occur after NONPENETRATING surgery: transscleral ciliary body laser, panretinal photocoagulation, radioactive plaque therapy

diseases.relationship_to_vkh: SO and VKH have NEARLY IDENTICAL immunogenetics and share many pathologic ophthalmic features (including Dalen-Fuchs nodules) - by DEFINITION, VKH patients have NO history of ocular injury, which is the key distinguishing criterion between the two.
diseases.differential_note: Phacoantigenic uveitis (lens_induced_uveitis) can occur CONCURRENTLY with SO and shares similar features; in the absence of SO, phacoantigenic uveitis is differentiated by ABSENCE of choroidal thickening.

diseases.name: Sarcoidosis (ocular)
diseases.course: AAU in acute-onset systemic disease; CAU (granulomatous) in older/chronic pulmonary disease
key_features: Mutton-fat KP, Koeppe (pupillary margin) and Busacca (iris stroma) nodules, white anterior vitreous clumps - classic anterior granulomatous triad
Glaucoma from sarcoid uveitis complications is a POOR PROGNOSTIC SIGN for severe vision loss - flags a more aggressive disease course
Vitreous 'string of pearls' (semi-linear strands of cells) in addition to snowballs
Dalen-Fuchs nodules can occur (shared histologic finding with VKH and sympathetic ophthalmia)
Candlewax drippings (taches de bougie) - irregular nodular granulomas along venules
Retinal macroaneurysms may develop
Sarcoidosis can cause inflammatory OPTIC NEUROPATHY even WITHOUT uveitis - don't require uveitis to be present to consider sarcoid in unexplained optic neuropathy

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.note: Abnormal in up to 90% of sarcoidosis patients AT SOME POINT (may not persist throughout disease course); bilateral hilar +/- mediastinal lymphadenopathy strongly suggestive

investigations.name: High-resolution chest CT
investigations.note: More sensitive than CXR when clinical suspicion high - but weigh radiation/cost vs likelihood of changing management if no other extraocular signs present

investigations.name: Serum ACE and/or lysozyme
investigations.note: Combined sensitivity 61%, specificity 88% for elevated ACE, lysozyme, or both (biopsy-proven/probable cases). CAVEAT: ACE unreliable if patient takes an ACE-inhibitor medication (falsely low) - check lysozyme instead in that case. Lysozyme can be falsely elevated in chronic renal failure/end-stage renal disease.

investigations.name: Bronchoscopy with BAL and/or endobronchial ultrasound-guided transbronchial needle aspiration
investigations.note: Characteristic finding: mononuclear alveolitis with increased CD4+ lymphocytes

investigations.name: Screening ECG
investigations.note: Some advocate obtaining in ALL suspected ocular sarcoidosis patients - cardiac sarcoidosis can be ASYMPTOMATIC and cause sudden cardiac death, so this could be lifesaving even without cardiac symptoms

investigations.name: FDG-PET
investigations.note: Expensive, high radiation - reserve for cases where diagnosis would truly change management (distinguishing neoplastic/inflammatory/infectious)

investigations.name: Review of systems for neuro symptoms
investigations.note: New headaches, seizures, bowel/bladder dysfunction, gait disturbance, paresthesias

treatment.initial: Local and systemic corticosteroids for initial/short-term control. Topical cycloplegia to prevent synechiae until anterior segment controlled. Posterior disease usually needs SYSTEMIC steroids (intravitreal can be adjunctive but leaves systemic disease untreated).
treatment.imt_indication: Persistent/recurrent vision-threatening disease despite corticosteroids, OR complications from steroid use
treatment.imt_options: Methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, azathioprine, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, LEFLUNOMIDE (new option vs what we had). TNF inhibitors adalimumab/infliximab effective for sarcoid uveitis specifically.
treatment.paradox_warning: Paradoxically, a SARCOID-LIKE SYNDROME is itself a rare adverse effect of TNF inhibitor treatment - cross-reference drug_induced_uveitis entity.
poor_prognosis_factors: DELAYED involvement of a uveitis specialist - timely referral itself is a modifiable prognostic factor

iop_pattern.detail: Glaucoma listed among typical complications alongside cataract, PS, band keratopathy, vitreous haemorrhage, maculopathy.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Sarcoidosis Investigation/Ocular features section

kp_distribution.detail: Mutton-fat KP is one of the IWOS 7 key signs of intraocular sarcoidosis; classically distributed gravity-driven inferior/Arlt's-triangle pattern, distinct from the diffuse pattern of viral anterior uveitis.
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (IWOS mutton-fat KP sign) + cross-referenced literature on Arlt's-triangle association with granulomatous disease (non-Kanski)

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski: ocular sarcoidosis can present as anterior, intermediate, posterior, or panuveitis depending on the case.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: usual onset 20-50yo, BUT a SECOND peak exists at 50-65yo (late-onset sarcoidosis) - late-onset patients are MORE likely to have uveitis and LESS likely to have asymptomatic CXR findings than younger patients. Investigate sarcoidosis as a uveitis cause at ANY age. Pediatric cases (< 5yo) are rare and present differently (more cutaneous/joint, less pulmonary) - overlaps clinically with JIA and Blau syndrome in this age group.

organ_involvement_rates.cardiac_clinically_detectable: 5% (but autopsy studies suggest 20-30% true involvement - likely underdiagnosed)

epidemiology_aao.mortality: ~5% overall; most common cause of sarcoidosis death is pulmonary disease, followed by cardiac disease
epidemiology_aao.race_disparity: US prevalence: 36/100,000 in Black individuals vs 11/100,000 in White individuals; cumulative lifetime risk 2.4% (Black) vs 0.85% (White). Lower socioeconomic status associated with increased severity/morbidity/mortality, likely reflecting limited care access and physician implicit bias (AAO explicitly names this health equity factor).
epidemiology_aao.geographic: Highest prevalence in northern European countries (40/100,000)
epidemiology_aao.familial_risk: Siblings of sarcoidosis patients have 5-FOLD increased risk
epidemiology_aao.ocular_specific: Uveitis is the MOST COMMON manifestation of ocular sarcoidosis (61% of ocular sarcoid patients). Sarcoidosis accounts for 5-10% of ALL uveitis cases in US tertiary care centers. Most sarcoid-uveitis (71%) is ANTERIOR. Posterior segment involvement occurs in up to 20% of ocular sarcoidosis cases.

acute_presentation_syndromes.lofgren_syndrome: Erythema nodosum + febrile arthropathy + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + acute iritis. Responds well to systemic steroids, GOOD long-term prognosis.
acute_presentation_syndromes.heerfordt_syndrome: NEW to our KB - 'uveoparotid fever': uveitis + parotitis + fever + facial nerve palsy - another distinct acute sarcoidosis presentation pattern.

diseases.blau_syndrome_crossref: Blau syndrome (familial juvenile systemic granulomatosis) shares granulomatous dermatitis+arthritis+uveitis with sarcoidosis and is caused by NOD2 gene mutations (this CONFIRMS the investigation note we flagged as 'needing verification' on our blau_syndrome entity - now verified via AAO). Ophthalmic presentation resembles ocular sarcoidosis; suspect Blau when there's a FAMILY HISTORY of granulomatous disease, especially in a young child.

diseases.name: Ocular Toxoplasmosis
diseases.granulomatous: spill-over anterior uveitis may be granulomatous or resemble FUS
key_features: Classic: focal white retinochoroiditis adjacent to a PIGMENTED retinochoroidal SCAR ('satellite lesion') with moderate overlying vitritis ('headlight in the fog')
In the ABSENCE of an old scar, recently acquired disease often presents as focal retinochoroiditis alone (de novo lesion)
Up to 20% of patients have ACUTELY ELEVATED IOP at presentation
Kyrieleis arteriolitis: perivasculitis near active lesion with diffuse venous sheathing + SEGMENTAL ARTERIAL PLAQUES
Lesions near/involving the optic nerve may be MISTAKEN for optic neuritis

ATYPICAL presentations: PUNCTATE OUTER RETINAL TOXOPLASMOSIS (PORT - small multifocal outer retinal lesions with subretinal exudation, scant overlying vitritis), unilateral pigmentary retinopathy mimicking RP, neuroretinitis, can SIMULATE herpetic ARN

Immunocompromised/older patients: MORE SEVERE atypical picture (large/multiple/bilateral lesions +/- scars) - especially if patient received corticosteroids WITHOUT concomitant antiparasitic coverage (can cause widespread retinal necrosis progressing to panophthalmitis/blindness/eye loss)
diseases.epidemiology: Responsible for 20-60% of posterior uveitis depending on country (85% in Brazil)
investigations: Toxoplasma IgG (exposure) / IgM (recent, critical in pregnancy)
PCR intraocular fluid (16-67% sensitive, highly specific)

indications_for_treatment: Sight-threatening lesion (macula, papillomacular bundle, optic disc, major vessel)
treatment.classic_triple_therapy: Pyrimethamine (loading 75-100mg 1-2 days then 25-50mg/day x4wks) + sulfadiazine 1g QID + oral folinic acid + prednisolone 1mg/kg (started after 24-48h antimicrobial)
alternatives: Co-trimoxazole (low-cost, better tolerated, maybe slightly less effective)
Clindamycin 300mg QID added or instead of pyrimethamine
Intravitreal clindamycin+dexamethasone (as effective as triple therapy in reactivated infection)
treatment.duration: 4-6 weeks treatment, complete healing over 4-6 months
treatment.prophylaxis: Co-trimoxazole 160/800mg TID x12 months reduces recurrence from 22% to 3% for up to 3 years
treatment.pregnancy: Careful selection; intravitreal therapy or azithromycin+clindamycin+/-prednisolone may be appropriate; spiramycin for prevention of transmission
treatment.avoid: Depot intra/periocular steroids (e.g. triamcinolone) - can cause uncontrolled progression
treatment.intravitreal_option_precise: Intravitreal clindamycin (1mg/0.1mL) +/- periocular dexamethasone (400ug/0.1mL) - off-label, can combine with systemic therapy OR use as monotherapy if systemic therapy not tolerated (cross-reference appendix_b_intravitreal_antimicrobial_reference_aao - dosing matches exactly)
treatment.corticosteroid_protocol_precise: In immunocompetent patients: systemic corticosteroid (0.25-0.75mg/kg, typically <=60mg/day) may be considered AFTER 48 HOURS of antimicrobial therapy - NEVER before/without antimicrobial coverage. Long-acting PERIOCULAR/INTRAVITREAL corticosteroid formulations (e.g. triamcinolone) are CONTRAINDICATED due to risk of potentiating severe retinitis progressing to panophthalmitis/blindness/eye loss. Topical steroids CAN be used liberally for anterior segment inflammation. Continue steroid 3-5 weeks or until early cicatrization signs; maintain antimicrobial coverage for the ENTIRE steroid period.
treatment.hiv_context: HIV/AIDS + toxoplasmosis: EXTENDED systemic treatment needed given frequent cerebral involvement association (56% of cases) and high recurrence rate off medication. Atovaquone + pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine synergy may reduce dose/toxicity for AIDS patients specifically.
treatment.secondary_prophylaxis: Long-term intermittent trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (160/800mg 3x/week) DECREASED reactivation risk over a 20-month study period in patients with recurrent disease. Prophylactic antimicrobial around intraocular SURGERY (especially cataract surgery) in patients with inactive scars NEAR the disc/fovea has been raised as a consideration given reports linking cataract surgery to reactivation risk - but NO CONSENSUS yet on this approach or optimal regimen.

iop_pattern.detail: 'Spill-over' anterior uveitis is common in ocular toxoplasmosis. It may be granulomatous or resemble FUS. Elevated IOP may follow.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Toxoplasmosis Ocular features section

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: PRIMARILY posterior (focal/multifocal retinochoroiditis). 'Spill-over' anterior uveitis is explicitly described as common in Kanski - this is the classic textbook example of posterior disease with anterior spill-over, not a true panuveitis.
diseases.age_group_note: Kanski: 'First presentation with symptomatic ocular infection occurs at an average age of 29 years.' Note: postnatal childhood acquisition accounts for >50% of childhood cases and congenital scars may be found incidentally at any later age - symptomatic presentation age is what is explicitly stated.

epidemiology_aao.clinical_significance: MOST COMMON form of infectious posterior uveitis in BOTH adults and children
epidemiology_aao.us_seroprevalence: CDC: 11% of US population >=6yo infected; ~2% of THOSE develop ocular toxoplasmosis
epidemiology_aao.brazil_contrast: ~80% of southern Brazil population infected, up to 18% of THOSE develop eye disease - dramatically higher ocular-disease rate than US, possibly due to GREATER GENOTYPIC HETEROGENEITY of parasites in Brazil vs North America (regional strain virulence differences)
epidemiology_aao.pregnancy_risk_us: NHANES 2009-2010: age-adjusted seroprevalence 9.1% in women of childbearing age (15-44yo). Primary infection during pregnancy: ~0.2-1.1 per 1000 pregnancies (800-4400 US women/year among 4 million annual pregnancies)
epidemiology_aao.transplacental_transmission: 40% of primary maternal infections result in congenital infection; transmission risk HIGHEST in 3rd trimester, but SEVERITY of fetal disease is INVERSELY proportional to gestational age (earlier infection = worse disease, later infection = higher transmission but milder/latent disease)
epidemiology_aao.maternal_reinfection_note: Chronic/recurrent maternal infection during pregnancy does NOT usually confer major congenital risk (maternal immunity protects) EXCEPT if the mother is reinfected with a NEW MORE VIRULENT STRAIN despite existing immunity

congenital_presentation.sabin_tetrad: Retinochoroiditis + hydrocephalus/microcephaly + intracranial calcifications + cognitive impairment - occurs in <10% of infected children (classic tetrad is actually UNCOMMON)
congenital_presentation.most_common_finding: Retinochoroidal lesions - up to 80% of congenital cases, bilateral in ~85%, posterior pole/macula predominant
congenital_presentation.delayed_presentation: Up to 85% of infected children develop retinochoroiditis after a MEAN of 3.7 YEARS (not necessarily at birth) - 25% of these become blind in one or both eyes
congenital_presentation.treatment_recommendation: Most experts recommend antiparasitic therapy during the FIRST YEAR OF LIFE for congenital toxoplasmosis REGARDLESS of presence of ocular/systemic signs - treat even if asymptomatic

diseases.acquired_vs_congenital_paradigm_shift: IMPORTANT: toxoplasmosis after infancy was PREVIOUSLY thought to represent reactivation of congenital disease, but it is NOW RECOGNIZED that a SUBSTANTIAL proportion (up to 2/3 in one study) of ocular toxoplasmosis in children/adults represents NEWLY ACQUIRED postnatal infection, not congenital reactivation - this shifts the differential/counseling approach for adult-onset cases.

diagnosis_serology_detail.igg: Appears after first 2 weeks, typically detectable FOR LIFE, CROSSES placenta - supports diagnosis in appropriate clinical context but doesn't distinguish old vs new infection alone
diagnosis_serology_detail.igm: Increases early in ACUTE phase, typically detectable <1 YEAR, does NOT cross placenta - IgM confirms CONGENITAL infection in newborns but indicates NEWLY ACQUIRED disease in adults

always_treat: Pregnant women with recently acquired disease
treatment_indications_aao.immunocompetent_observation_option: Some clinicians OBSERVE small peripheral lesions without notable vision decrease or vitritis in immunocompetent patients - firm evidence that antimicrobial therapy alters natural history is LIMITED in this population

treatment_table_aao.source: AAO Table 12-2, Systemic Treatment of Toxoplasmosis

classic_triple_therapy.adverse_effects: Pyrimethamine: myelosuppression/leukopenia/thrombocytopenia. Sulfa: rash/GI intolerance/crystalluria/kidney stones/Stevens-Johnson syndrome.

treatment_table_aao.azithromycin_alone_or_substitution: 500mg daily, OR 500mg x1 day then 250mg daily
treatment_table_aao.congenital_toxoplasmosis_dosing: Pyrimethamine 2mg/kg x1 day then 1mg/kg daily; sulfadiazine 50mg/kg BID; folinic acid 7.5mg/day
treatment_table_aao.pregnancy_dosing: Spiramycin 400mg 3x/day - reduces tachyzoite transplacental transmission, safe (low teratogenicity risk), LIMITED US AVAILABILITY but obtainable directly from FDA. Substitutions: azithromycin, clindamycin, atovaquone. Sulfonamides safe in FIRST 2 TRIMESTERS only.

hiv_aids_context_aao.atypical_presentation: In AIDS: lesions are LARGER, bilateral disease in up to 40% of cases (vs typically unilateral in immunocompetent), solitary OR multifocal patterns. Inflammation (choroid/retina/vitreous) is LESS PROMINENT than in immunocompetent patients paradoxically. Trophozoites/cysts found in GREATER numbers within retinitis areas; organisms occasionally seen INVADING THE CHOROID (not observed in immunocompetent patients).
hiv_aids_context_aao.scar_absence: Because AIDS-associated ocular toxoplasmosis is more often NEWLY ACQUIRED or from DISSEMINATION (not reactivation of old ocular disease), the classic adjacent-old-scar pattern may be ABSENT - do not require a scar to consider this diagnosis in an AIDS patient
hiv_aids_context_aao.differential_difficulty: Can be difficult to distinguish from acute_retinal_necrosis, necrotizing herpetic retinitis (nonnecrotizing_herpetic_retinopathy), or syphilitic_uveitis in this population - definitive diagnosis may require aqueous/vitreous culture + PCR
hiv_aids_context_aao.cns_workup_mandatory: Prompt diagnosis is critical since untreated disease INEVITABLY progresses in immunocompromised hosts, AND ocular toxoplasmosis may be associated with CEREBRAL or DISSEMINATED toxoplasmosis (major AIDS morbidity/mortality causes) - all AIDS patients with active ocular toxoplasmosis need CT/MRI of the head + infectious disease consultation to rule out CNS involvement
hiv_aids_context_aao.treatment_regimen: Synergistic combination of pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim + azithromycin + atovaquone and/or clindamycin often required (broader combination than typical immunocompetent regimen). Corticosteroids used CAUTIOUSLY given further immunosuppression risk - only with adequate antimicrobial coverage. Consider pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine bone marrow toxicity when choosing regimen. MAINTENANCE therapy may be needed if immune status remains poor.

diseases.name: Ocular Toxocariasis
diseases.laterality: unilateral in ~2/3 with permanent visual impairment
key_features: Ophthalmic presentations: chronic endophthalmitis (25%), posterior pole granuloma (25%), peripheral granuloma (50%, MOST COMMON) - see manifestation_patterns_table_aao for age ranges by pattern
ANY of these presentations may produce LEUKOCORIA
Uncommon variants: unilateral pars planitis with diffuse peripheral inflammatory exudates, optic nerve granulomas, DUSN-like presentation (cross-reference our new dusn entity)
Stool analysis for larvae is NOT USEFUL - ova are not shed in the GI tract (larvae don't complete lifecycle in humans, just migrate)

investigations.name: Serologic testing
investigations.note: Suggests prior exposure but negative serology does NOT exclude OT

investigations.name: Antibody testing of OCULAR fluid
investigations.note: May be POSITIVE even when serum serology is negative - more sensitive for active ocular disease

investigations.name: B-scan ultrasonography and/or CT
investigations.note: Assess vitreous membranes/tractional detachment AND confirm absence of calcium (retinoblastoma differentiator)

investigations.name: Vitrectomy specimen
investigations.note: May yield larvae directly; PCR not readily available for OT

treatment.ot: Anthelminthic therapy NOT well-established for OT specifically - may be considered if larvae appear ACTIVE
treatment.mainstay: Local and systemic CORTICOSTEROIDS to reduce inflammation and minimize structural complications
treatment.surgical: Structural complications may later be amenable to vitreoretinal surgical techniques

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski describes 'chronic endophthalmitis' pattern with peripheral retina/pars plana involvement (snowbanking-like exudate) plus posterior pole/peripheral granuloma.
diseases.age_group_note: Kanski: visceral toxocariasis 'usually occurs in a child aged 2-7 years' (anak); ocular toxocariasis 'tends to occur in older children and adults' (remaja/dewasa) - explicitly contrasted with visceral form.

epidemiology_aao.animal_infection_rate: CDC: 30% of dogs <6 months infected with T. canis; 25% of cats infected with T. cati
epidemiology_aao.us_seroprevalence: NHANES 2011-2014: overall Toxocara seroprevalence 5.1% in US (children antibody-positivity associated with LACK of health insurance - a health equity marker)
epidemiology_aao.ocular_toxocariasis_prevalence: ~1% of tertiary uveitis referrals (northern California cohort)
epidemiology_aao.vt_vs_ot_distinction: Visceral toxocariasis (VT) usually affects children <3yo (fever, cough, hepatomegaly, pneumonitis, meningoencephalitis, PERIPHERAL EOSINOPHILIA). Ocular toxocariasis (OT) found in OLDER children, typically WITHOUT substantial eosinophilia. VT and OT RARELY present simultaneously - largely distinct clinical presentations by age/systemic-involvement.

manifestation_patterns_table_aao.source: AAO Table 12-3, Patterns of Manifestation of Ocular Toxocariasis

chronic_endophthalmitis.lesion: Chronic unilateral uveitis, cloudy vitreous, cyclitic membrane

localized_granuloma.lesion: Macula/peripapillary region; solitary white retinal elevation, minimal reaction, 1-2 disc diameter

peripheral_granuloma.lesion: Peripheral hemispheric masses with dense connective tissue strands in vitreous, may connect to disc; RARELY bilateral

diseases.critical_note: AAO adds precision to the retinoblastoma differential: children with retinoblastoma are typically YOUNGER than OT patients, LACK substantial inflammation, and show LESION GROWTH over time (vs OT's more static/inflammatory picture) - these are the discriminating features beyond just 'consider both'. B-scan/CT confirming ABSENCE of calcium supports OT over retinoblastoma (retinoblastoma often but not always shows calcification).
diseases.differential_note: Full differential per AAO: retinoblastoma_masquerade, infectious endophthalmitis, Coats disease, familial exudative vitreoretinopathy, persistent fetal vasculature, toxoplasmosis_retinitis, pars_planitis/undifferentiated intermediate uveitis, retinopathy of prematurity, combined hamartoma of retina/RPE, dusn. Also consider migrating larvae of OTHER helminths (e.g. Baylisascaris procyonis) which can mimic OT.

diseases.name: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) Retinitis
diseases.course: progressive without treatment; indolent (peripheral) vs fulminant patterns
diseases.laterality: one eye initially, bilateral in 50% if untreated
key_features: Classic 'pizza pie'/'Margherita pizza' appearance: dense white retinal infiltration + flame-shaped haemorrhages along vascular arcades
Anterior uveitis usually mild
Strong association with LOW CD4+ count
Immune Recovery Uveitis (IRU) - severe inflammation post-ART reconstitution, can progress to phthisis
diseases.context: Common opportunistic infection in AIDS; also immunocompromised from other causes
diseases.screening: Low CD4 counts: 3-monthly if <50/uL, 6-monthly 50-100/uL, yearly if >100/uL

treatment.mainstay: ART (restores ability to suppress CMV) - discontinue antiviral when CD4 >100-150/uL
treatment.systemic: Valganciclovir oral - induction 900mg BID up to 3 weeks, maintenance 900mg daily (watch neutropenia, treat with filgrastim)
treatment.local: Intravitreal ganciclovir (2mg), foscarnet (1.2mg), or cidofovir (20ug) for macula/optic nerve-threatening lesions
treatment.surgical: Vitrectomy + endolaser + silicone oil for CMV-related RD (~75% success)
treatment.non_aids_context: Management in patients undergoing systemic immunosuppression/chemotherapy usually requires close comanagement with other medical subspecialists (not just infectious disease as in classic AIDS context).
treatment.immune_recovery_uveitis_detail: IRU can occur MONTHS TO YEARS after therapy in patients with immune recovery, DESPITE absence of CMV reactivation - occurs when the reconstituted immune system reacts to RESIDUAL CMV ANTIGENS in the eye (not active infection). Treatment: topical, periocular, and oral corticosteroids.

diseases.prognosis: Poor if macula involved despite treatment
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Primarily posterior (retinitis). Kanski notes anterior uveitis 'can occur but is usually mild with little or no injection' - included as secondary/mild, not primary feature.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given; described by immunocompromise/CD4 status, not age.

clinical_variants_aao.classic_fulminant: Large areas of retinal hemorrhage against whitened/edematous/necrotic retina background - typically posterior pole, near vascular arcades, following nerve fiber layer distribution and blood vessels ('pizza pie' pattern, cross-reference our existing Kanski-derived description)
clinical_variants_aao.granular_indolent: Found most often in RETINAL PERIPHERY, little/no hemorrhage/edema/vascular sheathing; active retinitis progresses from lesion BORDERS. This is the form MORE LIKELY in patients WITHOUT AIDS (more anterior chamber/vitreous inflammation and occlusive vasculitis than the classic form)
clinical_variants_aao.perivascular_frosted_branch: A 'frosted-branch angiitis' variant - undifferentiated retinal perivasculitis, initially described in IMMUNOCOMPETENT children but also seen in immunocompromised patients

diseases.pathophysiology: Hematogenous spread across blood-ocular barrier -> infection of retinal vascular endothelial cells -> cell-to-cell viral transmission within retina. Histology: primary full-thickness coagulative necrotizing retinitis + secondary diffuse choroiditis. Pathognomonic cytomegalic changes: large eosinophilic intranuclear inclusions + small basophilic cytoplasmic inclusions (also in RPE/vascular endothelium).

congenital_cmv.retinitis_prevalence: 11-22% of children with congenital CMV infection
congenital_cmv.delayed_presentation_warning: CMV retinitis can occur LATER in childhood even in children with initially NO discernible lesions and NO systemic reactivation evidence - even ASYMPTOMATIC congenital CMV children should be monitored regularly for later ocular involvement
congenital_cmv.sequelae: RD in up to 1/3 of affected children; optic atrophy and cataract common

diseases.epidemiology_modern_context: With ART, CMV retinitis incidence has DECREASED 80% in AIDS patients with adequate medical access. Now MORE COMMONLY diagnosed in patients immunosuppressed by CHEMOTHERAPY, IMT for systemic inflammatory disease, or after SOLID ORGAN/STEM CELL TRANSPLANT - i.e. the typical CMV retinitis patient profile has shifted away from classic AIDS. RARE: can develop after LOCAL corticosteroid injection including intravitreal.
diseases.screening_note: No specific formal screening guidelines exist for immunosuppressed patients with CMV viremia - ophthalmic exam indications include ocular symptoms OR active/recently-active multiorgan CMV infection.

hiv_context_aao.historical_significance: Before potent ART, CMV was the most common opportunistic infection in AIDS, occurring in up to 40% of AIDS patients (usually CD4+ <50 cells/uL). Now uncommon where potent ART is available.
hiv_context_aao.geographic_disparity: CMV infection remains an INCREASING problem in resource-limited regions, PARTICULARLY SOUTHEAST ASIA - directly relevant to Indonesia context, contrasting with the declining trend in resource-rich settings
hiv_context_aao.screening: CD4+ <50 cells/uL: monitor every 3 MONTHS for CMV retinitis development, since it CAN BE ASYMPTOMATIC
hiv_context_aao.mortality_link: CMV retinitis signifies a 2-FOLD increased mortality risk specifically in patients with CD4+ <100 cells/uL (this effect NOT observed at CD4+ >=100). Antiviral-RESISTANT CMV further increases mortality risk.

precise_dosing.iv_induction: Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV twice daily OR foscarnet 90mg/kg IV twice daily, for 2 weeks, then maintenance with daily dosing of either
precise_dosing.oral_alternative: Valganciclovir 900mg PO twice daily for 3 weeks, then maintenance 900mg/day
precise_dosing.intravitreal: Effective for intraocular disease alone or as adjunct; may be the ONLY option if systemic therapy not tolerated (myelotoxicity from (val)ganciclovir, nephrotoxicity from foscarnet)

hiv_context_aao.discontinuation_criteria: Can SAFELY discontinue maintenance therapy once CD4+ >=100 cells/uL sustained for 3-6 months. ART-NAIVE patients may need only 6 months of anti-CMV therapy with good reconstitution; ART-EXPERIENCED patients may need LONG-TERM maintenance. Even after discontinuation, patients remain at RECURRENCE risk and need 3-month interval monitoring.
hiv_context_aao.retinal_detachment_risk: RD occurs in up to 50% of AIDS+CMV retinitis patients (during active disease OR after successful treatment). With potent ART: reduced to 0.06/patient-year. Risk factors: involvement of ALL 3 retinal zones, lower CD4+, more extensive retinitis. Usually needs PPV with LONG-TERM SILICONE OIL tamponade given extensive necrosis/multiple posterior holes; anatomical reattachment achievable in 90%.

diseases.differential_note: In HIV/AIDS context specifically, ALSO consider toxoplasmosis_retinitis and syphilitic_uveitis as masquerades of viral retinitis - these can mimic CMV/herpetic retinitis in immunocompromised patients.

diseases.name: Progressive Retinal Necrosis (PRN/PORN)
key_features: Begins as patchy areas of OUTER retinal whitening that coalesce RAPIDLY

CONTRAST with ARN: posterior pole MAY be involved EARLY (vs ARN's peripheral-then-posterior progression); vitreous cell/haze TYPICALLY ABSENT (vs ARN's heavy vitritis); retinal VASCULATURE minimally involved, at least initially (vs ARN's occlusive arteriolitis) - this triad of differences (posterior-early, minimal-vitritis, minimal-vascular-involvement) is the key differentiator from ARN
diseases.prognosis: Extremely poor - no light perception outcome in >50% of eyes

treatment.resistance_note: Often RESISTANT to IV acyclovir ALONE (unlike typical ARN response)
treatment.successful_approach: Combination SYSTEMIC + INTRAVITREAL therapy using foscarnet AND ganciclovir
treatment.long_term: Long-term SUPPRESSIVE antiviral therapy required in HIV/AIDS patients who cannot achieve immune reconstitution via ART

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski explicitly states anterior uveitis and vitritis are MINIMAL in PRN - this is a key differentiator from CMV retinitis and ARN, so anterior/intermediate are deliberately NOT included here.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given; described by immunocompromise status, not age.
diseases.relationship_to_arn: PORN is considered a MORPHOLOGIC VARIANT of ARN, occurring specifically in PROFOUNDLY immunosuppressed patients (CD4+ <=50 cells/uL), most commonly advanced AIDS.

epidemiology_aao.most_common_cause: VZV (though HSV has also been isolated)
epidemiology_aao.cutaneous_zoster_history: 67% of PORN+HIV/AIDS patients have a history of cutaneous zoster
epidemiology_aao.prognosis: POOR - in the largest reported series, 67% of PORN patients ended with NO LIGHT PERCEPTION final VA

diseases.name: Acute Retinal Necrosis (ARN)
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (viral - HSV in younger, VZV in older patients)
key_features: Onset: acute fulminant disease WITHOUT systemic prodrome often, sometimes months-years after primary infection or following cutaneous/systemic herpetic infection (varicella, zoster, herpetic encephalitis)

Progression: substantial ANTERIOR segment inflammation + KP + PS + elevated IOP FIRST, together with heavy vitreous cellular infiltration; WITHIN 2 WEEKS the classic triad evolves: occlusive retinal arteriolitis + vitritis + multifocal yellow-white PERIPHERAL retinitis

Peripheral lesions initially discontinuous/scalloped, coalesce within DAYS into a confluent 360-degree cream-colored peripheral retinitis progressing POSTERIORLY, leaving full-thickness necrosis + arteriolitis + phlebitis +/- hemorrhage
Combined tractional-rhegmatogenous RD in 75% of patients - very high rate
diseases.diagnostic_criteria: American Uveitis Society criteria (panuveitis + peripheral necrosis foci + circumferential spread + occlusive vasculitis + rapid progression without treatment)

treatment.urgency: Do NOT delay antiviral initiation while awaiting PCR results - given rapid progression, high RD rate, and guarded visual prognosis, empiric treatment should start immediately on clinical suspicion
treatment.iv_induction: IV acyclovir 10mg/kg every 8 hours for 10-14 days (effective against HSV and VZV) - watch for REVERSIBLE creatinine/liver enzyme elevation; reduce dose if frank renal insufficiency
treatment.oral_alternative_induction: Oral valacyclovir up to 2g 3x daily - successful alternative to IV acyclovir with similar bioavailability

continuation_after_iv.vzv: Acyclovir 800mg PO 5x/day, OR valacyclovir 1-2g PO 3x/day, OR famciclovir 500mg PO 3x/day - continued for 3 MONTHS
continuation_after_iv.hsv1: Oral antiviral dose is ONE-HALF the VZV dose
continuation_after_iv.duration_benefit: Extended antiviral therapy may reduce contralateral/bilateral ARN incidence by 80% over 1 year - a strong argument for the full 3-month continuation course, not just acute treatment

treatment.intravitreal_adjunct: First-line OR for treatment-failure cases: intravitreal ganciclovir (2.0mg/0.05 or 0.1mL) and foscarnet (2.4mg/0.1mL) WITH systemic antiviral. Combination of high-dose oral + intravitreal MAY decrease RD/severe-vision-loss risk (no RCT data yet). Injections repeated 2-3x/week until stable, then weekly if needed.
treatment.corticosteroid_timing: AFTER 24-48 hours of antiviral therapy: prednisone 1mg/kg/day up to 60-80mg/day, tapered over several weeks
treatment.anticoagulation: Aspirin/anticoagulants have been used for the associated hypercoagulable state to prevent vascular occlusions, but results INCONCLUSIVE
treatment.prophylactic_laser: Prophylactic barrier laser photocoagulation at the posterior border of necrotic lesions is CONTROVERSIAL (used by some practitioners) to try to prevent RD
treatment.rd_surgery: Vitrectomy techniques PREFERRED over standard scleral buckling when RD occurs
treatment.prognosis_caveat: Optic nerve atrophy may remain VISUALLY LIMITING even with a favorable retinal ANATOMICAL outcome - anatomical success doesn't guarantee functional/visual success

diseases.prognosis: More than half achieve only 6/60 due to retinal/optic nerve ischemia or RD
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski explicitly lists 'prominent anterior uveitis and vitritis (panuveitis)' as an American Uveitis Society diagnostic criterion for ARN.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO gives virus-specific age patterns rather than a single range: VZV/HSV-1-caused ARN patients tend OLDER (mean 40yo); HSV-2-caused ARN patients tend YOUNGER (<25yo). No single age bucket captures this - see epidemiology_aao for the virus-specific breakdown.

epidemiology_aao.age_pattern: Most cases cluster 5th-7th decades overall, but VZV/HSV-1 patients tend OLDER (mean 40yo) while HSV-2 patients tend YOUNGER (<25yo)
epidemiology_aao.causative_virus_frequency: VZV is the MOST COMMON cause, followed by HSV-1, HSV-2, and rarely CMV (per PCR-based studies)
epidemiology_aao.fellow_eye_involvement: ~36% of cases, USUALLY within 6 weeks of onset, but can be months-years later
epidemiology_aao.encephalitis_risk: HIGHER risk of encephalitis/meningitis with HSV-1-caused ARN than VZV-caused ARN

official_diagnostic_criteria.source: American Uveitis Society Criteria for Diagnosis of Acute Retinal Necrosis (Holland GN et al, Am J Ophthalmol 1994) - clinical diagnosis, INDEPENDENT of viral etiology or host immune status
criteria: One or more foci of retinal necrosis with discrete borders, located in the PERIPHERAL retina (macular lesions do NOT exclude diagnosis if peripheral retinitis also present)
Rapid progression in the ABSENCE of antiviral therapy

investigations.name: PCR of AQUEOUS (not vitreous) fluid
investigations.note: Aqueous sampling is usually SUFFICIENT for diagnosis - has largely SUPPLANTED viral culture, intraocular antibody titers, and serology. Quantitative PCR can also give viral load/activity/treatment-response information.

investigations.name: Endoretinal biopsy
investigations.note: Rare cases where PCR is negative but clinical suspicion remains high

diseases.name: Presumed Ocular Histoplasmosis Syndrome (POHS)
key_features: Classic diagnostic TRIAD: histo spots WITHOUT vitreous cells + peripapillary pigment changes + macular CNV
Active CNV: yellow-green subretinal membrane typically surrounded by a PIGMENT RING, +/- intraretinal/subretinal fluid and hemorrhage
Massive subretinal exudation/hemorrhage/RD can progress to subretinal FIBROSIS if untreated
diseases.epidemiology: Endemic areas e.g. Mississippi river valley USA

treatment.acute_granulomatous_lesions: Rarely seen, but treat with oral or regional (periocular) corticosteroids if present
treatment.cnv_first_line: Intravitreal anti-VEGF is the PRIMARY treatment for OHS-associated macular CNV
treatment.alternatives: Thermal laser photocoagulation, photodynamic therapy, intravitreal corticosteroids, submacular surgery (cross-reference BCSC Section 12 for full CNV management)

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski explicitly states vitritis is ABSENT in the classic POHS triad - anterior/intermediate deliberately excluded.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski.

epidemiology_aao.demographics: Mostly northern European descent, equal sex risk
epidemiology_aao.endemic_areas: Ohio and Mississippi River valleys (classic), but can occur in non-endemic areas too
