epidemiology_aao.histo_spot_prevalence: ~1.5% of patients from endemic areas show typical peripheral histo spots, often FIRST appearing during ADOLESCENCE
epidemiology_aao.linear_streaks: 5% of patients show linear equatorial streaks
epidemiology_aao.scar_progression: New choroidal scars develop in >20% of patients over time, but only 3.8% of THOSE progress to CNV
epidemiology_aao.cnv_risk_stratified: Macular histo spot present: 15% CNV risk within 5 years. NO macular spot: 5% risk - i.e. macular spot location TRIPLES the 5-year CNV risk
epidemiology_aao.maculopathy_age: Mean age at CNV-related maculopathy presentation: 41 years

diseases.differential_note: CNV differential: AMD, myopic degeneration, angioid streaks, choroidal rupture, undifferentiated CNV, multifocal_choroiditis_panuveitis, punctate_inner_choroidopathy. Scarring differential (granulomatous mimics): toxoplasmosis_retinitis, tb_uveitis, Coccidioidomycosis, syphilitic_uveitis, sarcoidosis_uveitis, toxocariasis_ocular.

diseases.name: Tuberculosis-associated Uveitis
key_features: CLINICAL PEARL (AAO): possible ocular TB manifestations include necrotizing scleritis, choroidal granuloma (tuberculoma), serpiginous-like choroiditis, and retinal vasculitis (often OCCLUSIVE)
Tubercular uveitis is typically CHRONIC GRANULOMATOUS, anterior OR posterior OR both - isolated granulomatous AAU possible but MORE LIKELY to occur WITH posterior disease
Anterior: mutton-fat KP, iris nodules, PS, secondary glaucoma - though NONgranulomatous uveitis also possible
Waxing/waning course with macular edema development; neuroretinitis can occur
Other posterior findings: subretinal abscess, CNV, optic neuritis, panophthalmitis

kp_distribution.detail: TB anterior uveitis is typically granulomatous with mutton-fat/large KP and broad PS, following the classic gravity-driven Arlt's-triangle distribution rather than the diffuse pattern of viral anterior uveitis.
kp_distribution.source_note: Cross-referenced literature on granulomatous KP distribution (non-Kanski specific for TB, but consistent with Kanski's general granulomatous-KP description)

investigations.name: Tuberculin skin test (PPD)
investigations.note: Indicates PAST exposure, not proof of active disease. In BCG-vaccinated patients, PPD reaction is common/nonspecific (doesn't necessarily mean TB infection) - use IGRA instead in this population

investigations.name: IGRA (interferon-gamma release assay)
investigations.note: More useful than PPD in BCG-vaccinated patients specifically - positive IGRA suggests true TB exposure regardless of BCG history

investigations.name: Anti-cord-factor ELISA
investigations.note: Newer test - rapid serodiagnosis of pulmonary TB, supportive for ocular diagnosis too

investigations.name: Chest imaging (CXR first, CT if needed)
Nucleic acid amplification (16S rRNA transcription-mediated amplification, or PCR) of ocular fluid
investigations.note: YIELD MAY BE LOW - M. tuberculosis has a thick cell wall and is less likely present in aqueous/vitreous even with true ocular TB. A negative ocular PCR does NOT rule out TB-associated uveitis.

investigations.name: Chorioretinal biopsy with PCR + histology
investigations.note: Reserve for atypical/vision-threatening cases where ruling out masquerade syndromes is critical

treatment.standard_regimen: 2-month INDUCTION with isoniazid+rifampin+pyrazinamide+ethambutol (quadruple therapy), then 2-drug continuation for 4-7 months. >95% of immunocompetent patients successfully treated with full adherence.

cots_indications_for_quadruple_therapy.source: Collaborative Ocular Tuberculosis Study (COTS) Consensus Group guidelines
clear_indications: Recent conversion to positive TB test
cots_indications_for_quadruple_therapy.presumptive_indication: Positive TB test + NORMAL CXR: quadruple therapy MAY still be indicated if ocular inflammation is strongly suggestive of TB pattern, OR uveitis has been recalcitrant to systemic IMT

treatment.paradoxical_worsening: After starting quadruple drug therapy, tubercular uveitis may PARADOXICALLY WORSEN - requires concurrent topical and/or systemic corticosteroids for the inflammatory component (this is a well-recognized phenomenon, not treatment failure)
treatment.imt_timing: If chronic tubercular uveitis develops from delayed diagnosis, systemic IMT may be cautiously started AFTER AT LEAST 1 MONTH of quadruple drug therapy, WITH infectious disease specialist agreement
treatment.latent_tb_before_immunosuppression: Patients with latent TB starting systemic IMT (especially TNF inhibitors) should be treated for 6-12 months with isoniazid +/- rifapentine or rifampin FIRST

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski: TB uveitis can show anterior uveitis, vitritis (intermediate-type involvement), choroidal granuloma/choroiditis (posterior), and retinal vasculitis - spans all compartments depending on case.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski; described as variable.

epidemiology_aao.global_burden: Worldwide: >10.4 million new cases/year, 1.7 million deaths/year. ~1/3 of world's population infected. 95% of cases in RESOURCE-LIMITED countries.
epidemiology_aao.regional_variation: US tertiary care uveitis incidence attributable to TB: <1%. INDIA referral centers: up to 10% - dramatic geographic difference highly relevant for practice in TB-endemic settings.
epidemiology_aao.us_risk_factors: 71.5% of US TB infections occur in persons BORN OUTSIDE the US (most important risk factor in that context) - plus diabetes, HIV, homelessness, substance use, congregate living/working settings
epidemiology_aao.reactivation_predominance: Up to 90% of TB cases result from REACTIVATION of latent infection (not new/primary infection) - relevant to why TNF-inhibitor screening for latent TB is so critical before starting immunosuppression
epidemiology_aao.affinity: M. tuberculosis has affinity for HIGHLY OXYGENATED tissues - explains why lesions concentrate in lung apices AND the choroid specifically (both highly oxygenated) - a useful pathophysiologic explanation for why the eye is a target organ at all
epidemiology_aao.extrapulmonary_testing_caveat: Of the 20% with extrapulmonary disease: 50% have a NORMAL chest radiograph, and up to 20% have a NEGATIVE PPD skin test - a normal CXR or negative PPD does NOT rule out TB-associated uveitis

diseases.ocular_involvement_detail: Most TB-uveitis patients have NO active TB infection detectable elsewhere in the body at the time. Disease may result from direct ocular infection OR an immunologic reaction to extraocular organism (i.e. hypersensitivity mechanism, not just direct infection) - this dual mechanism explains why some cases respond to anti-TB therapy alone while others need concurrent anti-inflammatory treatment.

choroidal_involvement_patterns.disseminated_choroiditis: MOST COMMON presentation - 5 to HUNDREDS of deep discrete yellowish tubercles (0.5-3.0mm), posterior pole predominant, may accompany granulomatous AAU/vitritis/disc edema/NFL hemorrhages. Choroidal tubercles may be one of the EARLIEST signs of disseminated disease and are MORE COMMON in immunocompromised hosts.
choroidal_involvement_patterns.tuberculoma: Alternative presentation: single focal LARGE (4-14mm) elevated choroidal mass, may cause neurosensory RD + macular star formation
choroidal_involvement_patterns.serpiginous_like_choroiditis: Also called 'multifocal serpiginoid choroiditis' - can MASQUERADE as atypical serpiginous choroiditis (cross-reference serpiginous_choroidopathy and relentless_placoid_chorioretinitis - all three can look similar, TB must always be excluded)
choroidal_involvement_patterns.hiv_caveat: In HIV/AIDS patients, tubercular choroiditis may PROGRESS DESPITE effective anti-TB therapy - treatment response is less reliable in this population

diseases.eales_disease_crossref: Retinal TB involvement should be DIFFERENTIATED from Eales disease (cross-reference our new eales_disease entity) - a peripheral retinal vasculitis in otherwise-healthy young men 20-40yo, POSSIBLY associated with tuberculin hypersensitivity (some PCR studies detect M. tuberculosis DNA in Eales-disease ocular fluids), but conceptually treated as a related-but-distinct entity.

indications: Ocular findings suggestive of TB (choroidal granuloma, serpiginous-like choroiditis, necrotizing scleritis)
High pretest probability from TB exposure risk factors and/or systemic symptoms
Screening before starting systemic immunosuppressive medication (especially TNF inhibitors)

diseases.name: Ocular Syphilis (Acquired)
diseases.course: variable; can occur at any stage
key_features: PATHOGNOMONIC finding: 'syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinitis' - clinical + angiographic appearance is essentially pathognomonic for SECONDARY syphilis specifically. Placoid yellowish-gray RPE-level lesions, FAF hyperautofluorescent, FA early hypofluorescence+late staining with retinal perivenous staining, ICGA hypofluorescent, OCT shows RPE irregularity/disorganization/outer retinal loss.
Anterior findings: iris roseola, iris papulosa (vascularized papules), iris nodosa (large red nodules), gummata, interstitial keratitis, PS, lens dislocation, iris atrophy
Posterior: MOST COMMON is focal/multifocal chorioretinitis (small grayish-yellow, postequatorial, may become confluent) with variable vitritis
Punctate INNER retinal infiltrates are a distinctive described finding

Syphilitic retinitis can mimic acute retinal necrosis / PORN, but progresses MORE SLOWLY and responds DRAMATICALLY to IV penicillin - this differential response to treatment can itself be diagnostically informative if empiric treatment is trialed
Immunocompromised patients: optic neuritis/neuroretinitis MORE likely as initial presentation; recurrences possible even after appropriate treatment

kp_distribution.detail: Syphilitic anterior uveitis may be granulomatous or non-granulomatous and bilateral in 50%; when granulomatous, follows the classic Arlt's-triangle inferior distribution rather than the diffuse viral pattern.
kp_distribution.source_note: Cross-referenced literature (non-Kanski specific for syphilis KP distribution, consistent with Kanski's general granulomatous-KP description)

diseases.note: The 'great masquerader' - always test for syphilis in atypical/unexplained uveitis

investigations.name: BOTH treponemal AND nontreponemal serology (reverse sequence: treponemal first)
Lumbar puncture for CSF-VDRL and cell count/protein
investigations.note: Especially with neuro symptoms. Positive CSF-VDRL and/or pleocytosis = diagnostic for neurosyphilis (CSF-VDRL can be falsely nonreactive even with active CNS disease though). If abnormal, REPEAT CSF exam every 6 months until cell count/protein normalize and VDRL nonreactive.

investigations.name: Dark-field microscopy / direct fluorescent antibody (primary syphilis lesion)
PCR of intraocular fluid, CSF, or mucosal/ulcer swabs
HIV testing
investigations.note: ALL syphilis-diagnosed patients should get HIV testing given high coinfection rate

investigations.name: Public health reporting
investigations.note: Newly diagnosed syphilis must be reported per local regulations - partner notification/contact tracing

treatment.regimen: REGARDLESS of immune status, treat with the NEUROSYPHILIS regimen: IV aqueous penicillin G for 10-14 days (this is the standard for ALL ocular syphilis given the neurosyphilis conceptual framework above)
treatment.first_choice: Penicillin - required for neurosyphilis, congenital infection, pregnancy, or HIV coinfection (no good alternatives in these situations)
treatment.penicillin_allergy: Desensitization often required. For non-neurosyphilis/non-HIV patients: doxycycline or tetracycline alternatives. For ocular syphilis specifically: ceftriaxone or chloramphenicol reported effective alternatives.
treatment.jarisch_herxheimer: Monitor in FIRST 24 HOURS: fever, chills, hypotension, tachycardia, malaise, AND possible worsening of intraocular inflammation (may need local/systemic corticosteroids) - supportive care usually sufficient
treatment.critical_warning: Using systemic/periocular/intravitreal CORTICOSTEROIDS in patients with UNDIAGNOSED syphilis can WORSEN the disease and cause IRREVERSIBLE vision loss - this is why syphilis testing is essentially mandatory before steroid-heavy uveitis treatment in any atypical/undiagnosed case
treatment.delayed_diagnosis_consequence: Significantly delayed diagnosis can lead to CHRONIC uveitis requiring ongoing anti-inflammatory treatment even AFTER the antibiotic course completes

iop_pattern.detail: Roseolae (dilated iris capillaries) may develop into yellowish nodules; IOP may be elevated.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Acquired Syphilis Ocular features section

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski: syphilitic anterior uveitis, chorioretinitis (posterior), and syphilis serology is also part of standard intermediate uveitis (IU) investigation workup, implying IU presentation is recognized.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski for the uveitis itself.
diseases.epidemiology_aao: REEMERGING globally - incidence rates increasing among men (especially MSM) and women in the US since 2000 low point. Congenital syphilis nearly DOUBLED in US (9.4 to 15.7 per 100,000 live births). Despite this, syphilis causes <2% of ALL uveitis cases - but remains critical to test for given its status as 'one of the great masqueraders in medicine' and being CURABLE with appropriate treatment even in HIV coinfection.
diseases.neurosyphilis_concept: CONCEPTUALLY IMPORTANT: because the eye is an extension of the CNS, OCULAR SYPHILIS IS CONSIDERED A MANIFESTATION OF NEUROSYPHILIS - this has direct treatment implications: ALL syphilitic uveitis (regardless of immune status or apparent disease stage) is treated with the NEUROSYPHILIS regimen (IV penicillin), not lesser regimens for earlier-stage disease.

congenital_syphilis.presentation: Can present at BIRTH or DECADES LATER
congenital_syphilis.early_congenital_uveitis: Most frequent form: multifocal chorioretinitis, then retinal vasculitis - both can cause bilateral 'salt-and-pepper' retinopathy (peripheral, posterior pole, or single quadrant) that is NON-PROGRESSIVE with potentially NORMAL visual acuity - don't over-interpret this retinopathy pattern as active/progressive disease
congenital_syphilis.late_congenital_hallmark: Nonulcerative stromal INTERSTITIAL KERATITIS in up to 50% of untreated late congenital cases, more common in GIRLS. Hutchinson TRIAD = interstitial keratitis + CN VIII deafness + Hutchinson teeth. Keratouveitis (anterior uveitis accompanying interstitial keratitis) can be hard to visualize due to corneal haze.
congenital_syphilis.transmission_note: Transplacental, mainly after 10th week of pregnancy; maternal PRIMARY/SECONDARY syphilis more likely transmitted than latent syphilis

acquired_syphilis_staging.primary: Painless chancre at inoculation site, ~3 week incubation, heals spontaneously
acquired_syphilis_staging.secondary: 6-8 weeks after chancre resolution: lymphadenopathy + generalized maculopapular rash (prominent on palms/soles). Uveitis occurs in ~10% of secondary syphilis cases.
acquired_syphilis_staging.tertiary: ~1/3 of untreated patients progress here (benign tertiary = gumma in skin/mucosa/choroid/iris; cardiovascular syphilis; neurosyphilis). Uveitis in up to 5% of tertiary cases. NOTE: uveitis CAN occur at ANY stage including primary disease - staging doesn't reliably predict ocular risk.

serology_table_aao.reverse_sequence_screening: NEWER preferred approach: start with TREPONEMAL immunoassay first (faster, automated, detects early/latent disease better) rather than the traditional nontreponemal-first sequence. Treponemal tests have HIGHER positive predictive value in uveitis patients specifically.
serology_table_aao.nontreponemal_use: Correlates with DISEASE ACTIVITY (titers rise in primary/secondary, fall with treatment/latency) - useful for MONITORING therapy response
serology_table_aao.treponemal_use: Becomes positive in secondary stage and stays positive FOR LIFE - NOT useful for monitoring treatment response, only for confirming exposure/diagnosis
serology_table_aao.congenital_diagnosis_specific: Use FTA-ABS IgM test specifically (not standard VDRL/FTA-ABS IgG, which cross placenta and are positive in ALL infants of infected mothers regardless of actual infant infection)
false_positive_nontreponemal_causes: Pregnancy, vaccination, advanced age, IV drug use
Other infections: leprosy, bacterial endocarditis, TB, mononucleosis, HIV, atypical pneumonia, malaria
Other spirochetal infections: rickettsial, Lyme, LEPTOSPIROSIS (cross-reference our new leptospirosis entity)
Other treponemal infections: yaws, pinta, bejel
serology_table_aao.false_negative_notes: Prozone effect (high antibody titers paradoxically prevent complex formation) in primary/secondary syphilis; late-stage inactive spirochetes; both tests may be falsely negative in recent exposure (<2-4 weeks) - and both false-positive AND false-negative rates are HIGHER in HIV-infected patients

hiv_aids_context_aao.presentation_differences: In AIDS patients: VITRITIS WITHOUT CHORIORETINITIS can be the FIRST manifestation (atypical presentation). MORE LIKELY to present with optic neuritis or neuroretinitis compared to HIV-negative patients.
hiv_aids_context_aao.classic_aids_finding: Unilateral or bilateral pale-yellow PLACOID retinal lesions preferentially involving the MACULA (syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinitis) - same entity as described in Ch.11 but explicitly called out as a classic AIDS-associated syphilis manifestation
hiv_aids_context_aao.suggestive_sign: Discrete creamy-yellow SUPERFICIAL retinal precipitates overlying areas of retinitis is VERY SUGGESTIVE of syphilis - though this sign can occur REGARDLESS of HIV status
hiv_aids_context_aao.disease_course: May be MORE AGGRESSIVE in HIV-infected patients
hiv_aids_context_aao.hiv_specific_treatment: 18-24 million units IV penicillin G daily for 10-14 days, FOLLOWED BY 2.4 million units IM benzathine penicillin G weekly for 3 WEEKS (this follow-on IM regimen is specific to the HIV-coinfected treatment protocol - more prolonged than the standard regimen). Monitor quantitative RPR given recurrence risk.

diseases.name: Lyme Disease (Borreliosis) - ocular
key_features: Uveitis (when it occurs, rarely) can be ANY type but intermediate is most common
Peripheral multifocal choroiditis pattern described: multiple small round 'punched-out' lesions with vitritis, resembling SARCOIDOSIS
Choroidal involvement can cause pigment epithelial clumping resembling SYPHILIS or RUBELLA changes

Neuro-ophthalmic manifestations (papillitis MOST common, neuroretinitis, optic neuritis, papilledema, multiple cranial neuropathies) occur MORE FREQUENTLY than uveitis itself in LD - worth remembering neuro-ophthalmic exam findings may be more likely than intraocular inflammation

investigations.name: Serum ELISA for Lyme IgM/IgG, followed by confirmatory Western immunoblot
investigations.note: ONLY indicated with compatible exposure history/tick bite risk/systemic LD symptoms - NOT a routine uveitis screen. False positives occur with syphilis, other infections, and various rheumatologic diseases.

investigations.name: CNS investigation
investigations.note: May be warranted in uveitis+LD patients, similar approach to syphilitic uveitis workup

treatment.severe_posterior_segment: IV antibiotics per neurologic-LD dosing: ceftriaxone 2g IV daily, OR cefotaxime 2g IV q8h, OR penicillin G 18-24 million units/day IV divided q4h
treatment.jarisch_herxheimer: Can occur with antibiotic therapy, similar to syphilis treatment
treatment.anterior_segment_adjunct: Topical corticosteroids + mydriatics AFTER antibiotics started
treatment.systemic_steroid_caution: Routine use of SYSTEMIC corticosteroids is CONTROVERSIAL - associated with INCREASED antibiotic treatment failure risk, so use cautiously

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski: can be anterior (granulomatous or non), intermediate (the most common form), or posterior (multifocal choroiditis, vasculitis, neuroretinitis). Not explicitly termed 'panuveitis' by Kanski for this entity.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski.
diseases.major_correction_note: IMPORTANT: AAO Ch.11 explicitly states 'intraocular inflammation is NOT a common manifestation of LD' and that 'serologic screening for LD does NOT need to be part of a standard workup for new-onset uveitis' in a patient WITHOUT tick-bite risk factors or systemic LD symptoms. This somewhat tempers Kanski's framing (which lists Lyme prominently in intermediate uveitis differentials) - LD-associated uveitis should be considered ONLY when there is a compatible exposure history or systemic LD symptoms, not as a routine screen for all new uveitis.

epidemiology_aao.us_organism: Borrelia burgdorferi (US); Borrelia afzelii/garinii outside US
epidemiology_aao.vector: Ixodes scapularis (northeast/mid-Atlantic/midwest US), Ixodes pacificus (western US)
epidemiology_aao.demographics: Slightly more men (53%); BIMODAL age distribution: children 5-14yo AND adults 50-59yo

staging_aao.stage_1_localized: Erythema chronicum migrans ('bull's-eye' lesion, appears within 2-28 days in >=70%) + constitutional symptoms. Ocular: follicular conjunctivitis in ~11% (most common stage 1 ocular finding), episcleritis less common.
staging_aao.stage_2_early_disseminated: Days-weeks after exposure, hematogenous spread to skin/CNS/joints/heart/eyes. Up to 80% develop joint manifestations if untreated (mono/oligoarthritis, large joints). Neuro involvement up to 15% (Lyme neuroborreliosis: meningitis, encephalitis, radiculitis, Bell palsy - up to 25% of new CN VII palsy in endemic areas attributed to Lyme). INTRAOCULAR INFLAMMATION IS RARE but occurs mainly in this stage - intermediate uveitis is the MOST COMMON uveitis type when it does occur, may have severe vitritis with granulomatous AC reaction.
staging_aao.stage_3_late_disseminated: >5 months after infection. Episodic->chronic arthritis (HLA-DR4/-DR2 associated in North America). Ocular: MOST COMMON stage 3 finding is presumed immune-mediated KERATITIS (bilateral, patchy, focal, stromal) - months to years after infection onset.

diseases.name: Endogenous Bacterial/Fungal Endophthalmitis
key_features: Presentation SPECTRUM: some patients ambulatory/afebrile, others show active systemic infection signs (fever >101.5F, elevated WBC, positive extraocular cultures)
Ocular symptoms: acute pain, photophobia, blurred vision, SEVERELY reduced VA. Substantial AC inflammation (fibrin, hypopyon) + vitreous involvement. BOTH EYES may be affected SIMULTANEOUSLY.
Chorioretinal abscess + retinal hemorrhages including WHITE-CENTERED ROTH SPOTS may be present. Rarely: periorbital/eyelid edema.

DIAGNOSTIC CHALLENGE: the nonocular infectious source can be VERY DIFFICULT to identify, especially osteomyelitis, sinusitis, or pneumonia MISDIAGNOSED as a simple URI - detailed history and ROS are essential, lab tests alone cannot substitute
diseases.critical_note: If the associated systemic infection is MISSED, sepsis may develop and the patient may DIE - this is a systemic emergency requiring infectious disease involvement, not just an eye infection to treat locally. Morbidity includes cataract, RD, suprachoroidal hemorrhage, vitreous hemorrhage, macular scar, hypotony, phthisis in severe cases. Prognosis DIRECTLY related to the offending organism AND the patient's systemic status.

investigations.name: Vitreous and aqueous cultures + appropriate stains
Vitrectomy
investigations.note: May be required for diagnostic sampling

investigations.name: Blood and other body fluid cultures
investigations.note: Helps confirm diagnosis AND establish/guide systemic therapy

treatment.intravitreal: Typically VANCOMYCIN + CEFTAZIDIME at time of vitreous sampling
treatment.empiric_dual_coverage: When fungal organisms may ALSO be involved, EMPIRIC treatment of BOTH fungal and bacterial causes is indicated - don't wait for definitive speciation if fungal is plausible
treatment.systemic: Infectious disease specialist evaluation ESSENTIAL to identify/treat the systemic source. IV antibiotics may be needed for SEVERAL WEEKS depending on organism.
treatment.approach: Initial antimicrobial choice often EMPIRIC, then TAILORED to culture results as they return

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Endophthalmitis by nature involves all compartments; Kanski frames this under panuveitis/endophthalmitis category.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given; described by risk factor (debilitation, IV drug use, indwelling catheter etc.), not age.

epidemiology_aao.rarity: Uncommon - accounts for <10% of ALL endophthalmitis cases
epidemiology_aao.regional_pattern: IN ASIA specifically, the MOST COMMON cause of endogenous bacterial endophthalmitis is KLEBSIELLA species infection from LIVER ABSCESSES - a distinct regional pattern directly relevant to Indonesia, contrasting with Western patterns (Streptococcus/Staph aureus/Bacillus predominant in endocarditis/cutaneous/IVDU contexts)
epidemiology_aao.extraocular_focus_rate: An extraocular infectious focus exists in up to 90% of cases, even though the eye may be the ONLY location where infection is initially FOUND - active search for the source is essential
common_gram_negative: Enteric organisms: E. coli, KLEBSIELLA species
possible_sources: LIVER ABSCESS (especially relevant given the Asia/Klebsiella pattern)

diseases.name: Multiple Evanescent White Dot Syndrome (MEWDS)
key_features: White dots may DISAPPEAR before exam - imaging (FAF/OCT/ICGA) is key to diagnosis
Characteristic orange granular fovea with dulled reflex
ICGA: hypofluorescent spots often MORE numerous than clinically visible
Self-limiting, improves spontaneously by 8-10 weeks
Fovea often shows granular/stippled 'peau d'orange' appearance that may PERSIST even after the white retinal lesions themselves have faded - a residual clue useful even after acute lesions resolve
Lesions classically described as 'wreathlike' on high-magnification FA

treatment.escalation: Consider local/systemic treatment (or reconsider the diagnosis) if central macular involvement is substantial or multiple recurrences occur - recurrence is atypical enough for MEWDS that it should prompt diagnostic reconsideration

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Primarily posterior (choriocapillaritis); Kanski notes 'subtle posterior vitritis in 50%' - included as secondary.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: mean age at onset 28 years (range 10-50 per comparison table), moderate myopia typical.
diseases.prognosis: No treatment usually required. Vision typically returns to baseline within 1-2 months; imaging abnormalities mostly resolve, though residual photopsias/blind-spot enlargement may persist for months. Recurrences are RARE.
diseases.critical_reclassification_note: IMPORTANT: MEWDS has historically been associated/grouped with MFCPU, AZOOR, and acute macular neuroretinopathy (AMN). However, AAO Ch.9 explicitly states that multimodal imaging now suggests AMN is a MICROVASCULAR event in the deep capillary plexus of the macula, NOT a primary inflammatory/uveitic process - AMN was 'previously considered' a white dot syndrome but this classification is now considered OUTDATED. See acute_macular_neuroretinopathy entity for the corresponding flag.

diseases.name: Acute Posterior Multifocal Placoid Pigment Epitheliopathy (APMPPE)
key_features: Large deep yellow-white PLACOID lesions at posterior pole
Associated cerebral vasculitis possible - can cause STROKE
Poor prognostic factors: age >60, unilaterality at onset, >=6 month interval before fellow eye involved, recurrence, choroidal vein leakage
diseases.critical_note: RARE but potentially LIFE-THREATENING association with cerebral vasculitis. ANY APMPPE patient with CNS symptoms (even just headache) requires URGENT neurologic evaluation including neuroimaging and CSF studies - do not dismiss headache as incidental in this population.

treatment.typical: Often simply OBSERVED (self-limited disease) - corticosteroids may be considered to hasten lesion resolution, especially with extensive macular involvement
treatment.cns_vasculitis_present: PROMPT systemic corticosteroid treatment indicated to reduce CNS morbidity/mortality - this is the urgent exception to the usual observe-only approach

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Primarily posterior (choriocapillaritis); Kanski states 'anterior uveitis and vitritis are usually very mild' - included as secondary/mild, not primary.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO explicit: 'typically before age 50 years' - dewasa bucket, both sexes equally.
infectious_associations_aao: Post-vaccination: varicella, hepatitis B, swine flu, meningococcus vaccines
diseases.prognosis: Most patients: good prognosis, VA returns to 20/40+ within 6 months. However 20% left with residual visual dysfunction. Risk factors for vision loss: foveal involvement at presentation, OLDER age at presentation, UNILATERAL disease (paradoxically - vs bilateral), longer interval between initial and fellow-eye involvement, and recurrence.

diseases.name: Serpiginous Choroidopathy
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (idiopathic) - MUST exclude TB and syphilis (serpiginoid mimics)
key_features: Starts around optic disc, extends serpentine/snake-like pattern
Recurrent over years, ~25% end with VA <6/60
Associated with HLA-B7
diseases.differential: TB and syphilis can cause identical 'serpiginoid' picture - therapeutic approach very different, must rule out both

treatment.initial: Systemic and/or local corticosteroids to quiet active lesions, particularly fovea-threatening ones
treatment.early_imt_recommended: EARLY addition of systemic IMT improves long-term prognosis by reducing progression/recurrence - do not wait for steroid failure
imt_options: Cytotoxic therapy (cyclophosphamide or chlorambucil) - MORE SEVERE adverse effects than other IMT, but has been shown to induce LONG DRUG-FREE REMISSIONS specifically in serpiginous choroiditis - a notable exception where the more aggressive/toxic option has a unique remission-inducing benefit
TNF inhibitors (only once TB test negative and/or TB treated)
treatment.cnv_adjunct: Anti-VEGF and/or intravitreal corticosteroids for inflammatory CNV
treatment.tb_variant: TB-associated serpiginous-LIKE choroiditis requires quadruple anti-TB drug therapy, and may ALSO need corticosteroids/IMT for post-infection inflammation once TB is treated

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Primarily posterior; Kanski states 'anterior uveitis and vitritis are common but usually mild' - included as secondary/mild.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: affects adult men and women equally - age 20-60 per comparison table, broader than our earlier 'middle age' framing.
diseases.macular_variant_note: In up to 1/3 of patients, lesions arise PRIMARILY IN THE MACULA rather than the classic peripapillary-origin pattern - 'macular serpiginous choroiditis' variant. Important not to dismiss serpiginous choroiditis just because the lesion doesn't start at the disc.
diseases.cnv_risk: CNV develops at the border of an old scar in up to 25% of patients
associations: TB (causes a serpiginous-LIKE choroiditis - must always be excluded, see differential_note)
diseases.critical_note: Rare mimics that MUST be excluded: herpetic or syphilitic choroiditis, and especially TB (tuberculous uveitis can present as serpiginous-like/serpiginoid choroiditis - ALL patients with serpiginous features need TB screening, cross-reference tb_uveitis and source_conflict awareness already in KB).

diseases.name: Birdshot Retinochoroidopathy
key_features: Middle-aged women, HLA-A29 positive in >95%
Cream-coloured ovoid choroidal patches radiating from disc, sparing macula
Vitritis, floaters, nyctalopia, dyschromatopsia, photopsia
MONITOR WITH VISUAL FIELD TESTING, not visual acuity
diseases.prognosis: ~1/3 end with VA <6/60, though improving with aggressive sustained IMT; choroiditis responds better to treatment than the retinal component

treatment.first_line: Sub-Tenon triamcinolone especially if monocular disease
treatment.systemic: Up to 90% need systemic treatment - early referral to uveitis specialist
treatment.agents: Steroids initially, often needs cyclosporine or mycophenolate +/- infliximab
treatment.timeline: IMT typically takes 2-4 months for SOME therapeutic improvement, up to 6-12 months for FULL effect - set expectations accordingly. Some patients need combination therapy (typically an antimetabolite + anti-TNF agent).
treatment.intravitreal_option: Long-acting intravitreal fluocinolone acetonide insert (0.18mg) or implant (0.59mg) - option for patients who cannot tolerate systemic therapy (though may be insufficient as monotherapy alone) or as adjunct for breakthrough inflammation.
treatment.observation_criteria: Asymptomatic/minimally symptomatic patients WITHOUT objective findings (macular edema, angiographic edema/vasculitis, visual field/ERG dysfunction) may be closely monitored without treatment - but AAO notes the MAJORITY of BCR patients will require corticosteroids + steroid-sparing IMT.

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Primarily posterior (stromal choroiditis); Kanski: 'vitritis is present in either or both eyes' - included as secondary.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO Ch.9: age range 30-70 (was TRADITIONALLY 50-60, but now increasingly diagnosed <45yo due to better diagnostic testing) - broadened from our earlier Kanski-based 'middle-aged women' framing. Spans dewasa+lansia.
diseases.hla_a29_precision: AAO gives PRECISE test characteristics: HLA-A29 sensitivity 96%, specificity 93%. However, HLA-A29 occurs in ~7% of the general US population while birdshot itself is rare - so the allele ALONE (without compatible clinical findings) has LOW positive predictive value. Testing the allele is not a substitute for clinical pattern-matching.
differential_diagnosis_aao: sarcoidosis_uveitis (especially - can present with very similar chorioretinal lesion morphology/distribution)
diseases.critical_note: Despite good visual acuity and minimal vitreous cells, BCR can progress silently as diffuse inflammatory retinal degeneration (VF loss + ERG dysfunction) - monitoring VISUAL ACUITY ALONE will MISS disease progression. This reinforces our existing note to monitor with visual field, not VA - AAO adds that ERG should also be part of monitoring.

diseases.name: Punctate Inner Choroidopathy (PIC)
diseases.laterality: young myopic women, bilateral in 80% (often asymmetric)
key_features: Minimal/absent anterior uveitis and vitritis (unlike MFC)
Small yellow-white macular spots, evolve to atrophic scars resembling POHS
MNV in up to 40% - major cause of visual loss

treatment.mild: Active lesions may regress WITHOUT anti-inflammatory therapy; treatment may be limited to intermittent intravitreal anti-VEGF alone
treatment.escalation_indications: New/persistently active inflammatory lesions in vision-threatening locations, OR incomplete response to anti-VEGF alone -> add corticosteroids and/or systemic IMT
treatment.note: When inflammatory activity is controlled with systemic IMT, retinal function/VA may improve even in the PRESENCE of perifoveal CNV - i.e. treating the inflammation can help even without directly treating the CNV.

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski explicitly states anterior uveitis and vitritis are 'usually absent or very mild' - deliberately NOT tagged secondary here since this near-absence is itself a distinguishing feature vs MFC (which has 50% anterior uveitis).
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: median age at onset ~30 years (range 20-40 per comparison table).
diseases.comparison_vs_mfcpu: PIC lesions SMALLER (100-300um) than MFCPU lesions (>125um, often larger), more confined to POSTERIOR POLE. PIC patients are MORE LIKELY than MFCPU patients to develop CNV, but LESS LIKELY to have bilateral vision loss - i.e. PIC has higher CNV risk per-lesion but the overall visual morbidity burden is more contained than MFCPU.

diseases.name: Acute Macular Neuroretinopathy (AMN)
key_features: Young healthy women, flu-like prodrome, increased incidence since COVID-19 era
Wedge-shaped parafoveal lesions best seen on INFRARED imaging
OCT: hyperreflectivity starting at outer plexiform layer
FA usually normal or faint hypofluorescence

treatment.approach: No proven treatment; self-limited but scotoma often persists

diseases.age_group_note: Kanski: 'typically affects healthy young adult women.'
diseases.reclassification_note_aao: IMPORTANT RECLASSIFICATION: Kanski Ch.12 (our original source for this entity) presents AMN within the 'inflammatory conditions of the posterior pole' section, implying an inflammatory/uveitic process. However, AAO BCSC Ch.9 (2025-2026) explicitly states AMN is now understood to be a MICROVASCULAR event in the deep capillary plexus of the macula, NOT a primary inflammatory process - AMN was 'previously considered' a white dot syndrome/uveitic entity but current multimodal imaging evidence has REDEFINED it as vascular, not inflammatory. AAO refers readers to BCSC Section 12 (Retina and Vitreous) rather than covering AMN as a uveitic entity in this uveitis textbook.
diseases.tool_scoring_implication: Given this reclassification, AMN should probably be treated with LOWER priority/different framing in a uveitis differential diagnosis tool - it may be more appropriate as a 'consider this non-inflammatory mimic' flag rather than a peer differential alongside true white dot syndromes. Retained in this KB for completeness and because Kanski (a primary source) still frames it as uveitis-adjacent, but this AAO note should be surfaced to the user if this entity scores highly, so they understand the current classification controversy/evolution rather than assuming it's confirmed inflammatory.

diseases.name: Herpes Simplex Anterior Uveitis
diseases.granulomatous: variable - KP may be fine/stellate/diffuse (Fuchs-like) OR large and grouped
diseases.laterality: unilateral (may occur with or without active corneal disease)
key_features: Acute OR chronic anterior uveitis, +/- keratitis; classically UNILATERAL (bilateral reported but atypical)
Dermatitis/rash/conjunctivitis/episcleritis/scleritis MAY accompany HSV/VZV uveitis but TYPICALLY NOT CMV-associated uveitis - presence of these external signs favors HSV/VZV over CMV
Corneal findings: DENDRITIFORM epithelial involvement, reduced corneal sensation, and neurotrophic keratitis are seen with HSV/VZV but NOT CMV - a key negative discriminator for CMV
Stromal keratitis MORE typical of HSV/VZV than CMV
Hyphema, hypopyon, and posterior synechiae CAN occur with HSV/VZV but do NOT routinely occur with CMV-associated anterior uveitis
Iris atrophy (patchy or sectoral) is a LATER finding - NOT present in the first days-to-weeks of an initial episode, visible as transillumination defects on retroillumination

iop_pattern.detail: Elevated IOP is common in herpes simplex anterior uveitis, alongside patchy/sectoral iris atrophy. AAO Ch.12 gives PRECISE numbers: herpetic trabeculitis can cause IOP as high as 50-60 mmHg. This is a helpful diagnostic HALLMARK - most other uveitic entities cause LOW IOP (ciliary body hyposecretion), so acute high IOP with anterior uveitis should raise herpetic suspicion.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Herpes Simplex Anterior Uveitis section

kp_distribution.pattern: diffuse_beyond_midline (variable - can also be large/grouped rather than fine)
kp_distribution.detail: KP may be fine/stellate/diffuse across the cornea (Fuchs-like, extending beyond Arlt's triangle) OR large and grouped - variable morphology is itself part of the diagnostic ambiguity with CMV/VZV. AAO clarifies further: diffuse STELLATE KP occurs with ALL THREE herpetic viruses (HSV/VZV/CMV) - this is NOT itself discriminating between them. What IS discriminating: ring-shaped clusters or scant small white-domed KP are specifically PATHOGNOMONIC for CMV (not HSV/VZV).
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (KP morphology) + cross-referenced literature on Arlt's triangle exceptions (non-Kanski)

diseases.differential_note: Distinguishing herpetic from CMV iridocyclitis can be difficult - both have elevated IOP; CMV tends to have LESS ciliary injection, LIMITED flare, FEW cells, and PS are RARE (vs herpetic which may have more prominent anterior chamber activity).

investigations.name: Clinical history (recurrent same-eye episodes, keratitis history)

antiviral_dosing.note: HIGHER doses generally needed for VZV than HSV

treatment.adjuncts: Topical corticosteroids + cycloplegic agents + IOP-lowering agents (often needed throughout the course, sometimes tapered as inflammation controlled; recalcitrant IOP elevation may need surgery)
treatment.systemic_steroid_caution: Systemic corticosteroids sometimes necessary but should ONLY be used WITH concurrent systemic antiviral therapy
treatment.prophylaxis: Long-term suppressive low-dose antiviral may be indicated (though randomized trial evidence is lacking): acyclovir 400mg BID (HSV) or 800mg BID (VZV), or valacyclovir 1g/day
treatment.diagnostic_trial_logic: If suspected herpetic anterior uveitis does NOT improve with empiric acyclovir/valacyclovir, this treatment FAILURE itself should prompt PCR testing of aqueous for CMV - non-response is diagnostically informative, not just a treatment problem.
treatment.workup_threshold: Anterior chamber paracentesis to distinguish HSV/VZV from CMV should be considered WHEN diagnostic uncertainty would actually CHANGE treatment - not a reflexive test for every case

diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski Ch.12 for this entity. (Non-Kanski source cited elsewhere in this entity's differential_note mentions HSV anterior uveitis 'more common among people under 60' - supplementary, not used here per strict-Kanski-only rule for this field.)

diseases.name: Varicella Zoster Virus (VZV) Anterior Uveitis
diseases.course: starts 1-3 weeks after acute skin rash (herpes zoster ophthalmicus, HZO); may be recurrent
diseases.laterality: unilateral, associated with ipsilateral HZO history
key_features: Primary VZV infection (chickenpox): up to 40% develop a BILATERAL acute nongranulomatous anterior uveitis that is ASYMPTOMATIC, MILD, and SELF-LIMITING - often unrecognized
HZO (reactivation in V1 dermatome): Hutchinson sign (vesicles at nose tip) indicates nasociliary nerve involvement and INCREASED likelihood of ocular involvement - actively look for this sign
VZV can cause chronic UNILATERAL anterior uveitis even when cutaneous component was in the PAST or MINIMAL/ABSENT (zoster sine herpete) - don't require an obvious rash history to consider VZV
Trabeculitis can cause very high IOP (50-60mmHg) - same mechanism/magnitude as HSV
HZO can be associated with VASCULITIS leading to anterior segment ischemia, retinal artery occlusion, and scleritis; orbital vasculitis may cause cranial nerve palsies

iop_pattern.detail: Sectoral iris atrophy often present, corneal sensation may be reduced and IOP raised.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, VZV Anterior Uveitis section

kp_distribution.detail: Like HSV and CMV anterior uveitis, VZV-related KP typically distribute diffusely across the entire corneal endothelium rather than being confined to the classic inferior Arlt's-triangle zone - part of the broader viral anterior uveitis triad pattern.
kp_distribution.source_note: Cross-referenced literature on Arlt's triangle exceptions (non-Kanski) - Kanski itself does not detail KP distribution specifically for VZV

investigations.name: PCR analysis of aqueous
investigations.note: indicated exceptionally

antiviral_dosing.valacyclovir: 500mg-1g 3x/day (higher doses typically needed for VZV vs HSV)

treatment.note: Same general approach as HSV - see herpes_simplex_anterior_uveitis treatment entry for shared details (adjuncts, steroid caution, prophylaxis dosing)

diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski Ch.12 for this entity.
diseases.clinical_pearl_aao: AAO Clinical Pearl - herpesvirus-associated anterior uveitis features: unilateral presentation, ocular hypertension WITH acute inflammation, decreased corneal sensation + epithelial/stromal disease (MORE common HSV/VZV), endotheliitis, corneal stromal immune ring (MORE likely CMV). Iris atrophy/transillumination defects are LATE findings, not present at initial presentation.

diseases.name: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) Anterior Uveitis
key_features: KP pattern: ring-shaped clusters OR scant small white-domed KP - PATHOGNOMONIC for CMV (distinguishes from the diffuse stellate KP pattern shared by all three herpetic viruses)
Corneal stromal immune ring is MORE LIKELY with CMV than HSV/VZV
Associated with GLAUCOMATOCYCLITIC CRISIS (cross-reference posner_schlossman_syndrome) and FUCHS UVEITIS SYNDROME - both can present with elevated IOP

Does NOT typically show: dermatitis/rash/conjunctivitis/episcleritis/scleritis (unlike HSV/VZV), dendritiform epithelial disease, reduced corneal sensation, neurotrophic keratitis, or routine hyphema/hypopyon/PS - absence of these HSV/VZV-typical features supports CMV

iop_pattern.pattern: elevated - most prominent/consistent feature among the three viral anterior uveitides
iop_pattern.detail: Elevated IOP is very common. CMV has been reported as a cause of Posner-Schlossman syndrome. This, combined with minimal ciliary injection/flare/cells and RARE posterior synechiae, helps distinguish CMV from more inflamed HSV/VZV anterior uveitis.
iop_pattern.source_note: Kanski Ch.12, Cytomegalovirus Anterior Uveitis section

kp_distribution.detail: KP of variable morphology distributed diffusely (not confined to Arlt's triangle), often described as small/medium and sometimes coin-shaped endotheliitis-related lesions in the literature - contributes to the low-injection, low-flare, diffuse-KP picture typical of CMV anterior uveitis.
kp_distribution.source_note: Kanski Ch.12 (KP of a range of morphology) + cross-referenced literature on Arlt's triangle exceptions (non-Kanski)

diseases.differential_note: vs HSV/VZV anterior uveitis: CMV has LESS injection, LESS flare, FEWER cells, PS RARE, and characteristically FAILS TO RESPOND to aciclovir/steroids - this non-response can itself be the diagnostic clue.

investigations.name: PCR and antibody assay of aqueous sample
investigations.note: should be considered if clinical suspicion

investigations.name: Trial of aciclovir/steroid response (diagnostic by failure to respond)

treatment.standardization_caveat: There is NO standardized antiviral therapy for CMV-associated anterior uveitis (unlike HSV/VZV)
treatment.historical_approach: Previously managed with topical corticosteroids + IOP-lowering agents ALONE (no antiviral)
treatment.limitations: Long-term systemic antiviral treatment may be limited by drug TOXICITY or RESISTANCE; relapses are COMMON after discontinuation of therapy - set expectations for a chronic/relapsing course accordingly

diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski Ch.12 for this entity.
diseases.mechanism_of_antiviral_resistance: CRITICAL MECHANISTIC FACT: CMV-associated anterior uveitis will NOT respond to the antiviral therapy used for HSV/VZV (acyclovir/valacyclovir/famciclovir) because CMV LACKS the virally-encoded THYMIDINE KINASE necessary for these drugs' metabolism/activation. This explains WHY treatment non-response is itself a diagnostic clue pointing toward CMV.

diseases.name: Phacoantigenic Uveitis (Lens-Induced, immune-mediated)
key_features: Immune response to lens proteins following incomplete cataract extraction, trauma, or rarely capsular degeneration in mature cataract
Most common modern scenario: RETAINED LENS FRAGMENTS after phacoemulsification (posterior segment after capsular rupture/zonular dehiscence, or overlooked fragment in AC)
Corneal oedema common adjacent to AC lens fragment
IOP FREQUENTLY ELEVATED
Lens fragments may be visible in anterior or posterior segment
Vitritis if fragments lie within vitreous cavity
Can occur within 24 HOURS of capsular rupture (e.g. cataract extraction in the fellow eye previously sensitizing the patient)
Histology: ZONAL granulomatous inflammation at site of lens injury - neutrophils clustered around lens material with surrounding lymphocytes/plasma cells/epithelioid cells/occasional giant cells

iop_pattern.detail: IOP is frequently elevated in lens-induced uveitis, often with corneal oedema adjacent to an AC lens fragment.

diseases.differential_note: Must be differentiated from bacterial endophthalmitis, which is usually more severe.

treatment.definitive: Surgical removal of all lens material from AC or via pars plana vitrectomy as required
treatment.conservative_option: Small lens fragments in posterior segment can often be managed conservatively - absorb slowly over months
treatment.adjunct: Cycloplegia and IOP-lowering treatment commonly indicated
treatment.complications: CMO, glaucoma, epiretinal membrane, rarely retinal detachment/cyclitic membrane

diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Kanski: vitritis of variable severity 'usually present if lens fragments lie within the vitreous cavity' - included as secondary, dependent on fragment location.
diseases.age_group_note: No explicit age range given by Kanski; described by clinical scenario (post-cataract surgery/trauma), not age.
diseases.aao_reclassification_note: AAO BCSC Ch.8 splits 'lens-induced uveitis' into TWO distinct clinical entities with different mechanisms: (1) PHACOANTIGENIC uveitis (immune reaction to lens protein after capsule disruption - THIS entity, matches our existing Kanski-derived content: elevated IOP, PS common, small/large KP, granulomatous OR non-granulomatous) and (2) PHACOLYTIC uveitis (mechanical - macrophages clog trabecular meshwork from leaked lens protein through an INTACT capsule in mature/hypermature cataract - see new phacolytic_uveitis entity, KP and synechiae typically ABSENT, refractile bodies characteristic). Previously our KB conflated these; now split per AAO's clearer distinction.
diseases.critical_note: Must differentiate from postoperative INFECTIOUS endophthalmitis - visual outcomes deteriorate rapidly in infectious endophthalmitis if not treated quickly/aggressively, so this distinction is time-critical, not just academic.

diseases.name: Tubulointerstitial Nephritis and Uveitis (TINU) Syndrome
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.5 Table 5-10/5-11.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Per AAO Table 5-10, TINU presents as anterior uveitis; per Kanski (already noted elsewhere in our reference.md), it can also be intermediate/posterior/panuveitis - AAO's table entry is simplified to its most common anterior presentation.
diseases.course: acute, may be recurrent; renal disease often resolves but ocular inflammation can become chronic
diseases.age_group_note: AAO BCSC Ch.8: 'median age at onset of 15 years' but occurs across a WIDE age range - originally described in adolescent girls/women up to early 30s, now recognized in broader age range. Median 15 falls in remaja bucket, but the described range (up to early 30s) extends into dewasa.
key_features: Anterior uveitis: acute, BILATERAL, nongranulomatous, may be recurrent
Symptoms MORE SEVERE with recurrent disease: fibrin, posterior synechiae, LARGER KPs, rarely hypopyon

Posterior segment involvement CAN occur: vitritis, multifocal chorioretinal lesions/scars, retinal vascular leakage, optic disc/macular edema (broader than 'anterior only' - update our anatomic_class if needed)
Systemic symptoms often PRECEDE uveitis, but uveitis can also precede systemic symptoms/renal disease - order is NOT fixed
IMPORTANT: renal disease flares do NOT occur simultaneously with ocular flares - the two organ systems can flare independently and asynchronously

kp_distribution.detail: Not specified in AAO Ch.5 excerpt

treatment.initial: Very RESPONSIVE to high-dose oral corticosteroids initially
treatment.course: Renal disease tends to RESOLVE over time; OCULAR inflammation can become CHRONIC and require systemic IMT - i.e. the eye disease often outlasts the kidney disease and needs separate, longer-term management

required_for_clinical_diagnosis: Abnormal serum creatinine OR decreased creatinine clearance
Abnormal urinalysis: increased urinary beta-2-microglobulin, proteinuria, eosinophils, pyuria/hematuria, urinary white cell casts, normoglycemic glycosuria
Associated systemic illness: fever, weight loss, anorexia, fatigue, arthralgias, myalgias (+/- abnormal LFTs, eosinophilia, elevated ESR)

diseases.pathogenesis_note: Etiology unclear. Strongly associated with HLA-DRB1*01:02. Predominance of activated CD4+ T lymphocytes in kidney interstitium suggests cellular immunity role. Renal biopsy shows severe interstitial fibrosis. Seroreactivity against BOTH retinal AND renal antigens demonstrated - suggests a shared-antigen autoimmune mechanism (similar concept to the MS/Muller-cell cross-reactivity theory). Infection and drug reaction are potential triggers.

diseases.name: Posner-Schlossman Syndrome (Glaucomatocyclitic Crisis)
diseases.source: Formalized as its own entity - previously only referenced as a note under cmv_anterior_uveitis. AAO BCSC Ch.5 Table 5-10 explicitly names this as a distinct suspected-disease category.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious/possibly viral (increasingly linked to CMV per Kanski cross-reference)
diseases.course: recurrent, episodic (hours to several days per episode, recurring over many years)
key_features: Recurrent UNILATERAL, acute, MILD nongranulomatous anterior uveitis with markedly elevated IOP
Symptoms vague: discomfort, blurred vision, halos (from corneal edema)
Signs: corneal edema, SMALL KPs, LOW-GRADE AC inflammation (disproportionate to the IOP spike), slightly dilated pupil
An INCREASE in KP number is often noted with recurrences

iop_pattern.pattern: elevated - markedly, often disproportionate to the degree of visible inflammation
iop_pattern.detail: AAO explicitly groups 'increased IOP' with herpetic iritis and Posner-Schlossman/glaucomatocyclitic crisis as a shared clinical branch point in its differential diagnosis table - i.e. these are the diseases to consider FIRST when IOP spikes accompany anterior uveitis.

diseases.differential_note: Cross-reference cmv_anterior_uveitis - increasingly recognized as CMV-associated in a subset of cases (see cmv_anterior_uveitis.iop_pattern note in this KB, sourced from Kanski). AAO's Table 5-10 lists Posner-Schlossman/glaucomatocyclitic crisis and herpetic iritis together under the SAME clinical branch ('Increased IOP' associated factor) - supports treating them as overlapping differentials to consider together, not mutually exclusive. AAO Ch.8 note: comparison studies exist specifically distinguishing CMV-positive vs CMV-negative Fuchs uveitis syndrome AND Posner-Schlossman - these two entities (fuchs_uveitis_syndrome and posner_schlossman_syndrome) are increasingly viewed as related/overlapping viral anterior uveitis phenomena rather than fully distinct diseases.

investigations.name: PCR of aqueous humor for CMV DNA
investigations.note: Consider especially if corneal endotheliitis + linear KP + male patient - may guide antiviral treatment trial

treatment.traditional: Topical corticosteroids + antiglaucoma medications (including systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitors if necessary)
treatment.monitoring: Long-term surveillance required in addition to careful IOP monitoring
treatment.cmv_associated_subset: Consider antiviral therapy if CMV DNA confirmed on aqueous PCR

diseases.etiology_note_aao: Recent studies suggest ocular CMV infection as a possible cause in a subset of cases, with specific CMV genotypes associated with anterior-uveitis presentation vs retinitis presentation. CMV-ASSOCIATED subset specifically shows: corneal ENDOTHELIITIS, LINEAR (not diffuse-stellate) KP pattern, and MALE preponderance - these three features help identify which Posner-Schlossman cases are likely CMV-driven and might benefit from antiviral therapy.

diseases.name: Uveitis-Glaucoma-Hyphema (UGH) Syndrome
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.5 Table 5-10.
key_features: Occurs specifically in PSEUDOPHAKIC patients (post-cataract surgery with IOL)
Triad: uveitis + glaucoma (elevated IOP) + hyphema (esp. recurrent MICROhyphema)
Mechanism: chafing of iris (anterior or posterior surface) by IOL edges/loops/haptics causes mechanical irritation
MORE common with older RIGID anterior chamber IOLs (ACIOLs) vs newer ACIOLs and scleral-fixated IOLs

Since ACIOL use is now rare, UGH today is encountered MOST COMMONLY with UNINTENTIONAL SULCUS placement of a single-piece hydrophobic acrylic IOL (which is designed for in-the-bag placement, not sulcus) - this is the key modern clinical scenario to recognize
Can occur even with technically APPROPRIATE IOL placement if the IOL-capsule complex is mobile (e.g. in pseudoexfoliation syndrome)

iop_pattern.detail: IOP elevation is part of the defining triad

diseases.differential_note: AAO Table 5-10 lists this specifically under the 'Increased IOP' branch of anterior uveitis differential, alongside Posner-Schlossman/glaucomatocyclitic crisis and herpetic iritis - KEY DISTINGUISHING CONTEXT is pseudophakic status, which the other two do not require.

investigations.name: Slit-lamp exam for IOL position/chafing, retroillumination for iris transillumination defects
Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) or anterior segment OCT
investigations.note: Evaluate IOL position/tilt relative to iris/sulcus - AAO Fig 8-11 shows UBM revealing a tilted 1-piece PC IOL with haptic in sulcus

treatment.first_line: Topical corticosteroids and/or cycloplegia for many cases
treatment.escalation: Some cases require IOL REPOSITIONING or EXPLANTATION
clinical_pearl_workup_for_persistent_pos: Evaluate for iris chafing even if IOL appears to be correctly in the capsular bag
Investigate infectious causes including Cutibacterium acnes
Use topical corticosteroids tapered VERY SLOWLY
Employ systemic IMT if necessary, ONLY AFTER infectious causes sufficiently investigated

diseases.spectrum_note: IOL-associated uveitis exists on a SPECTRUM from mild inflammation up to full UGH syndrome - not every case of IOL-chafing uveitis has all 3 components (uveitis+glaucoma+hyphema) at once.
diseases.other_iol_complications: Iris-supported IOL or ACIOL motion can cause intermittent corneal touch -> corneal edema (endothelial damage); other IOL-contact complications include low-grade anterior uveitis, peripheral anterior synechiae, recurrent microhyphema, recalcitrant glaucoma, macular edema. ACIOLs should be removed/exchanged when penetrating keratoplasty is performed.

diseases.name: Toxic Anterior Segment Syndrome (TASS)
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.5 Table 5-10.
key_features: Postsurgical (typically cataract surgery) or penetrating eye trauma / indwelling systemic lines-instrumentation-infection context per AAO Table 5-10
Acute sterile inflammatory reaction from a toxic contaminant introduced during surgery (e.g. residue on instruments, incorrect solution concentration) - NOT an infection
diseases.differential_note: AAO Table 5-10 lists TASS in the SAME differential branch as infectious endophthalmitis (both triggered by 'postsurgical or penetrating eye trauma or systemic indwelling lines/instrumentation/infection') - the critical distinguishing workup is B-scan/vitreous culture to RULE OUT true infection, since TASS is sterile and infectious endophthalmitis is not; misdiagnosing one as the other has serious treatment implications (TASS = steroids helpful; infectious endophthalmitis = steroids alone can be catastrophic without antimicrobials).

investigations.name: B-scan for vitritis (to help exclude/assess for endophthalmitis)
Vitreous culture (to rule out infectious endophthalmitis)

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - general principle is aggressive topical/systemic steroid once infection is excluded, since TASS is a sterile toxic reaction, not needing antimicrobials.

diseases.name: Chronic Postoperative (Low-Grade) Endophthalmitis - Cutibacterium acnes
diseases.source: MASSIVELY ENRICHED from stub - full detail now available from AAO BCSC Ch.14 (Endophthalmitis), as originally flagged/anticipated.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (bacterial - Cutibacterium acnes, formerly Propionibacterium acnes; low-virulence)
key_features: Distinctive course: MULTIPLE RECURRENCES of chronic indolent inflammation in a previously-operated eye (typically cataract extraction) - recurrence pattern itself is a hallmark
Onset timing VARIABLE - can occur at ANY point postoperatively but often DELAYED by many MONTHS (contrast with acute postop endophthalmitis which is explosive within days)
Findings: chronic anterior segment inflammation, hypopyon, KP, INTRACAPSULAR PLAQUES (whitish plaques between posterior capsule and IOL - classic C. acnes finding), +/- vitritis
Responds to corticosteroid therapy BUT RECURS after steroid taper - this steroid-responsive-but-recurring pattern is itself a diagnostic clue distinguishing it from purely noninfectious causes
Severe cases: corneal decompensation or iris neovascularization possible
C. acnes sequesters in the RELATIVELY ANAEROBIC space between IOL and posterior capsule, forming colonies visible as whitish plaques

ND:YAG CAPSULOTOMY CAN TRIGGER/WORSEN disease by liberating sequestered organisms into the vitreous cavity - important procedural trigger to be aware of, avoid YAG capsulotomy if this diagnosis is suspected
diseases.differential_note: AAO Table 5-10 lists this in BOTH the 'moderate severity' AND 'chronic' anterior uveitis branches under 'postsurgical' context - important to consider in ANY postsurgical eye with atypical, treatment-resistant chronic uveitis, not just acute presentations.

investigations.name: Aerobic, anaerobic, AND fungal cultures of intraocular fluids (aqueous, capsular plaque, vitreous)
investigations.note: Needle aspiration or pars plana vitrectomy for sampling

investigations.name: Gram stain and fungal stain
investigations.note: Value should NOT be underestimated, especially for fungal cases - can give rapid actionable information long before culture positivity

investigations.name: PCR with C. acnes-specific primers + pan-fungal + pan-bacterial targets
Extended culture hold
investigations.note: IMPORTANT: cultures must be held by microbiology lab for >=2 WEEKS given slow-growing/fastidious organisms - standard shorter hold times may cause false-negative results. If initial cultures negative but suspicion remains high, REPEAT.

treatment.bacterial_stepwise: Ranges from intravitreal antibiotic ALONE, up to pars plana vitrectomy + intravitreal antibiotic +/- PARTIAL capsulectomy. May not fully eradicate if organism sequestered in equatorial lens capsule.
treatment.definitive_bacterial: IOL EXPLANTATION + COMPLETE capsulectomy + intravitreal vancomycin injection may be CURATIVE when partial measures fail - decision made case-by-case based on clinical course/inflammation severity/vision loss. Literature suggests MORE THAN ONE surgical procedure often needed.
treatment.fungal_more_difficult: Requires WEEKLY intravitreal antifungal injections (amphotericin or voriconazole) +/- systemic antifungal in severe cases. In VITRECTOMIZED eyes, antifungals often injected TWICE weekly (faster clearance). MULTIPLE vitrectomies may be necessary.

bacterial_vs_fungal_variant.bacterial_most_common: Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium acnes) - a commensal anaerobic pleomorphic gram-positive rod normally found on eyelid skin/conjunctiva
bacterial_vs_fungal_variant.other_bacterial: Staph epidermidis, Corynebacterium species (low-virulence gram-positive), gram-negative bacteria, Mycobacterium species
bacterial_vs_fungal_variant.fungal_organisms: Candida parapsilosis, Aspergillus flavus, Torulopsis candida, Paecilomyces lilacinus, Verticillium species - can present SIMILARLY to C. acnes disease
bacterial_vs_fungal_variant.fungal_discriminating_signs: Corneal infiltrate/edema, mass in iris/ciliary body, necrotizing scleritis development, OR vitreous snowballs with 'STRING-OF-PEARLS' appearance - these favor fungal over bacterial
bacterial_vs_fungal_variant.critical_clue: Intraocular inflammation WORSENING after topical/periocular/intraocular CORTICOSTEROID therapy should AUTOMATICALLY raise suspicion for FUNGAL etiology specifically (over C. acnes bacterial)

differential_diagnosis_clinical_pearl.source: AAO Ch.14 Clinical Pearl - differential for chronic postoperative intraocular inflammation

diseases.broader_differential: Also consider: lens_induced_uveitis / phacolytic_uveitis (retained cortical material or intravitreal lens fragments), sympathetic_ophthalmia (if FELLOW eye had prior surgery/trauma), vitreoretinal_lymphoma (masquerade).

diseases.name: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener granulomatosis) - ocular
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.5 Table 5-10 (also appears in Ch.10 TOC as a dedicated topic, not yet extracted in full).
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: AAO Table 5-10 lists GPA under the posterior uveitis 'Vasculitis' branch; also listed in Table 5-4 as causing BOTH arteritis and phlebitis (arteritis_and_phlebitis category). Kanski (already in our KB) separately notes cytoplasmic ANCA (c-ANCA) association with scleritis and/or peripheral ulcerative keratitis as evidence of GPA, but does not give GPA its own full uveitis entity - AAO gives it more prominence as a distinct panuveitis/posterior-with-systemic-manifestation entity (Ch.10 dedicated section, not yet extracted).
key_features: Sinusitis with BLOODY nasal discharge, pulmonary symptoms, arthritis
Dermatologic: purpura (often lower extremities), ulcers, subcutaneous nodules in 50%
Neurologic in 1/3: mononeuritis multiplex (MOST common), cranial neuropathies, seizures, stroke, cerebral vasculitis

Orbital involvement can be from CONTIGUOUS SPREAD of sinus disease, or a distinct orbital pseudotumor; can progress to orbital cellulitis/dacryocystitis via secondary infection; can cause COMPRESSIVE ischemic optic neuropathy

vs Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA): related ANCA-vasculitis, MPA biopsy LACKS granulomatous findings (GPA has them), MPA associated with p-ANCA/MPO (GPA with c-ANCA/PR3), ophthalmic involvement LESS common in MPA than GPA

investigations.name: c-ANCA / anti-PR3 ELISA
investigations.note: c-ANCA pattern sensitive AND specific for GPA, present in up to 95% of affected patients. PR3 = most common target antigen. Best practice: use BOTH indirect immunofluorescence (more sensitive) AND ELISA for PR3-ANCA/MPO-ANCA (more specific).

investigations.name: p-ANCA / anti-MPO
investigations.note: Associated with MPA, renal-limited vasculitis, pauci-immune GN instead of GPA

investigations.name: Chest radiography
investigations.note: May show nodular, diffuse, or cavitary lesions

investigations.name: Tissue biopsy (necrotizing/granulomatous inflammation)
investigations.note: Establishes histologic diagnosis

treatment.regimen: Systemic corticosteroids + IMT such as rituximab or cyclophosphamide
treatment.role_of_ophthalmologist: CRITICAL for early recognition - timely diagnosis/treatment may prevent both ocular morbidity AND patient MORTALITY, not just vision loss

classic_triad: Necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis of upper AND lower respiratory tract
Necrotizing vasculitis of small arteries and veins

epidemiology_aao.glomerulonephritis: Develops in 85% eventually - HIGH mortality risk, so early detection important
epidemiology_aao.ocular_over_time: Up to 50% develop ocular involvement OVER TIME (even if absent initially)
epidemiology_aao.scleritis_rate: Up to 40% of GPA patients have scleritis of ANY type, particularly diffuse anterior or necrotizing, +/- peripheral ulcerative keratitis
epidemiology_aao.uveitis_rate: ~10% of ophthalmic GPA patients have nonspecific unilateral/bilateral anterior, intermediate, or posterior uveitis with varying vitritis
epidemiology_aao.vision_loss: Up to 40% of GPA patients, especially with long-standing/inadequately treated disease
epidemiology_aao.untreated_mortality: 1-year mortality WITHOUT treatment: 80% - this is a life-threatening systemic disease, not just an eye disease

iop_pattern.detail: Not a defining IOP feature per AAO Ch.10 - vision loss mechanisms are more via scleritis/keratitis/vasculitis/optic neuropathy than IOP elevation specifically.

diseases.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) - ocular/retinal vasculitis
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.5 Table 5-10 (also a dedicated Ch.10 topic, not yet extracted in full).
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: AAO Table 5-10 lists SLE under posterior uveitis 'Vasculitis' branch; Table 5-4 classifies SLE as 'Primarily Arteritis'.
key_features: Lupus RETINOPATHY (the most common posterior finding) - a VASCULOPATHY not a vasculitis: cotton-wool spots, retinal nonperfusion/infarction via HYPERCOAGULABLE STATE from autoantibodies causing thrombosis (arterial and/or venous) - NOT primary inflammatory vessel wall infiltration
Prevalence of lupus retinopathy: 3% in mild systemic SLE, up to 29% in more ACTIVE systemic disease - correlates with systemic disease activity
Severe vaso-occlusive disease associated with CNS lupus and antiphospholipid antibodies
Lupus CHOROIDOPATHY: choroidal thrombosis -> choroidal infarction, multifocal RPE detachments, subretinal fluid
Extensive nonperfusion can cause retinal neovascularization and vitreous hemorrhage

True inflammatory uveitis is UNCOMMON/RARE - if genuine intraocular inflammation is prominent, consider whether it's actually a DIFFERENT diagnosis (SLE is often a distractor/incidental finding rather than the true uveitis cause)

investigations.name: ANA, anti-dsDNA, C3, C4
investigations.note: Reserve for patients with SLE-suggestive signs/symptoms, not as routine uveitis screening (see ana_testing_caveat)

investigations.name: Antiphospholipid antibodies
investigations.note: Especially if vaso-occlusive disease present

treatment.primary_approach: Treat the underlying SYSTEMIC disease - corticosteroids and IMT including hydroxychloroquine
treatment.vaso_occlusive_or_antiphospholipid: May require antiplatelet therapy or systemic anticoagulation
treatment.severe_sight_threatening: Aggressive therapy: IV immunoglobulin, cyclophosphamide, and/or plasma exchange
treatment.ischemic_complications: Panretinal photocoagulation, intravitreal anti-VEGF, vitrectomy as needed for proliferative retinopathy/vitreous hemorrhage
treatment.hydroxychloroquine_monitoring: IMPORTANT: patients on hydroxychloroquine need REGULAR ophthalmic exams to screen for retinal TOXICITY (a separate iatrogenic concern from the SLE disease itself) - per 2020 joint ACR/AAD/Rheum Derm Society/AAO statement

diseases.major_correction_note: IMPORTANT REFINEMENT from AAO BCSC Ch.10: 'the association between SLE and uveitis is LOW' - true uveitis is only RARELY associated with SLE, despite SLE being a classic teaching point in uveitis differentials. The MOST COMMON posterior segment manifestation is 'lupus retinopathy', which is fundamentally a VASCULOPATHY (via a hypercoagulable state from autoantibodies causing arterial/venous thrombosis) rather than a true inflammatory retinal vasculitis. Our entity here should be understood as covering this broader SLE-ocular-involvement picture, of which TRUE uveitis is the rare/minor component, not the main feature.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: affects primarily women of CHILDBEARING age; higher incidence among Black and Hispanic women in the US.

epidemiology_aao.systemic_manifestation_rates: Acute cutaneous disease (malar rash, discoid lupus, photosensitivity, mucosal lesions) 70-80%; arthritis 80-85%; renal disease 50-75%; Raynaud phenomenon 30-50%; neurologic involvement 35%
epidemiology_aao.ocular_manifestation_rate: Ocular manifestations occur in 50% of SLE patients overall (this includes eyelid discoid lesions, secondary Sjogren, scleritis, cranial nerve palsies, optic neuropathy, retinal/choroidal vasculopathy - NOT primarily uveitis)
epidemiology_aao.ana_testing_caveat: Recent analysis: ANA testing in the context of intraocular inflammation has a LOW positive predictive value of only 4.4% - ANA testing should be LIMITED to patients with SLE-suggestive signs/symptoms, or JIA patients (where ANA has separate, validated uveitis-risk-stratification value) - NOT used as a generic uveitis screening test.

diseases.differential_note: Distinguish lupus retinopathy from HYPERTENSIVE retinopathy secondary to SLE-induced hypertension/nephritis - both can show arteriolar narrowing, retinal hemorrhage, disc edema, and can coexist in the same patient.

diseases.name: Multiple Sclerosis (MS)-associated Intermediate Uveitis
diseases.source: Formalized as its own entity - previously only mentioned inline within lyme_uveitis/tb_uveitis differential notes. AAO BCSC Ch.5 Table 5-10 and dedicated Ch.8 subsection 'Intermediate Uveitis > Multiple Sclerosis' (TOC confirms; full Ch.8 text not yet extracted).
key_features: Intermediate uveitis is the MOST COMMON manifestation of MS-associated uveitis
BILATERAL in 95% of cases
Most patients have MILD vitritis with periphlebitis
Retinal vasculitis pattern (if present): PRIMARILY PHLEBITIS (matches iop_reference/vasculitis-type table already in KB)
Compared to IDIOPATHIC intermediate uveitis: MS-associated IU is MILDER overall, and MACULAR EDEMA IS LESS COMMON - this severity/pattern difference itself is a discriminating clue
Periphlebitis severity is NOT clearly correlated with optic neuritis, systemic exacerbations, or MS disease severity - ocular and neurologic activity can be independent

investigations.name: MRI of brain and C-spine
investigations.note: ONLY if neurologic signs/symptoms present, OR before starting a TNF inhibitor regardless of symptoms

treatment.shared_mechanism_drugs: Some MS disease-modifying therapies ALSO treat MS-associated uveitis/macular edema: Interferon shown effective for macular edema in MS-associated uveitis. Ocrelizumab (humanized anti-CD20, similar mechanism to rituximab) is an MS disease-modifying therapy that may help via a mechanism already used for other noninfectious uveitis/scleritis.
treatment.caution: Fingolimod (an MS disease-modifying therapy) can RARELY CAUSE macular edema as a side effect - important not to misattribute new macular edema in an MS patient purely to uveitis activity without considering the medication itself.
treatment.tnf_inhibitor_contraindication: TNF inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in demyelinating disease - brain MRI to rule out demyelinating disease should be obtained BEFORE starting a TNF inhibitor in ANY patient with 'idiopathic' intermediate uveitis, not just those with known MS, since occult/early demyelinating disease could be unmasked/worsened.

diseases.age_group_note: AAO explicit: MS usually affects White women 20-50 years of age (dewasa bucket).

epidemiology_aao.relative_risk: Uveitis is 10x MORE COMMON in MS patients than general population
epidemiology_aao.frequency_in_ms: Reported up to 30% of MS patients develop uveitis
epidemiology_aao.temporal_relationship: Uveitis onset MAY PRECEDE MS diagnosis by 5-10 years - i.e. uveitis can be the FIRST manifestation, long before neurologic diagnosis

diseases.critical_screening_note: IMPORTANT: patients with intermediate uveitis who fit the MS demographic (White woman 20-50yo) do NOT automatically warrant MRI/neurologic workup UNLESS they have actual neurologic signs/symptoms. However, an MRI SHOULD be considered before starting a TNF inhibitor regardless of symptoms, because TNF inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in demyelinating disease and can exacerbate MS - this is a treatment-safety screen, not a diagnostic screen.
associations: HLA-DR15 (associated with the MS+uveitis combination specifically)
diseases.pathogenesis_note: Proposed cross-reactivity between myelin-associated glycoprotein and Muller cells (retinal glial cells) - a proposed shared-antigen mechanism linking CNS demyelination and retinal periphlebitis.

diseases.name: Vitreoretinal Lymphoma (Masquerade Syndrome)
diseases.source: ENRICHED with full detail from AAO BCSC Ch.15 (Masquerade Syndromes) - previously a stub added from Ch.5 table reference only.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Listed by AAO under BOTH the intermediate uveitis differential (Table 5-10: 'Older than 50 years' branch) AND posterior uveitis 'Chorioretinitis without vitritis > Focal' branch (as 'Neoplastic masquerade' with 'history of carcinoma' for metastatic disease specifically) - i.e. vitreoretinal lymphoma can mimic BOTH intermediate and posterior/panuveitis presentations, which is exactly why it's a dangerous masquerade.
diseases.course: chronic, progressive if untreated, often relapsing/refractory to standard uveitis therapy
diseases.laterality: often bilateral (though can present asymmetric/unilateral initially)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO Ch.15 explicit: 'mean age at onset is between 50 and 70 years' - spans our dewasa (18-60) and lansia (61+) buckets. No convincing gender predilection. Immunosuppressed patients at increased risk.
key_features: RED FLAG: patient 50-70 years old presenting with 'uveitis' - especially INTERMEDIATE uveitis or focal posterior/panuveitis - that is atypical, poorly responsive to standard steroid therapy, or has vitreous cells out of proportion to other inflammatory signs
Most common presenting symptoms: decreased vision and floaters
Variable vitritis + AC cells; posterior segment shows cream-colored/yellow SUBRETINAL or SUB-RPE infiltrates with overlying RPE detachments
Can MIMIC acute retinal necrosis, toxoplasmosis, frosted-branch angiitis, or retinal arteriolar occlusion with multifocal chorioretinal scars/vasculitis - broad masquerade potential
98% are B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (subset of primary CNS lymphoma/PCNSL); ~2% T-cell

KEY CLINICAL TRAP: anti-inflammatory (steroid) treatment often TEMPORARILY improves vitreous cellular infiltration, which can falsely reassure clinicians and delay correct diagnosis - a case that responds partially/transiently to steroids should NOT be considered ruled out for VRL
>2/3 of VRL patients develop CNS disease, usually within 29 months of diagnosis (behavioral changes, hemiparesis, cerebellar signs, seizures, cranial nerve palsies)
diseases.critical_note: MASQUERADE SAFETY-NET ENTITY (unchanged from prior note, now with stronger evidence base): this should score/surface prominently whenever a case is age 50-70 (or broader 'older adult') AND presents as intermediate or atypical posterior/panuveitis, regardless of how well other specific infectious/autoimmune entities also score. ALL patients with suspected VRL should be urgently evaluated for CNS lymphoma (preferably MRI) even WITHOUT neurologic symptoms - CSF analysis shows lymphoma cells in only 1/3 of suspected-VRL patients, so a negative CSF does not rule it out.

investigations.name: Ultrasonography
investigations.note: May show vitreous debris, elevated subretinal lesions, exudative RD

investigations.name: Fluorescein angiography (FA)
investigations.note: Hypofluorescent areas from sub-RPE tumor blockage or RPE clumping; unusual 'leopard-spot' pattern of alternating hyper/hypofluorescence

investigations.name: Fundus autofluorescence (FAF)
investigations.note: Hyperautofluorescent AND hypoautofluorescent granular changes

investigations.name: OCT
investigations.note: Nodular elevations at RPE/sub-RPE level and/or VERTICAL hyperreflective lesions - fairly distinctive OCT sign

investigations.name: ICGA
