investigations.note: Ill-defined hypofluorescent lesions in late phase

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.note: URGENT, for ALL suspected VRL cases even without neuro symptoms - CNS lymphoma found in ~25% of intracranial lymphoma patients who develop intraocular disease, and conversely most VRL patients eventually develop CNS disease

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture / CSF analysis
investigations.note: Lymphoma cells found in only 1/3 of suspected-VRL cases - a negative result does NOT rule out VRL

investigations.name: Vitreous biopsy
investigations.note: Most common definitive diagnostic procedure. STOP systemic corticosteroids 2-4 weeks BEFORE biopsy to increase yield. Need >=1mL undiluted vitreous + cytospin prep. Up to 1/3 of biopsies are FALSE NEGATIVE - if strong clinical suspicion persists after negative biopsy, consider retinal biopsy and/or sub-RPE aspirate.

investigations.name: Flow cytometry / immunophenotyping
investigations.note: Demonstrates B-lymphocyte clonality via kappa/lambda light chain predominance, CD19/CD20/CD22 markers, or gene rearrangements

investigations.name: MYD88 L265P mutation PCR
investigations.note: Present in 62-88% of VRL cases - strong supportive evidence when positive, but absence does not rule out VRL

investigations.name: Vitreous cytokine analysis: IL-10:IL-6 ratio
investigations.note: IL-10 elevated in lymphoma vitreous; IL-6 elevated in inflammatory uveitis vitreous. An ELEVATED IL-10:IL-6 RATIO supports VRL diagnosis - useful adjunct when cytology is ambiguous

treatment.primary: Intravitreal chemotherapy (methotrexate and/or rituximab), local external beam radiation of the eye, and/or systemic chemotherapy depending on CNS involvement
treatment.concomitant_cns_disease: High-dose systemic chemotherapy + intrathecal therapy + whole-brain radiotherapy and/or autologous stem cell transplantation
treatment.common_regimen: High-dose systemic methotrexate with rituximab is among the most commonly used regimens
treatment.note: Some specialists use prophylactic CNS treatment even in patients with seemingly isolated ocular disease
treatment.not_treated_as: This is NOT managed as inflammatory uveitis - misdiagnosis and treatment with immunosuppression alone can allow progression to fatal CNS disease

diseases.prognosis: Responds well to INITIAL treatment but high relapse rate and CNS involvement risk lead to generally poor long-term prognosis. Median survival: 2-3 months with supportive care alone; 1-5 months with surgery alone; up to ~40 months with modern treatment. 5-year overall survival ~60%. Worse prognosis factors: advanced age, worse neurologic functional class, multiple CNS lesions, deep nuclei/periventricular (vs superficial) lesions.

diseases.name: Uveal (Choroidal) Lymphoma
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Primarily a low-grade choroidal lymphoma mimicking chronic posterior uveitis; acute anterior uveitis signs (pain/redness/photophobia) can also occur if angle structures are infiltrated.
diseases.etiology_group: masquerade (neoplastic - low-grade lymphoma of uveal tract)
key_features: Gradual, painless, unilateral or bilateral vision loss
Early-stage: multifocal cream-colored/yellow choroidal lesions that can MIMIC sarcoidosis-associated uveitis or birdshot chorioretinopathy - important differential trap
Macular edema may be present
Angle infiltration by lymphocytes can cause elevated IOP
Presentation may overlap with posterior scleritis and uveal effusion syndrome
Fleshy salmon-pink episcleral/conjunctival masses if present are FIRMLY ATTACHED (not mobile) - distinguishes from subconjunctival lymphoma which IS mobile
diseases.differential_note: Key masquerade traps: mimics sarcoidosis or birdshot chorioretinopathy on fundus exam. Histology (mature lymphocytes/plasma cells) is quite different from vitreoretinal_lymphoma's pleomorphic lymphoid cell appearance - the two lymphoma entities are histologically distinct despite similar names.

investigations.name: Skull-base to mid-thigh CT +/- PET
investigations.note: Standard workup once uveal lymphoma suspected/confirmed, to evaluate for systemic lymphoma

treatment.options: Corticosteroids, radiation, or both - variable results
treatment.note: Systemic/periocular corticosteroid therapy and external beam radiotherapy can both lead to rapid lesion regression

diseases.name: Ocular Manifestations of Systemic Lymphoma (hematogenous spread)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: RARE hematogenous spread of systemic lymphoma to choroid, subretinal space, vitreous, and/or anterior chamber
Can manifest with PSEUDOHYPOPYON, vitritis, cream-colored subretinal infiltrates, retinal vasculitis, necrotizing retinitis, or diffuse choroiditis/uveal masses

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - managed as systemic lymphoma with oncology co-management.

diseases.name: Ocular Manifestations of Leukemia
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: Retinal findings: intraretinal hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, WHITE-CENTERED hemorrhages, microaneurysms, peripheral neovascularization
Rare: leukemic cell invasion of vitreous cavity
Choroidal involvement can cause exudative retinal detachment with angiographic findings resembling VKH syndrome - important masquerade trap
Can present with hypopyon or hyphema, iris heterochromia, or gray-yellow PSEUDOHYPOPYON
diseases.differential_note: Exudative RD with VKH-like angiographic pattern is a key masquerade trap - consider leukemia (with CBC/blood smear) in atypical or treatment-resistant 'VKH-like' presentations, especially in a patient with any hematologic red flags.

treatment.note: Systemic leukemia treatment; ocular findings (e.g. exudative RD) can resolve with systemic treatment of the underlying leukemia per AAO Fig 15-6 case example.

diseases.name: Uveal Melanoma presenting as Ocular Inflammation
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: ~5% of uveal melanoma patients present with signs of ocular inflammation (episcleritis, anterior/posterior uveitis, or panuveitis) rather than the classic asymptomatic pigmented mass
Most inflammation-presenting tumors are epithelioid-cell or mixed-cell choroidal melanomas

investigations.name: Ultrasonography
investigations.note: Characteristically LOW internal reflectivity - useful for atypical/inflamed-appearing cases

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - see BCSC Section 4, Ophthalmic Pathology and Intraocular Tumors.

diseases.name: Diffuse Infiltrating Retinoblastoma (masquerade presentation)
diseases.source: NEW entity, formalizing what our reference.md already flagged as a critical differential for toxocariasis_ocular but never gave its own entry. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO explicit: 'Affected patients are usually between age 4 and 6 years at presentation' - falls in our anak bucket (0-9).
key_features: Only 1-3% of ALL retinoblastomas, but this specific DIFFUSE INFILTRATING subtype is the one that mimics inflammation

Diagnostically confusing: LIMITED fundus visibility + MINIMAL OR ABSENT calcification on radiography/ultrasound (unlike classic retinoblastoma which usually shows calcification) - this absence of calcification is itself a trap that can falsely reassure against retinoblastoma
Conjunctival chemosis, AC cells, PSEUDOHYPOPYON, iris nodules, vitritis
KEY DISTINGUISHING SIGN: the pseudohypopyon typically SHIFTS WITH HEAD POSITION CHANGES and is WHITE (vs the yellowish color of a true inflammatory hypopyon)
diseases.critical_note: MASQUERADE SAFETY-NET for children: any child aged ~4-6 years with atypical uveitis, especially with a white (not yellow) shifting pseudohypopyon and no view-limiting calcification on imaging, must have diffuse infiltrating retinoblastoma actively excluded before treating as inflammatory uveitis. Cross-reference our existing toxocariasis_ocular entity, which already flags retinoblastoma as a mandatory differential for pediatric granuloma/leukocoria presentations - this entity formalizes that as its own diagnosis, not just a footnote.

investigations.name: Careful fundus exam under anesthesia if needed
Ultrasound/CT for calcification (note: may be minimal/absent in this subtype)

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - urgent pediatric oncology/ocular oncology referral required; NOT managed as inflammatory uveitis.

diseases.name: Juvenile Xanthogranuloma (JXG) - ocular
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO explicit: 'Patients usually present before 1 year of age' - firmly in our anak bucket.
key_features: Characteristic reddish-yellow SKIN lesions (histiocytic process, mainly skin and eyes, rarely viscera)
Iris involvement can cause SPONTANEOUS HYPHEMA in an infant - a key red flag combination (infant + spontaneous hyphema + skin lesions)
Histology: large histiocytes with foamy cytoplasm and Touton giant cells (fewer in iris biopsy than skin biopsy)
diseases.key_pattern: Infant (<1yo) with spontaneous hyphema and/or reddish-yellow skin lesions

treatment.first_line: Topical, periocular, or systemic corticosteroid therapy for intraocular lesions
treatment.resistant_cases: Local resection, radiation, or immunomodulatory therapy

diseases.name: Metastatic Tumors presenting as Ocular Inflammation
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: Most common cause of intraocular malignancy in ADULTS overall is metastatic disease (lung and breast carcinoma most common primaries)
Anterior uveal metastasis: AC cells, iris nodules, iris neovascularization, elevated IOP

Retinal metastases EXTREMELY RARE - cutaneous melanoma most common primary for retinal mets (produces brown spherules), followed by lung/GI/breast (white-yellow, can simulate retinal vasculitis or necrotizing retinitis via perivascular sheathing)
Choroidal metastasis: vitritis, exudative RD, occasionally macular edema - often BILATERAL and MULTIFOCAL

investigations.name: Anterior chamber paracentesis
investigations.note: Can help confirm diagnosis for anterior uveal metastasis

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - see BCSC Section 4 for full oncologic management.

diseases.name: Bilateral Diffuse Uveal Melanocytic Proliferation (BDUMP)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Not true uveitis/panuveitis by SUN criteria (it's a melanocytic proliferation, not inflammation), but included here because it clinically MIMICS VKH-like panuveitis presentation and needs to be in the differential.
key_features: PARANEOPLASTIC: associated with an underlying systemic malignancy - the ocular finding is often the presenting clue to an occult cancer
Rapid vision loss, cataracts, multiple pigmented AND nonpigmented placoid iris/choroidal nodules, exudative retinal detachments
CAN MIMIC Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome - critical masquerade trap given VKH is already a prominent entity in our KB
diseases.differential_note: Cross-reference vkh_syndrome - BDUMP is the paraneoplastic masquerade for VKH-like bilateral panuveitis presentation with exudative RD. Given VKH itself already requires ruling out several things, BDUMP should specifically be considered when the patient has ANY known or suspected underlying malignancy, unexplained rapid bilateral cataracts, or when the presentation is otherwise atypical for classic VKH (e.g. no prodromal meningismus/auditory symptoms).

investigations.name: Search for underlying systemic malignancy
investigations.note: Treatment is directed at finding and treating the underlying malignancy - this IS the diagnostic priority, not local eye treatment

treatment.note: Treatment should be directed at finding and treating the UNDERLYING MALIGNANCY - this is a paraneoplastic sign, not a primary ocular disease to treat locally.

diseases.name: Retinitis Pigmentosa (nonneoplastic masquerade)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: Can have variable vitreous AND anterior chamber cells + macular edema - genuinely mimics uveitis, not just a lookalike on paper
Distinguishing features: nyctalopia, POSITIVE FAMILY HISTORY of RP, waxy optic disc pallor, arteriolar attenuation, bone-spicule pigmentary pattern in midperiphery
ERG severely depressed/extinguished even EARLY in disease
diseases.differential_note: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: late-stage birdshot_retinochoroidopathy can also show severely depressed/extinguished ERG, arteriolar attenuation, and disc pallor - making RP vs late birdshot genuinely difficult to distinguish in some cases. Family history and bone-spicule pattern are the more RP-specific clues; birdshot's HLA-A29 positivity and history of earlier active choroiditis lesions favor birdshot instead.

treatment.note: Not a treatable inflammatory condition - see BCSC Section 12, Retina and Vitreous.

diseases.name: Ocular Ischemic Syndrome (OIS) (nonneoplastic masquerade)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
diseases.etiology_group: masquerade (non-neoplastic - vascular/ischemic, usually carotid artery obstruction)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO explicit: 'Patients with OIS are typically men aged 65 years or older' - falls in our lansia bucket (61+); also male-predominant.
key_features: Results from hypoperfusion of the entire eye (sometimes orbit too), usually from carotid artery obstruction
Corneal edema, AC cells, moderate AC FLARE that is OUT OF PROPORTION to (greater than expected for) the cell count - a distinctive dissociation pattern
Anterior segment neovascularization may be present
Fundus: dilated retinal venules, NARROWED arterioles, medium-large intraretinal blot hemorrhages in mid/far periphery
FA: delayed arteriolar filling, diffuse posterior pole/disc leakage, capillary nonperfusion signs
diseases.key_pattern: Man >=65yo with flare-cell dissociation (flare out of proportion to cells) + anterior segment neovascularization

investigations.name: Carotid Doppler ultrasonography
investigations.note: Ipsilateral carotid stenosis >90% supports the diagnosis

investigations.name: Dilated fundus exam + FA
investigations.note: Key to distinguishing OIS from true uveitis

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - see BCSC Section 12, Retina and Vitreous, for OIS treatment (typically vascular/carotid management, not anti-inflammatory).

diseases.name: Chronic Peripheral Rhegmatogenous Retinal Detachment / Schwartz-Matsuo Syndrome
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: Chronic peripheral RRD can present with AC cell/flare AND vitreous inflammatory/pigment cells - genuinely mimics uveitis
Often GOOD vision unless macular edema develops (a clue: relatively preserved vision despite apparent 'chronic uveitis')

Schwartz-Matsuo syndrome: photoreceptor outer segments liberated from the subretinal space appear in the AC, mimicking inflammatory cells, and can obstruct trabecular outflow causing ELEVATED IOP and secondary open-angle glaucoma
diseases.critical_note: Careful DILATED FUNDUS EXAM WITH SCLERAL DEPRESSION is essential and diagnostic - peripheral pigment demarcation lines, subretinal fluid, retinal breaks, subretinal fibrosis, and peripheral retinal cysts are the findings that reveal the true diagnosis. A 'chronic anterior uveitis' that has NOT had a careful peripheral retinal exam should raise suspicion for this masquerade.

treatment.note: Retinal detachment repair, not anti-inflammatory therapy.

diseases.name: Retained Intraocular Foreign Body (masquerade presentation)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: Chronic intraocular inflammation from mechanical/chemical/toxic irritation of uveal tissue, particularly ciliary body
CRITICAL TRAP: the causative trauma is often UNRECOGNIZED or perceived as MILD by the patient - do not rely on a clear trauma history being volunteered
diseases.critical_note: High index of suspicion required for ANY chronic unexplained unilateral uveitis, even without a clearly reported trauma history. Delayed diagnosis risks proliferative vitreoretinopathy and endophthalmitis with poor visual prognosis; timely removal is often curative.

investigations.name: Careful trauma history (actively ask, don't wait for volunteering)

treatment.note: Removal of the foreign body is often curative if diagnosed and recognized quickly.

diseases.name: Pigment Dispersion Syndrome (nonneoplastic masquerade)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.15.
key_features: Pigment granules released into the AC from iris and/or ciliary body - can be confused with true inflammatory cells on slit-lamp exam
diseases.differential_note: Careful examination of corneal endothelium (Krukenberg spindle), iris (transillumination defects), and iridocorneal angle (dense trabecular pigmentation) distinguishes this from true anterior uveitis.

treatment.note: Not an inflammatory condition - see BCSC Section 10, Glaucoma, for full discussion.

diseases.name: Phacolytic Uveitis (Lens-Induced, mechanical)
diseases.source: NEW entity, split out from what was previously conflated into lens_induced_uveitis. AAO BCSC Ch.8.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (mechanical - lens protein leakage through intact capsule)
key_features: Occurs from leakage of lens protein through MICROSCOPIC openings in an INTACT lens capsule of a MATURE or HYPERMATURE cataract - NOT from capsule rupture (contrast with phacoantigenic uveitis)
Macrophages engorged with lens protein clog the trabecular meshwork causing ACUTE IOP RISE (phacolytic GLAUCOMA)
KEY DISTINGUISHING SIGN: KPs and synechiae are OFTEN ABSENT (unlike phacoantigenic uveitis where they are common) - this is the clearest bedside differentiator between the two lens-induced entities
Refractile bodies (lipid-laden macrophages) visible in the aqueous
diseases.differential_note: Cross-reference lens_induced_uveitis (phacoantigenic) - the two are distinguished primarily by: capsule status (intact in phacolytic vs disrupted in phacoantigenic), KP/synechiae presence (absent in phacolytic, common in phacoantigenic), and the presence of refractile bodies (characteristic of phacolytic).

investigations.name: Slit-lamp exam for refractile bodies, cataract maturity

treatment.immediate: IOP reduction, often via osmotic agents + topical medications
treatment.definitive: Followed QUICKLY by cataract extraction - this is curative, not just symptomatic

diseases.name: Pars Planitis
diseases.source: NEW entity - formalized as its own disease (previously only referenced generically as a subtype of intermediate uveitis without its own detailed entry, despite being 85-90% of ALL intermediate uveitis cases). AAO BCSC Ch.8.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (idiopathic intermediate uveitis subtype - diagnosis of exclusion)
diseases.course: variable: 10% self-limited/benign, 30% smoldering with remissions/exacerbations, 60% chronic without exacerbations
diseases.age_group_note: AAO explicit: BIMODAL age distribution, ages 5-40 years, concentrating in younger (5-15yo) and older (20-40yo) age groups - i.e. two distinct peaks, not a smooth single-peak distribution. No overall sex predilection.
key_features: MOST COMMON form of intermediate uveitis - constitutes 85-90% of all intermediate uveitis cases
Snowballs (vitreous cell aggregates) + inflammatory membranes on pars plana + snowbanks (inferior pars plana exudate) - snowbanks correlate with MORE SEVERE disease
Inferior peripheral retinal phlebitis with venous sheathing is common

Presentation differs by age: CHILDREN often present with eye redness/photophobia/discomfort AND considerable anterior chamber inflammation plus vitreous cells (more acute-looking); TEENAGERS/YOUNG ADULTS more often INSIDIOUS onset with floaters as the main complaint
Diagnosis of EXCLUSION - established by ruling out specific identifiable etiologies (sarcoidosis, MS, syphilis, Lyme, toxocariasis, vitreoretinal lymphoma)
diseases.critical_note: MASQUERADE SCREENING BUILT IN: new-onset vitreous cells in an OLDER patient (usually >=50-60yo, older than typical pars planitis demographic) should raise suspicion for vitreoretinal_lymphoma - cross-reference that entity. Distinguishing clue: VA may be BETTER than expected despite dense vitreous cell sheets, and macular edema is USUALLY ABSENT in lymphoma (vs pars planitis where macular edema is a hallmark complication in 50% of cases) - this VA/macular-edema dissociation pattern is a useful bedside discriminator.

complications.macular_edema: occurs in 50% of patients - the HALLMARK complication and major cause of vision loss; becomes chronic/refractory in ~10% of patients
complications.neovascularization: along inferior snowbank in up to 10% of cases -> can cause vitreous hemorrhage (MORE COMMON IN CHILDREN than adults) or contract causing peripheral tractional retinoschisis/RRD; rarely evolves into secondary peripheral retinal vasoproliferative tumors YEARS later
complications.retinal_detachment: localized peripheral (exudative or tractional) in 4-10% of patients; frank rhegmatogenous RD is RARE
complications.cataract: occurs in up to 60% of cases
complications.glaucoma: occurs in ~10% (both angle-closure and open-angle mechanisms)
complications.other: posterior synechiae, band keratopathy (with chronic inflammation), epiretinal membrane, vitreous opacification

diseases.prognosis: If macular edema resolves, long-term visual prognosis is good: ~75% of patients maintain VA of 20/40 or better after 10 years.

investigations.name: Sarcoidosis workup (ACE, CXR)
investigations.note: Sarcoidosis presents as intermediate uveitis in 7% of sarcoid-uveitis cases

investigations.name: MS evaluation / MRI brain if neuro symptoms present
investigations.note: Up to 15% of pars planitis patients eventually develop MS; up to 20% of MS patients have anterior+intermediate uveitis

investigations.name: Syphilis serology
investigations.note: Should be performed in ALL new-onset uveitis including intermediate

investigations.name: Lyme ELISA
investigations.note: Only if erythema chronicum migrans + new arthritis + tick bite history in endemic region - Lyme-associated uveitis is otherwise very rare

investigations.name: Toxocara antibody test
investigations.note: In children - complete absence of inflammation in contralateral eye + positive antibody supports toxocariasis over pars planitis

investigations.name: Macular OCT
investigations.note: May show macular thickening with/without frank edema, diffuse or focal over macular vessels, +/- epiretinal membrane

investigations.name: Fluorescein angiography
investigations.note: Should be obtained in patients with substantial clinical inflammation signs - FA often shows MORE inflammation than clinically apparent; relying on clinical exam + OCT alone may UNDERESTIMATE activity

investigations.name: Ultrasound biomicroscopy
investigations.note: Can demonstrate peripheral exudates/membranes on pars plana

treatment.observation: Very low-grade disease can sometimes be observed without treatment
treatment.first_line: Corticosteroids. Unilateral cases: local depot corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone acetonide or methylprednisolone acetate, posterior sub-Tenon or orbital floor) is appealing IF infectious causes sufficiently investigated first. Inflammation/macular edema often improve but may persist or recur within 2-3 months.
treatment.intravitreal_option: Intravitreal corticosteroids also used - POINT trial showed INTRAVITREAL more effective than PERIOCULAR for uveitic macular edema specifically (cross-reference treatment_algorithm_general_aao). Dexamethasone and fluocinolone acetonide intravitreal IMPLANTS are FDA-approved specifically for noninfectious intermediate uveitis.
treatment.severe_bilateral: Systemic corticosteroid: initial 1mg/kg/day (max 60-80mg/day), tapered every 1-2 weeks to <7.5mg/day after 8-10 weeks
treatment.imt: If steroid fails or high doses needed: antimetabolites, T-cell inhibitors, or biologics. SITE/FAST studies: methotrexate/azathioprine/mycophenolate/cyclosporine achieved sustained control in 70-80% of intermediate uveitis patients. Adalimumab is FDA-approved for noninfectious intermediate uveitis specifically.
treatment.tnf_inhibitor_caution: Because TNF inhibitors can EXACERBATE MS, obtain brain MRI to screen for demyelinating disease BEFORE starting a TNF inhibitor in pars planitis (cross-reference multiple_sclerosis_uveitis).
treatment.adjunct_ablative: Peripheral cryotherapy and/or laser photocoagulation to snowbanks/ischemic retina - can prevent/involute neovascularization and reduce vitreous hemorrhage risk WITHOUT increasing RRD risk. Anti-VEGF for retinal/choroidal neovascularization in otherwise-quiet eyes.
treatment.surgical: Pars plana vitrectomy (PPV) for non-resolving vitreous hemorrhage, tractional adhesions, RD, or epiretinal membrane; separating posterior hyaloid during PPV can help reduce macular edema. Consider PPV for substantial vitreous opacity despite adequate IMT. Increase perioperative immunosuppression/corticosteroids around PPV.

diseases.name: Drug-Induced Ocular Inflammation
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB at all. AAO BCSC Ch.8.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Varies by causative agent - most commonly anterior, but immune checkpoint inhibitors specifically can cause a full VKH-like panuveitis.
diseases.course: variable, often resolves with drug discontinuation
diseases.granulomatous: variable - brimonidine-associated uveitis is often GRANULOMATOUS
key_features: RARE overall, but important because DISCONTINUING the offending agent may be CURATIVE - a high-value diagnosis to catch

Causality can be graded using the Naranjo Algorithm (Adverse Drug Reaction Probability Scale) - 10 questions about reaction frequency, resolution on discontinuation, worsening with dose increase, recurrence on rechallenge -> classifies as definite/probable/possible/doubtful

strongly_associated: Rifabutin (antibiotic, used for M. avium complex)
moderately_associated: Systemic fluoroquinolones - ESPECIALLY moxifloxacin (may cause iris depigmentation + uveitis)
causative_agents_by_strength.paradoxical_tnf_inhibitors: Some TNF inhibitors (etanercept, adalimumab) can themselves cause NEW-ONSET uveitis, a psoriasis-LIKE rash, and a systemic SARCOID-LIKE syndrome - paradoxical since these same drugs also TREAT uveitis in other contexts
vaccines: Intravesical BCG (bladder cancer treatment) - can cause immune-mediated OR infectious uveitis
causative_agents_by_strength.immune_checkpoint_inhibitors: Cancer immunotherapy (e.g. nivolumab) can cause a SPECTRUM from mild anterior uveitis up to retinal vasculitis, choroiditis, and VKH-SYNDROME-LIKE PANUVEITIS - important masquerade-adjacent consideration in any cancer patient on immunotherapy presenting with new uveitis; can recur with subsequent treatment cycles
topical_ocular_hypotensives: Brimonidine (alpha-2 agonist) - classic presentation: red/irritated eye with extensive often-GRANULOMATOUS KPs + low-grade AAU in a patient on LONG-TERM brimonidine; can have corneal edema + vitreous inflammation too
Prostaglandin F2-alpha analogues (rare macular edema induction/worsening - can still be used in uveitic patients when other IOP-lowering options are insufficient, since this risk is rare)

diseases.differential_note: A patient on brimonidine for glaucoma who develops new granulomatous-appearing AAU should prompt consideration of stopping brimonidine BEFORE assuming a new systemic granulomatous disease (sarcoidosis/TB/syphilis) workup is needed - drug cause is common enough to check first in this specific med-exposure scenario.

treatment.anterior_only: Topical corticosteroids often sufficient once drug stopped
treatment.severe_or_posterior: Scleritis, higher-grade uveitis, or posterior segment involvement may need oral corticosteroids
treatment.definitive_stop: If vision-threatening or recurs on rechallenge, the causative medication should be permanently discontinued

diseases.name: Blau Syndrome (Familial Juvenile Systemic Granulomatosis)
diseases.source: Formalized as its own entity - previously only mentioned by name in JIA differential diagnosis list without detail. Combined from Kanski Ch.12 (brief mention) + AAO Ch.8 (named in JIA differential). UPDATED: NOD2 gene causation confirmed via AAO BCSC Ch.10 (previously flagged as needing verification).
diseases.age_group_note: Kanski: 'characterized by childhood onset' - anak bucket.
key_features: RARE autosomal dominant disorder
Triad: granulomatous disease of skin + eyes (panuveitis and multifocal choroiditis) + joints

Important differential for a child with apparent 'JIA-associated' panuveitis (not just anterior) - the multifocal choroiditis component distinguishes it from typical JIA-associated uveitis which is usually purely anterior
diseases.differential_note: Cross-reference jia_uveitis differential_diagnosis_aao list - Blau syndrome should specifically be considered when a child's presumed 'JIA-uveitis' includes POSTERIOR/choroidal involvement, since classic JIA-associated uveitis is anterior-only. AAO Ch.10 adds: pediatric sarcoidosis, JIA-associated uveitis, and Blau syndrome can overlap clinically in children under 5yo in terms of ocular and articular involvement - family history of granulomatous disease is the key clue favoring Blau syndrome specifically.

investigations.name: NOD2 gene testing
investigations.note: CONFIRMED via AAO BCSC Ch.10: Blau syndrome is caused by mutations in the NOD2 gene specifically - this resolves the earlier verification-needed flag on this entity.

treatment.note: Not detailed in either source excerpt - to be enriched from a future chapter/source if encountered.

diseases.name: Multifocal Choroiditis With Panuveitis (MFCPU)
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB by this name (Kanski's 'idiopathic multifocal choroiditis (MFC)' concept is related but this AAO entity is the fuller/more current formalization, including SUN 2021 classification criteria). AAO BCSC Ch.9.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: AAO: 'MFCPU is considered a posterior uveitis because the primary inflammation is in the choroid, and any associated anterior chamber or vitreous inflammation is usually MILD' - despite the name including 'panuveitis', it is classified/treated as primarily posterior.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (idiopathic, possible EBV/HSV trigger of autoimmune process)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: average age ~30 years (range 10-70), young women with MYOPIA typical profile.
key_features: Multiple oval/round lesions typically >125um at RPE/inner choroid level, ANY zone but MOST peripapillary
Acute lesions white-yellow/opaque; transition to 'punched-out' atrophic scars with pigmented border
Peripheral streaks/linear arrays of lesions, peripapillary pigmentary atrophy possible
Some patients show PIC pattern in one eye and MFCPU pattern in the fellow eye - same disease spectrum, asymmetric expression
diseases.differential_note: vs punctate_inner_choroidopathy: MFCPU lesions LARGER than PIC, PIC usually LACKS anterior/vitreous inflammation (MFCPU has mild AC/vitreous involvement). vs birdshot_retinochoroidopathy: MFCPU lesions SMALLER/more discrete/'punched out' than birdshot's larger cream-colored ovoid lesions; MFCPU patients typically YOUNGER than BCR patients and HLA-A29 NEGATIVE. vs poh_syndrome: both can cause peripheral chorioretinal streaks + peripapillary atrophy, but POHS typically does NOT cause anterior/vitreous inflammation or substantial subretinal fibrosis (unless CNV present) - MFCPU does have mild anterior/vitreous involvement.
diseases.complications: Macular edema, CNV, epiretinal membrane, macular subretinal fibrosis

investigations.name: TB, syphilis, sarcoidosis workup
investigations.note: Standard differential exclusion per general white-dot-syndrome workup principle

treatment.goal: Minimize progressive RPE atrophy and macular subretinal fibrosis that leads to vision loss - natural history of UNTREATED disease is poor, so an AGGRESSIVE approach is warranted from the start
treatment.evidence: Large retrospective cohort: IMT associated with 83% REDUCTION in posterior pole complication risk and 92% REDUCTION in risk of ending up 20/200 or worse - strong evidence for early aggressive IMT
treatment.first_line_short_term: Systemic/periocular/intravitreal corticosteroids often quiet active lesions short-term
treatment.long_term_control: Antimetabolites, T-cell inhibitors, and/or TNF inhibitors commonly required for long-term control; intravitreal fluocinolone implant may work for some
treatment.cnv_adjunct: Intravitreal anti-VEGF for inflammatory CNV, though controlling the underlying uveitis activity with corticosteroids/IMT may eliminate the need for ongoing anti-VEGF injections

diseases.name: Subretinal Fibrosis and Uveitis (SFU) Syndrome
diseases.source: NEW entity (Kanski's brief 'progressive subretinal fibrosis and uveitis syndrome (SFU)' concept now formalized with AAO detail). AAO BCSC Ch.9.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: described in otherwise healthy 20-40 year old women with myopia.
key_features: EXTREMELY RARE
HIGH-GRADE anterior chamber inflammation + mild-moderate vitritis (more anterior-segment-prominent than most other white dot syndromes)

Acute lesions resemble early MFCPU (white-yellow, 50-500um, posterior pole to midperiphery) but COALESCE into large areas of SUBRETINAL FIBROSIS - this progression to confluent fibrosis is the defining/naming feature
Histology: lymphocytic GRANULOMATOUS infiltration of choroid + marked gliosis + subretinal fibrosis
diseases.differential_note: Differential includes syphilitic_uveitis, tb_uveitis, sarcoidosis_uveitis, apmppe, relentless_placoid_chorioretinitis, serpiginous_choroidopathy, sympathetic_ophthalmia, toxoplasmosis_retinitis, poh_syndrome, and pathologic myopia.
diseases.prognosis: Guarded - but early aggressive IMT may slow/stop progression

treatment.biologic_option: Rituximab has shown efficacy in recent reports for this specific entity

diseases.name: Ampiginous Choroiditis (Relentless Placoid Chorioretinitis, RPC)
diseases.source: Formalized as its own entity (previously only a passing mention in our Kanski-derived notes). AAO BCSC Ch.9.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Primarily posterior/choroidal but with 'variable degrees of anterior segment inflammation and vitritis' unlike pure serpiginous/APMPPE.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (idiopathic, has features of both serpiginous choroiditis AND APMPPE)
diseases.course: chronic, relentlessly progressive (hence the name) - can show >50 multifocal lesions appearing/progressing over a 2-year period
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: typically 10-50 years of age.
key_features: Creamy-white placoid lesions clinically/angiographically SIMILAR to both serpiginous choroiditis AND APMPPE active lesions
KEY DISTINGUISHING FEATURE vs serpiginous: ampiginous lesions are often MULTIFOCAL and DISTINCTLY SEPARATE (vs serpiginous which arises from a SINGLE location and is clustered/contiguous)
Lesions occur in the PERIPHERY and may PREDATE posterior pole lesions (unlike serpiginous which classically starts peripapillary and extends)
Macular lesions do NOT radiate from the peripapillary area (unlike serpiginous)
Advanced disease can become clinically INDISTINGUISHABLE from serpiginous choroiditis, including CNV development - the distinction matters most EARLY in the disease course
diseases.differential_note: TB-associated serpiginous-like choroiditis must ALSO be ruled out in suspected ampiginous choroiditis - same TB-screening imperative as for serpiginous_choroidopathy.

treatment.note: Depending on severity/course: systemic and/or local corticosteroids, systemic IMT, and/or anti-VEGF therapy

diseases.name: Acute Retinal Pigment Epitheliitis (ARPE, Krill Disease)
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.9.
diseases.course: acute onset, spontaneous resolution over 6-12 weeks
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: aged 15-40 years, males and females affected equally.
key_features: ONE OF THE RAREST white dot syndromes
Clusters of small dark-gray/black spots (100-200um) with a YELLOW HALO/RIM in the deep retina, posterior pole
Halo disappears over time, pigment clumping may develop
Mild or NO vitritis
FA hallmark: central hypofluorescence with a lacy hyperfluorescent rim creating a 'HONEYCOMB' effect
Abnormal EOG with NORMAL ERG suggests the disease originates within the RPE specifically (useful electrophysiology localization clue)
diseases.prognosis: Good - self-limited, resolves spontaneously over 6-12 weeks

treatment.note: Usually OBSERVED without treatment given benign self-limited course

diseases.name: Acute Zonal Occult Outer Retinopathy (AZOOR)
diseases.source: NEW standalone entity - previously only mentioned as a differential-diagnosis item, not detailed. AAO BCSC Ch.9.
diseases.course: chronic, recurrent (31%), evolves over months to years
diseases.laterality: commonly unilateral at presentation, but fellow-eye involvement develops in MOST cases eventually
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: mean age at onset 37 years, range 15-65 (broad), 76% female.
key_features: DIAGNOSTIC TRAP: often presents with a CLINICALLY NORMAL-APPEARING FUNDUS despite significant visual field loss - diagnosis often delayed because early exam findings are minimal/absent
Prominent photopsias described characteristically as pulsations, windmills, bubbles, sprays, or sparkles - distinctive symptom vocabulary worth specifically asking about
Eventually: white annular ring (3-5mm) centered on optic disc; later islands of RPE granularity/depigmentation with possible vessel attenuation/sheathing
Systemic autoimmune disease present in 28% of patients
diseases.critical_note: Early in the disease, differential diagnosis includes serious neuro-ophthalmic mimics: optic neuritis, optic nerve compressive lesion, pituitary/intracranial tumor, and paraneoplastic/nonparaneoplastic autoimmune retinopathy - AZOOR should not be assumed without considering these more urgent alternatives first, especially when the fundus looks normal but the patient has real visual field loss.
diseases.differential_note: Also differentiate from mewds, DUSN, and inherited retinal degenerations.
diseases.prognosis: VF loss often STABILIZES in ~75% of patients over time. Long-term outcome data: 68% retain 20/40+ vision, but 27% end up 20/200 or worse. Unclear whether corticosteroid/IMT treatment actually alters disease course or visual outcome.

investigations.name: FAF
investigations.note: Hypoautofluorescence in RPE/choriocapillary atrophy zones; hyperautofluorescent border at active/progressing edge (lipofuscin-laden cells preceding RPE death)

investigations.name: OCT
investigations.note: Ellipsoid zone loss/irregularity in affected zones

investigations.name: ERG (full-field and 30-Hz flicker) + EOG
investigations.note: Dysfunction at photoreceptor-RPE complex AND inner retina; delayed 30-Hz flicker; reduced EOG light rise

investigations.name: Visual field testing
investigations.note: Deficits correspond to affected retinal zone: central/arcuate/ring scotoma, or hemianopic patterns

treatment.note: Unclear if treatment alters outcome. For patients with PROGRESSIVE RPE or visual field loss specifically, long-term IMT and/or extended-duration steroid implants may be considered.

diseases.name: Acute Idiopathic Maculopathy (AIM)
diseases.source: NEW entity, DISTINCT from acute_macular_neuroretinopathy (AMN) despite similar-sounding names - these are two genuinely different entities. AAO BCSC Ch.9.
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: typically aged 20-40 years, M=F.
key_features: NOT the same entity as acute macular neuroretinopathy (AMN) despite the similar name - do not confuse the two
Acute CENTRAL vision loss (down to 20/200) with macular SUBRETINAL FLUID
Viral prodrome common; SPECIFIC association with hand-foot-and-mouth disease / coxsackievirus - either the patient themselves had HFMD, or had EXPOSURE to a child with HFMD (adult AIM cases)
Turbid YELLOW subretinal fluid in macula; underlying RPE yellow-white-gray and thickened
Minimal intraretinal hemorrhages possible; AC/vitreous cells minimal or absent
diseases.differential_note: Differential includes central serous chorioretinopathy, choroidal infarction, CNV, vkh_syndrome, sympathetic_ophthalmia, serpiginous_choroidopathy, apmppe, posterior scleritis, and syphilitic_uveitis.
diseases.prognosis: Variable, depends on degree of RPE damage. Subretinal fluid typically resolves spontaneously and FAIRLY RAPIDLY. Residual RPE hypopigmentation/granularity or a 'bull's-eye' RPE pattern may remain. Poor outcome if subfoveal RPE degeneration or CNV develops.

treatment.escalation: Oral corticosteroids may be considered for significant vision loss or slow lesion resolution

diseases.name: Autoimmune Retinopathy (AIR) - paraneoplastic and nonparaneoplastic
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB, despite being explicitly highlighted by AAO as diagnostically challenging and important. AAO BCSC Ch.9.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Technically a retinal DEGENERATION, not classic 'uveitis' - included because it's a key differential/masquerade-adjacent entity for posterior visual field loss with photoreceptor dysfunction, and AAO devotes significant attention to it in the uveitis textbook specifically because of diagnostic overlap.
diseases.etiology_group: presumed immune-mediated retinal degeneration; subdivided into paraneoplastic (pAIR: includes CAR = cancer-associated retinopathy, MAR = melanoma-associated retinopathy) and nonparaneoplastic (npAIR)
diseases.age_group_note: npAIR: more common in females (ratio ~2:3... note AAO text says 'more common in females than males (ratio 2:3)' which is an unusual way to state it - interpreted as female:male effectively favoring female predominance), typically aged 40-60 years.
key_features: Progressive BILATERAL vision loss, photopsias, scotomas, dyschromatopsia, PHOTOAVERSION, and nyctalopia
Ophthalmic exam may be NORMAL initially - vessel attenuation, RPE disruption, optic nerve pallor, diffuse retinal atrophy develop LATER
AC and vitreous inflammation ABSENT or very minimal - this near-total absence of visible inflammation despite significant visual dysfunction is itself a key clue pointing away from typical uveitis
~50% of npAIR patients have a systemic autoimmune disease
diseases.electrophysiology_distinguishing_pattern: CAR: cone responses typically DEPRESSED. MAR: photoreceptor response NORMAL, followed by ATTENUATED B-WAVE (representing on-bipolar cell dysfunction, not photoreceptor dysfunction) - this ERG waveform pattern difference helps distinguish CAR from MAR when paraneoplastic AIR is suspected.
diseases.critical_note: DIAGNOSIS IS CHALLENGING AND CONTROVERSIAL: serum anti-retinal antibodies (ARAs), the traditional biomarker, have POOR correlation with disease phenotype - ARAs are found in healthy controls, other uveitic entities (birdshot_retinochoroidopathy, azoor), AMD, and diabetic retinopathy; conversely some clinically/ERG-confirmed AIR cases test ARA-negative. Testing itself is NOT STANDARDIZED between labs (variable results). CONCLUSION: a positive or negative serum ARA result should NOT be used alone to confirm or exclude AIR - this is fundamentally a DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION.
diagnostic_criteria_aao: Positive serum ARA test result (supportive, not required/sufficient alone)
Abnormal ERG with or without visual field loss
ABSENCE of overt (>1+ grade) intraocular inflammation
ABSENCE of fundus findings or alternative uveitic diagnosis explaining the VF/ERG loss
ABSENCE of inherited retinal degeneration (retinitis pigmentosa, cone dystrophy) - cross-reference retinitis_pigmentosa_masquerade, which is itself a key differential
ABSENCE of malignancy (specifically to diagnose npAIR - if malignancy present, it's pAIR instead)

paraneoplastic_specific_testing.anti_recoverin_antibodies: Strongly associated with CAR (cancer-associated retinopathy). Most common CAR-associated malignancy: SMALL CELL LUNG CANCER.
paraneoplastic_specific_testing.anti_trpm1_antibodies: Autoantibodies to transient receptor potential cation channel (TRPM1, expressed in both melanocytes AND retinal on-bipolar cells) strongly support MAR diagnosis in compatible clinical context.
paraneoplastic_specific_testing.caveat: ABSENCE of these specific autoantibodies does NOT rule out pAIR. When clinical suspicion for pAIR is strong, age/sex/risk-factor-appropriate CANCER SCREENING may be the most expeditious diagnostic path (rather than chasing more antibody tests).

treatment.general_caveat: Standard uveitis treatment strategies may NOT be as effective for AIR as for other inflammatory entities - manage expectations accordingly.
treatment.npair: Corticosteroids (local/systemic), conventional IMT, or biologic agents (e.g. rituximab)
treatment.pair: Treatment of the UNDERLYING MALIGNANCY is the FIRST STEP; additional measures: systemic/local corticosteroids, IVIG, and/or plasmapheresis
treatment.prognosis_caveat: Reversal of retinal damage is UNLIKELY with any treatment approach, though stabilization may be possible - set realistic expectations with the patient.

diseases.name: Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.10.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Per Table 5-4 (already in KB), PAN causes retinal vasculitis with BOTH arteritis and phlebitis.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (systemic necrotizing vasculitis of medium/small arteries)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: affects ages 40-60, occurs 1.5x more frequently in MEN.
key_features: RARE (annual incidence 0.7/100,000)
Characteristically SPARES THE LUNGS (unlike GPA which is respiratory-predominant) - useful distinguishing feature
Skin: subcutaneous nodules, purpura, livedo reticularis, Raynaud phenomenon
Mononeuropathy multiplex (motor+sensory deficits) in up to 70% of patients
Ocular involvement in up to 20% of PAN patients
Retinal vasculitis can cause amaurosis fugax or branch/central retinal artery occlusion
Choroidal/posterior ciliary artery vasculitis: lobular choroidal ischemia/infarcts -> initially exudative RD, later ELSCHNIG SPOTS (focal choroidal hyperpigmentation with hypopigmented margins)
Peripheral ulcerative keratitis (often with scleritis) may be an EARLY manifestation
diseases.differential_note: Unlike GPA, PAN is NOT associated with ANCA - important negative discriminator. Should be considered in the differential of ANCA-NEGATIVE retinal vasculitis specifically, because correct diagnosis/management can be LIFESAVING.

diagnostic_criteria_official.source: Clinical diagnosis based on presence of >=3 of 10 criteria (no definitive lab test exists)
criteria: Diffuse myalgias (excluding shoulder/hip girdle) or leg muscle weakness/tenderness
Elevated BUN >40mg/dL or creatinine >1.5mg/dL (not from dehydration/obstruction)
Arteriogram showing aneurysms/occlusions of visceral arteries (not from arteriosclerosis/fibromuscular dysplasia)
Biopsy of small/medium artery showing granulocytes in the artery wall

investigations.name: Rule out other vasculitides (no PAN-specific test exists)
Tissue biopsy (skin or kidney often)
investigations.note: May be confirmatory but diagnosis is often clinical

investigations.name: ANCA (expected NEGATIVE in PAN)
investigations.note: Distinguishes from GPA

investigations.name: Hepatitis B/C serology
investigations.note: May play pathogenic role in some cases

diseases.prognosis: 5-year survival WITHOUT treatment: only 13%. WITH treatment: improves to 80% - dramatic treatment-dependent mortality difference. Mortality typically from renal failure or mesenteric/cerebral/cardiac infarction.

treatment.urgency: Appropriate diagnosis and management can be LIFESAVING - this is a systemic emergency, not just an eye disease

diseases.name: Susac Syndrome (SICRET Syndrome)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.10.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Per Table 5-4 (already in KB), Susac syndrome causes PRIMARILY ARTERITIS retinal vasculitis pattern.
diseases.laterality: unknown (retinal component can be unilateral or bilateral over time)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: typical onset 20-40 years (range 7-70), women 3x more likely than men.
key_features: Classic TRIAD: (1) encephalopathy, (2) low-to-mid-frequency SENSORINEURAL HEARING LOSS and/or tinnitus, (3) BRANCH RETINAL ARTERY OCCLUSIONS - also called SICRET syndrome (Small Infarctions of Cochlear, Retinal, and Encephalic Tissue)
MOST patients do NOT show the complete triad at ONSET - diagnosis is challenging early, watch for evolving triad over time
Fundus: yellow arteriolar wall deposits ('Gass plaques') - DIFFER from cholesterol/Hollenhorst plaques by occurring AWAY from arteriolar bifurcations and may be TRANSIENT
Retinal arteries diffusely/locally narrowed with 'BOXCAR' segmentation of blood column at peripheral retinal arteries
Vitreous haze/cells MINIMAL OR ABSENT - important, since this is a vaso-occlusive process, not primarily inflammatory/cellular
diseases.differential_note: Differential includes multiple_sclerosis_uveitis, herpetic encephalitis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis, sarcoidosis_uveitis, and behcet_uveitis (also in Behcet's own differential list per cross-reference).

investigations.name: Fluorescein angiography
investigations.note: Active disease: focal nonperfused retinal arterioles with ARTERIAL WALL HYPERFLUORESCENCE (no embolic material or perivascular inflammatory reaction seen - distinguishes from a true vasculitis/embolic process)

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.note: Multifocal supratentorial white matter lesions involving the CENTRAL CORPUS CALLOSUM are highly suggestive of Susac syndrome - a fairly specific neuroimaging pattern

investigations.name: Formal audiology testing
investigations.note: Assess for sensorineural hearing loss when Susac is being considered

treatment.regimen: Combination of high-dose corticosteroids, IV immunoglobulin, cyclophosphamide, antimetabolite therapy, and rituximab
treatment.monitoring: Serial fluorescein angiography helps monitor for ophthalmic disease recurrence

diseases.name: Leptospirosis (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.11. HIGH RELEVANCE for Indonesia (tropical/subtropical endemic disease).
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (bacterial - gram-negative spirochete Leptospira interrogans, zoonotic)
diseases.course: biphasic systemic illness; ocular involvement often has a PROLONGED INTERVAL after systemic disease
key_features: Occurs MOST FREQUENTLY in tropical/subtropical regions - directly relevant given Indonesia's climate and endemic risk

Transmitted via exposure to contaminated soil/water contaminated with urine from livestock/rodents/dogs - risk groups: agricultural workers, sewer workers, veterinarians, fishery/slaughterhouse workers, swimmers, whitewater rafters

Biphasic disease: (1) leptospiremic phase - abrupt fever/chills/headache/myalgia/vomiting/diarrhea after 2-4 week incubation; ~10% progress to severe Weil disease (renal+hepatocellular dysfunction, 30% FATAL); (2) immune phase - meningitis, leptospiruria, cranial nerve palsies, myelitis, AND UVEITIS
Earliest/most common ocular sign: circumcorneal conjunctival hyperemia
Intraocular inflammation in 10-44% of infected patients: mild anterior uveitis, neuroretinitis, or PANUVEITIS with retinal vasculitis (more serious/vision-threatening)
Organism can PERSIST in immunologically privileged sites (brain, eye) for prolonged periods - explains delayed ocular presentation relative to systemic illness
diseases.differential_note: Differential includes aau_idiopathic_hlab27, pars_planitis, behcet_uveitis, eales_disease, sarcoidosis_uveitis, tb_uveitis, syphilitic_uveitis. Leptospirosis can cause a FALSE-POSITIVE RPR or FTA-ABS syphilis test - important interaction to know when working up a case that could be either.

investigations.name: Serologic assays (ELISA, complement-fixation for IgM)
investigations.note: Highly sensitive/specific; PCR-based assays under evaluation

investigations.name: Organism isolation from body fluids
investigations.note: Definitive diagnosis but not always practical

treatment.mild_moderate: Penicillin G 1.5 million units IV q6h, OR oral doxycycline 100mg BID x1 week
treatment.note: Unclear if antibiotics prevent long-term uveitis specifically, but should still be considered for ocular disease presenting even MONTHS after acute systemic illness
treatment.adjunct: Topical/periocular/systemic corticosteroids + mydriatic/cycloplegic for intraocular inflammation and complications
treatment.prognosis: Visual prognosis is QUITE FAVORABLE despite even severe panuveitis - reassuring for patient counseling

diseases.name: Nocardiosis (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.11.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (Nocardia asteroides - gram-positive rod with acid-fast beaded branching filaments, 'acts like a fungus')
key_features: Found in soil; infection via ingestion or inhalation causing insidious inflammation
IMMUNOCOMPROMISED individuals more susceptible; potentially LETHAL systemic disease (pneumonia + disseminated abscesses)
Ocular involvement RARE but may be the PRESENTING problem
Spectrum: mild anterior uveitis pain/redness up to severe panophthalmitis
Characteristic: isolated unilateral CHOROIDAL OR SUBRETINAL MASS/ABSCESS with MINIMAL vitritis
Panuveitis with multiple choroidal abscesses + overlying RD can MIMIC FUNGAL ENDOPHTHALMITIS - important differential trap

investigations.name: Culture from tissue/fluid, vitreous aspiration (Gram stain + culture)
Pan-bacterial 16S rRNA PCR or Nocardia-specific PCR

treatment.escalation: Combination antibiotic therapy may be necessary

diseases.name: Cat-Scratch Disease (Bartonellosis) - ocular
diseases.source: NEW entity, formalizing what was previously only referenced as a quick_pattern_matcher differential without a dedicated entity. AAO BCSC Ch.11.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Wide spectrum - can present anywhere from anterior uveitis to neuroretinitis to intermediate uveitis to panuveitis.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (Bartonella henselae, small fastidious gram-negative rod; also B. quintana)
diseases.laterality: usually unilateral (bilateral neuroretinitis reported, frequently asymmetric)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: highest age-specific incidence in children YOUNGER THAN 10 years.
key_features: Transmitted by scratches/licks/bites of domestic cats (especially kittens); cat flea is the vector among cats themselves
Seasonal (fall/winter), most prevalent in southern US states/California/Hawaii (geographic pattern, though relevant principle for any warm-climate region)
Systemic: papule/vesicle/pustule at inoculation site (3-10 days post-inoculation) -> regional lymphadenopathy + mild-moderate flu-like illness 1-2 weeks later
Ocular involvement in 5-10% of CSD patients
Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome (unilateral granulomatous conjunctivitis + regional lymphadenopathy) in ~5%

BEST-KNOWN posterior finding: NEURORETINITIS (1-2% of CSD patients) - abrupt vision loss, unilateral disc edema, MACULAR STAR, +/- focal/multifocal retinitis. VA ranges 20/25 to 20/200 or worse. Disc edema + peripapillary serous RD can PRECEDE the macular star by 2-4 weeks. Macular star resolves over ~8-12 weeks, may be partial/incomplete.
Most common finding overall: small focal retinitis (even more common than neuroretinitis)
Immunosuppressed patients: can show retinal VASOPROLIFERATIVE response with angiomatoid lesions
diseases.differential_note: Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome differential: tularemia, tb_uveitis, syphilitic_uveitis, sporotrichosis, acute Chlamydia trachomatis. Neuroretinitis differential is BROAD (see neuroretinitis_differential_reference if added) - includes many infectious and noninfectious causes, not specific to Bartonella alone.

investigations.name: Serum anti-B. henselae antibodies (IFA, EIA, or Western blot)
investigations.note: Single positive IgG or IgM titer sufficient to confirm diagnosis

investigations.name: Skin testing
investigations.note: Sensitivity up to 100%, specificity up to 98%

investigations.name: PCR (16S rRNA or B. henselae DNA)
Bacterial culture
investigations.note: May take several WEEKS for colonies to appear - impractical for acute diagnosis

treatment.note: No definitive treatment guidelines exist - often SELF-LIMITING with excellent overall systemic prognosis
treatment.typical_regimen: Immunocompetent patients >8yo: doxycycline 100mg PO BID x4-6 weeks. Severe cases: IV doxycycline or combined with rifampin 300mg PO BID; extend to 4 months in immunocompromised. Children: azithromycin.
treatment.steroid_use: Effectiveness of oral corticosteroids UNKNOWN/unproven, but frequently used when optic nerve/macula is threatened

diseases.name: Whipple Disease (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity - not previously in our KB. AAO BCSC Ch.11.
diseases.course: chronic multisystem, can be fatal if untreated
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: most common in middle-aged WHITE MEN.
key_features: RARE multisystem disease
Migratory arthritis 80%; GI symptoms (diarrhea, steatorrhea, malabsorption) 75%; intestinal protein loss -> pitting edema + weight loss; cardiomyopathy/valvular disease possible
CNS involvement 10%: seizures, dementia, coma; neuro-ophthalmic signs (cranial nerve palsies, nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia); some develop a progressive-supranuclear-palsy-LIKE condition
Intraocular involvement RARE (<5% of cases)
Bilateral PANUVEITIS + retinal vasculitis + multifocal chorioretinitis when it occurs; anterior uveitis + moderate vitritis present
Diffuse chorioretinal inflammation/vasculitis in perifoveal and midperipheral regions; retinal vascular occlusions/hemorrhages possible
Unusual finding: granular/crystalline deposits on iris, capsular bag, and IOL
diseases.differential_note: Differential for the retinal-vasculitis-with-multisystem-involvement pattern includes sarcoidosis_uveitis, systemic_lupus_erythematosus_uveitis, polyarteritis_nodosa, behcet_uveitis. vitreoretinal_lymphoma should ALSO be considered in OLDER adults with substantial vitreal infiltration.

investigations.name: Duodenal biopsy (PAS-positive bacillus in macrophages within intestinal villi)
investigations.note: GOLD STANDARD diagnostic test

investigations.name: PCR of peripheral blood and vitreous for T. whipplei DNA
Culture
investigations.note: Difficult but possible

treatment.duration: 1-3 months typical, but RELAPSES occur in 30% of cases requiring PROLONGED treatment up to 1 YEAR
treatment.prognosis: Retinal vasculitis CAN resolve with treatment, but neurologic deficits become PERMANENT. UNTREATED Whipple disease can be FATAL - this is a systemic emergency, not just an eye finding.

diseases.name: Eales Disease
diseases.source: NEW entity, formalized from a passing cross-reference (previously mentioned only inline within tb_uveitis notes). AAO BCSC Ch.11.
diseases.etiology_group: idiopathic/possibly infectious (peripheral retinal vasculitis, POSSIBLY associated with tuberculin hypersensitivity)
diseases.laterality: unilateral initially, fellow eye involvement subsequently
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: otherwise-healthy young men aged 20-40 years.
key_features: Peripheral retinal vasculitis presenting with RECURRENT, UNILATERAL retinal and vitreous hemorrhage, with SUBSEQUENT involvement of the fellow eye

Some studies using PCR have detected M. tuberculosis DNA in aqueous/vitreous/epiretinal membranes of Eales disease patients - suggests a possible TB-hypersensitivity link, though the disease is conceptually distinct from tb_uveitis proper
Periphlebitis commonly seen, may progress to venous occlusion, peripheral nonperfusion, neovascularization, and eventual tractional RD in some cases
diseases.differential_note: Should be DIFFERENTIATED from direct TB retinal involvement (tb_uveitis) even though there may be a pathogenic link via tuberculin hypersensitivity - Eales disease is a relatively distinct clinical entity defined by the young-healthy-male-recurrent-vitreous-hemorrhage pattern.

investigations.name: Fundus exam + FA for periphlebitis, nonperfusion, neovascularization

treatment.note: Not detailed in this AAO Ch.11 excerpt (cross-referenced to BCSC Section 12, Retina and Vitreous, for full management) - general principle involves treating neovascularization/vitreous hemorrhage complications, and anti-TB therapy may be considered given the hypothesized association.

diseases.name: Dengue Fever (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity - HIGH PRIORITY relevance for Indonesia (endemic Aedes aegypti transmission region). AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.course: acute systemic illness, ocular findings often delayed ~1 month after systemic onset
key_features: MOST COMMON mosquito-borne viral disease in humans; transmitted by Aedes aegypti; endemic in >100 tropical/subtropical countries - directly relevant to Indonesia

Systemic: fever, headache, myalgia, purpuric rash, bleeding manifestations (thrombocytopenia). Initial infection often LOW-GRADE and patients may NOT mention it unless SPECIFICALLY ASKED during history/ROS - active questioning needed
MOST COMMON ocular finding: petechial SUBCONJUNCTIVAL hemorrhage. Variable AC/vitreous cells may occur too

~1 MONTH after systemic disease onset, ~10% of patients develop maculopathy/'foveolitis' - sudden vision decrease + central scotoma, OFTEN WITHOUT visible clinical lesions on exam (imaging-detected findings only)

Dengue maculopathy imaging: FA shows early focal arteriolar 'knobby' hyperfluorescence in macula with late leakage/staining; OCT-angiography shows FOVEAL AVASCULAR ZONE disruption; OCT may show macular edema, subretinal fluid, or IS/OS junction disruption
diseases.critical_note: In NON-ENDEMIC regions (like the US, per AAO), dengue maculopathy should be considered in patients with suggestive findings AND a history of RECENT TRAVEL to an endemic area. In Indonesia (endemic), this should be a standing differential for unexplained sudden central vision loss/scotoma with recent febrile illness, even WITHOUT travel history, given local endemicity - actively ask about recent fever/dengue exposure.

investigations.name: OCT / OCT-angiography / FA for maculopathy detection
investigations.note: Foveolitis findings may be present on imaging even when exam looks unremarkable

treatment.note: NO well-defined treatment algorithm exists for posterior segment dengue involvement - local and systemic corticosteroids may be used empirically.

diseases.name: Chikungunya Fever (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity - HIGH PRIORITY relevance for Indonesia. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.course: typically benign course for ocular findings
diseases.granulomatous: variable (anterior uveitis can be granulomatous or nongranulomatous)
key_features: 'Chikungunya' translates to 'that which bends up' - refers to the polyarthropathy/tenosynovitis/stooped posture in some patients - a useful mnemonic/history clue

Systemic: fever, headache, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, myalgia, ARTHRALGIA (prominent), rash. Mosquito-transmitted; maternal-fetal transmission documented. Recent outbreaks: Africa, Asia, Europe, Americas.
Ocular (from 2006 India epidemic data): anterior uveitis AND retinitis, less commonly nodular episcleritis - each typically BENIGN course
Anterior uveitis (granulomatous OR nongranulomatous) is ASSOCIATED WITH OCULAR HYPERTENSION

Retinitis can resemble HERPETIC retinitis, BUT KEY DISTINGUISHING FEATURES: chikungunya retinitis = focal/multifocal/confluent retinochoroiditis in the POSTERIOR POLE with hemorrhage and MINIMAL vitritis; herpetic retinitis = PERIPHERAL retina involvement with HIGHER-grade vitritis - posterior-vs-peripheral location and vitritis grade are the key differentiators
diseases.differential_note: Chikungunya retinitis mimics herpetic retinitis clinically - use location (posterior vs peripheral) and vitritis severity (minimal vs high-grade) to distinguish, plus serologic/PCR confirmation.

treatment.note: Retinitis has been treated with systemic ACYCLOVIR + corticosteroids empirically, but NO EVIDENCE this improves visual outcome - acyclovir use here is notable given chikungunya is NOT a herpesvirus (may reflect empiric treatment while herpetic mimics are being excluded, or historical practice pattern) - treatment efficacy is unproven either way.

diseases.name: Zika Virus (ocular) - acquired and congenital
diseases.source: NEW entity (previously only referenced within our reference.md notes, never a full KB entity). AAO BCSC Ch.12. Relevant given regional arbovirus overlap with Dengue/Chikungunya in Indonesia.
diseases.course: acute in adults; permanent structural findings in congenital form
key_features: Mosquito-transmitted (often Aedes aegypti - SAME vector as Dengue); COINFECTION with dengue/chikungunya possible in endemic areas - consider all three together in an endemic-region febrile illness with ocular findings
Most infected patients: NO symptoms or minimal symptoms (fever, headache, rash, arthralgia, myalgia, conjunctivitis) resolving within a week

Acute adult ocular findings (individual case reports): conjunctivitis, anterior uveitis, posterior uveitis with numerous chorioretinal lesions, unilateral acute maculopathy - NO defined treatment, topical/systemic/local corticosteroids may be considered
CONGENITAL Zika syndrome (CDC 5 features): microcephaly, structural brain abnormalities, OCULAR findings, congenital contractures (e.g. clubfoot), hypertonia restricting movement soon after birth

Congenital ocular findings: microphthalmia, cataract, glaucoma, iris coloboma, retinal pigment mottling, chorioretinal atrophy, optic nerve hypoplasia/atrophy - IMPORTANTLY, NO REPORTS OF UVEITIS specifically in congenital Zika (structural/developmental findings only, not inflammatory)
diseases.differential_note: Congenital Zika presents with STRUCTURAL ocular abnormalities, not uveitis/inflammation - important to distinguish from congenital infections that DO cause true uveitis (toxoplasmosis, syphilis, rubella, CMV) despite superficial similarity in the 'congenital infection with ocular findings' category.

treatment.note: No defined treatment for acute adult ocular ZIKV disease - topical/systemic/local corticosteroids may be considered. No vaccine exists. Congenital findings are structural/permanent, not treatable inflammation.

diseases.name: West Nile Virus (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (viral - single-stranded RNA, family Flaviviridae, Japanese encephalitis virus serocomplex)
key_features: Endemic to Europe, Australia, Asia, Africa; transmitted bird-to-human via mosquito; peak late summer (July-December range); incubation 3-14 days

80% of infections SUBCLINICAL; 20% present as febrile illness +/- myalgia/arthralgia/headache/conjunctivitis/lymphadenopathy/rash. SEVERE neurologic disease (meningitis/encephalitis) especially with diabetes and advanced age
Ocular: pain, photophobia, conjunctival hyperemia, blurred vision. CHARACTERISTIC multifocal chorioretinitis in MOST affected patients + nongranulomatous anterior uveitis + vitreous cells
Chorioretinal lesions (200-1000um) often in LINEAR ARRAYS following retinal nerve fiber course - classic/distinctive appearance
Congenital WNV reported (chorioretinal scarring WITHOUT intraocular inflammation, analogous conceptually to congenital Zika's structural-not-inflammatory pattern)
diseases.differential_note: Differential: syphilitic_uveitis, multifocal_choroiditis_panuveitis, poh_syndrome, sarcoidosis_uveitis, tb_uveitis.
diseases.prognosis: Usually SELF-LIMITING with VA return to baseline over months. Vision loss possible from CNV, foveal scar, ischemic maculopathy, vitreous hemorrhage, tractional RD, optic nerve pathology, retrogeniculate damage. Diabetes is a risk factor for BOTH WNV-related death AND WNV-associated ocular involvement.

investigations.name: WNV serology
investigations.note: Prompted by systemic symptoms suggestive of WNV or meningoencephalitis

treatment.note: NO vaccine or specific antiviral treatment exists - supportive therapy only. Anterior uveitis: topical corticosteroids. Efficacy of systemic/periocular corticosteroids for chorioretinal disease UNKNOWN. Public health mosquito control is the mainstay of prevention.

diseases.name: Rift Valley Fever (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (viral - Rift Valley fever virus, family Bunyaviridae)
key_features: 3 possible clinical syndromes: uncomplicated febrile/influenza-like illness, hemorrhagic fever, or encephalitis
Ophthalmic: anterior uveitis, vitritis, MACULAR OR PARAMACULAR RETINITIS (classic finding, often SPARING the fovea), retinal hemorrhage, retinal vasculitis, optic nerve edema
Associated with animal exposure (e.g. livestock/animal abortus contact) in endemic regions

treatment.note: Not detailed in this AAO excerpt.

diseases.name: HTLV-1 Uveitis (HU) and Associated Conditions
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
key_features: HTLV-1 infection can cause 3 related conditions: HTLV-1 uveitis (HU), adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATL), and HAM/TSP (HTLV-1-associated myelopathy/tropical spastic paraparesis)
Retinal vasculitis (60%), exudative retinal lesions (25%), optic disc abnormalities (20%), uveitic macular edema (3%)
ATL can cause secondary RETINAL INFILTRATES (a masquerade-adjacent presentation - true leukemic infiltration, not primary uveitis)
HAM/TSP patients may have retinal degeneration, optic neuropathy, keratoconjunctivitis sicca

HTLV-1-associated keratopathy (chronic interstitial keratitis) described in Brazilian/Caribbean patients but NOT found in Japanese patients - notable geographic/population variation, likely genetic or environmental cofactor differences
diseases.differential_note: For HU cases progressing DESPITE therapy, actively consider MIMICS: retinal infiltration from ATL, or retinal degeneration from HAM/TSP - i.e. 'treatment-resistant HU' may actually be a different HTLV-1-related process, not just severe/refractory HU itself.

treatment.response: HU responds to topical, periocular, or systemic corticosteroids
treatment.recurrence: ~50% of patients experience RECURRENT disease despite treatment response

diseases.name: Ebola Virus Disease (ocular) / Post-Ebola Virus Disease Syndrome
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.course: acute illness, with delayed/recurrent ocular sequelae in SURVIVORS
key_features: Transmitted via direct contact with infected blood/body fluids; SEXUAL transmission documented in survivor populations

Acute EVD: hemorrhagic fever + bruising/headache/weakness/fatigue/nausea/vomiting/diarrhea. Ocular: conjunctivitis, subconjunctival hemorrhage, acute vision loss of unclear etiology. Symptoms 2-21 days post-exposure.

'Post-Ebola virus disease syndrome' in SURVIVORS: arthralgias, myalgias, fatigue, weight loss, headache, neurocognitive deficits, psychosocial issues, AND EYE DISEASE - UVEITIS is one of the MOST COMMON ophthalmic manifestations in survivors

Can present WEEKS after systemic viremia resolves: documented case of unilateral acute hypertensive anterior uveitis 9 WEEKS after systemic resolution, with virus ISOLATED from aqueous humor - the eye can harbor persistent virus long after systemic clearance
RPE cells may be a potential VIRAL RESERVOIR
Recurrent uveitis occurs MORE FREQUENTLY in Ebola survivors vs controls (prospective cohort data)
diseases.critical_note: The eye (and RPE specifically) can serve as an immunologically privileged reservoir for persistent Ebola virus - recurrent/delayed uveitis in a survivor should prompt consideration of this specific etiology even long after systemic recovery.

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - manage as per general uveitis principles with awareness of potential ongoing viral persistence.

diseases.name: SARS-CoV-2/COVID-19 (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
key_features: SARS-CoV-2 detected in tears in 0-57% of COVID-19 patients across studies (wide range); implications for ocular-surface transmission not yet fully elucidated
Ophthalmic manifestations RELATIVELY RARE overall: mild conjunctivitis and subconjunctival hemorrhage MOST common; retinopathy and vascular occlusions reported less commonly

Proposed mechanism for retinal vascular disease: widespread systemic inflammation + coagulation derangements, AND/OR direct viral infection - dual/combined mechanism, similar conceptually to other virus-associated vasculopathies (cross-reference systemic_lupus_erythematosus_uveitis's hypercoagulable vasculopathy mechanism for a parallel pattern)

treatment.note: Not detailed in this excerpt - manage per specific manifestation (conjunctivitis, vascular occlusion, etc.) using standard approaches for those presentations.

diseases.name: Epstein-Barr Virus (ocular)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (viral - EBV, Herpesviridae family, B-lymphocyte tropism)
diseases.laterality: usually bilateral for granulomatous anterior uveitis
key_features: Congenital EBV: can cause congenital cataract. Acquired EBV (infectious mononucleosis): MORE likely to have ocular findings, most commonly mild self-limited FOLLICULAR CONJUNCTIVITIS
Less frequent: epithelial/stromal keratitis, episcleritis, BILATERAL granulomatous anterior uveitis, dacryoadenitis, rarely cranial nerve palsies/Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome

Posterior involvement RARE: disc edema, optic neuritis, macular edema, retinal hemorrhages, retinitis (including ARN-like), punctate outer retinitis, choroiditis, MFCPU, pars planitis/vitritis, progressive subretinal fibrosis, secondary CNV
diseases.critical_note: CAUSALITY CAVEAT: EBV seroprevalence is VERY HIGH (90%) in adults - serum antibody testing is RARELY useful for proving causality of ocular findings. Presence of EBV DNA/antibodies in OCULAR fluid has been cited as evidence of causal role, but an EBV-ASSOCIATED INTRAOCULAR MALIGNANCY (especially in HIV-positive patients) could MASQUERADE as ocular inflammation - don't over-attribute ocular findings to EBV without considering this masquerade possibility, especially in immunocompromised patients. In the ABSENCE of systemic EBV disease or recent mononucleosis, EBV is UNLIKELY to be the true cause of ocular findings.

treatment.necrotizing_retinitis_arn_like: Systemic antiviral (acyclovir or ganciclovir) may be considered

diseases.name: Nonnecrotizing Herpetic Retinopathy
diseases.source: NEW entity - a distinct herpetic posterior uveitis category not previously captured (our KB had ARN/PORN/CMV retinitis but not this 'umbrella' nonnecrotizing category). AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (viral - herpesvirus family, presumed but not always confirmed)
diseases.course: variable; initially resistant to conventional therapy
key_features: UMBRELLA designation for presumed herpetic retinal lesions that do NOT fit ARN, PORN, or CMV retinitis criteria
Examples: acute retinochoroiditis with diffuse hemorrhages after acute VZV infection in CHILDREN; chronic choroiditis or retinal vasculitis in ADULTS

In a PCR + local-antibody study of 'idiopathic posterior uveitis' patients, 13% had a CONFIRMED viral etiology - suggesting this category is meaningfully underdiagnosed within 'idiopathic' posterior uveitis populations
Can present as uveitic macular edema, a BIRDSHOT-LIKE chorioretinopathy (cross-reference birdshot_retinochoroidopathy - important masquerade/mimic), or occlusive BILATERAL retinitis
Occurs in BOTH immunocompetent and immunocompromised patients
diseases.critical_note: TREATMENT-RESPONSE AS DIAGNOSTIC CLUE: disease is initially RESISTANT to conventional corticosteroid/IMT therapy, but response is FAVORABLE when switched to systemic ANTIVIRAL medication - i.e. a 'birdshot-like' or 'idiopathic' posterior uveitis that fails to respond to standard corticosteroid/IMT approach should prompt consideration of PCR-based viral workup and an antiviral treatment trial, especially before escalating immunosuppression further.

investigations.name: PCR + local antibody analysis of aqueous fluid for herpesviruses
investigations.note: Recommended in atypical/idiopathic posterior uveitis given the 13% confirmed-viral-etiology yield in one study

treatment.note: Switch to SYSTEMIC ANTIVIRAL medication when conventional corticosteroid/IMT approach fails - this treatment-response pattern is itself diagnostically informative.

diseases.name: Ocular Cysticercosis
diseases.source: NEW entity - explicitly endemic to Southeast Asia per AAO, directly relevant to Indonesia. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (helminthic - Cysticercus cellulosae, larval stage of pork tapeworm Taenia solium)
diseases.course: chronic until larvae die or are removed; larval death provokes acute panuveitis
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: usually affects ages 10-30 years, NO sex predilection.
key_features: MOST COMMON ocular tapeworm infection
Endemic to Mexico, Africa, SOUTHEAST ASIA, eastern Europe, Central/South America, India - directly relevant given Indonesia's location
Transmission: fecal-oral OR consuming undercooked pork; larvae penetrate intestinal mucosa, spread hematogenously via posterior ciliary arteries to the eye
MOST FREQUENTLY involves the SUBRETINAL space; larvae may perforate retina into vitreous cavity
Can also present as subconjunctival or eyelid nodule
Classic sign: globular translucent cyst with an invaginated/evaginated head (SCOLEX) that UNDULATES in response to examining light - a distinctive dynamic exam finding
Larvae visible in vitreous/subretinal space in up to 46% of infected patients
Symptoms range from ASYMPTOMATIC with good vision to floaters/foreign-body sensation/pain/photophobia/redness/decreased vision
Exudative, rhegmatogenous, or tractional RD may occur
diseases.differential_note: Leukocoria-associated differential: retinoblastoma_masquerade, Coats disease, retinopathy of prematurity, persistent fetal vasculature, toxocariasis_ocular, retinal detachment. Also consider dusn.
diseases.critical_note: TREATMENT TIMING IS CRITICAL: larval DEATH provokes panuveitis, and LASER PHOTOCOAGULATION ALONE may ALSO provoke severe inflammation. Therefore EARLY REMOVAL of intraocular larvae (via vitreoretinal surgery with perioperative systemic corticosteroids) is the advocated approach - do NOT simply observe or attempt laser destruction without surgical planning, as either could precipitate a severe inflammatory reaction.

investigations.name: Clinical exam - direct visualization of undulating scolex
CT (for neural cysticercosis)
investigations.note: May reveal intracerebral calcification or hydrocephalus if neural involvement suspected

treatment.first_line: Early SURGICAL removal of intraocular larvae via vitreoretinal techniques, WITH perioperative systemic corticosteroids
treatment.extraocular_disease: Anthelminthic drugs + systemic corticosteroids for extraocular disease specifically
treatment.avoid: Do not rely on laser alone or simple observation given the larval-death-provokes-panuveitis risk

diseases.name: Diffuse Unilateral Subacute Neuroretinitis (DUSN)
diseases.source: NEW standalone entity - previously only referenced within toxocariasis/toxoplasmosis differential lists, never detailed. AAO BCSC Ch.12.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (helminthic - migrating nematode in subretinal space, 2 possible organisms)
diseases.course: insidious, progressive through stages if untreated
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: mean age 14 years, but WIDE range 11-65 years - genuinely spans all our buckets, cannot be narrowed further without guessing.
