key_features: Caused by a migrating nematode; TWO organism types proposed by size/geography: SMALLER worm (400-1000um) = Ancylostoma caninum (dog hookworm) possibly, or T. canis (though never actually isolated from a DUSN eye); LARGER worm (1500-2000um) = Baylisascaris procyonis (raccoon roundworm), found in northern midwestern US/Canada. Reported OUTSIDE North America too.
Insidious UNILATERAL vision loss from recurrent focal/multifocal/diffuse retina+RPE+optic nerve inflammation

EARLY stage: moderate-severe vitritis, disc edema, multiple TRANSIENT focal gray-white lesions (1200-1500um) in postequatorial fundus, +/- overlying exudative RD. WORM MAY BE VISUALIZED in subretinal space especially in this early stage - actively search for it.
LATE stage: retinal arteriolar narrowing, optic atrophy, diffuse pigment epithelial degeneration, abnormal ERG
Systemic/lab workup typically NEGATIVE - this is a clinically-diagnosed disease, not lab-confirmed

differential_note.early_stage: sarcoidosis_uveitis, multifocal_choroiditis_panuveitis, apmppe, mewds, serpiginous_choroidopathy, behcet_uveitis, toxocariasis_ocular, poh_syndrome, nonspecific optic neuritis/papillitis
differential_note.late_stage: Posttraumatic chorioretinopathy, occlusive vascular disease, toxic retinopathy, retinitis_pigmentosa_masquerade - the late-stage picture can be genuinely confused with true retinal degeneration

diseases.critical_note: DIAGNOSIS IS MOST STRONGLY SUPPORTED BY DIRECTLY VISUALIZING THE WORM in the subretinal space - this should be actively searched for whenever DUSN is suspected, as it is both diagnostic AND directly treatable (via laser) if found.

treatment.medical_alone_insufficient: Corticosteroids ALONE only transiently control inflammation - not a definitive treatment
treatment.first_line_if_worm_visualized: DIRECT LASER PHOTOCOAGULATION of the worm in early disease stages - NOT inflammatory itself, and may be HIGHLY EFFECTIVE at halting progression
treatment.if_worm_not_visualized: Oral THIABENDAZOLE (22mg/kg BID x2-4 days, max 3g) OR ALBENDAZOLE (200mg BID x30 days, may be BETTER TOLERATED) to increase chance of identifying/treating the nematode
treatment.if_inflammation_persists_after_laser: Consider anthelminthic therapy for a presumed SECOND undetected nematode

diseases.name: Onchocerciasis (River Blindness)
diseases.source: Formalized/enriched from a brief Kanski-derived reference note (never had its own full KB entity). AAO BCSC Ch.12.
key_features: Endemic to sub-Saharan Africa and isolated foci in Central/South America. Worldwide: >=25 million infected, ~300,000 blind, 800,000 visually impaired - major global blindness cause
Humans are the ONLY host; transmitted by female black flies breeding near rivers ('River Blindness')
Microfilariae reach the eye via: direct corneal invasion from conjunctiva, scleral penetration (direct or via vascular bundles), possibly hematogenous spread
Microfilariae visible SWIMMING FREELY in the anterior chamber; also in cornea (dead microfilariae cause stromal PUNCTATE keratitis)
Anterior uveitis -> synechiae, secondary glaucoma, cataract. Posterior: RPE disruption/focal atrophy; advanced disease -> severe chorioretinal AND optic atrophy

investigations.name: Skin biopsy (small snips) or ocular examination for microfilariae
investigations.note: Confirms diagnosis; clinical suspicion based on exposure history in endemic area

treatment.first_line: IVERMECTIN every 3-6 months as long as skin/eye infection evidence persists - kills MICROFILARIAE but has NO PERMANENT effect on ADULT worms
treatment.adjunct_for_adult_worms: Concomitant DOXYCYCLINE kills adult worms indirectly by eradicating their symbiotic Wolbachia bacteria partner
treatment.critical_safety_warning: Patients COINFECTED with O. volvulus AND Loa loa are at risk of a FATAL ENCEPHALITIC REACTION to ivermectin - MANDATORY infectious disease specialist consultation for these patients before treatment.

diseases.name: HIV Retinopathy (microangiopathy)
diseases.source: NEW entity - the most common ocular finding in AIDS, previously not captured as its own entity (distinct from opportunistic CMV retinitis). AAO BCSC Ch.13.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: NOT a true uveitis/inflammatory process - a MICROANGIOPATHY. Included because it's the most common AIDS-associated ocular finding and a key differential/mimic for early CMV retinitis.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (HIV-related vascular/microangiopathic process, though HIV antigen itself detected in retinal vascular endothelium)
key_features: MOST COMMON ocular finding in AIDS patients
Characterized mainly by COTTON-WOOL SPOTS, also microaneurysms and retinal hemorrhages
Associated with HIGHER viral loads and LOWER CD4+ counts - severity tracks with systemic disease control
HIV has been isolated from human retina and its antigen detected in retinal vascular endothelial cells - HIV endothelial infection may directly contribute to cotton-wool spots/vascular changes
AIDS-associated ACCELERATED AGING may contribute to earlier-onset macular degeneration and cataracts as part of the broader ocular phenotype
diseases.differential_note: CRITICAL DIFFERENTIATION FROM EARLY CMV RETINITIS: HIV retinopathy cotton-wool spots can resemble EARLY CMV retinitis infiltrates. KEY DISCRIMINATOR: HIV retinopathy does NOT progress without treatment (it's not an active infection), whereas CMV retinitis PROGRESSES if untreated. This distinction is critical since HIV retinopathy needs NO specific ocular treatment, while CMV retinitis is vision/life-threatening if missed - serial observation to assess for progression can be diagnostically useful when the picture is ambiguous.

treatment.note: No specific treatment needed for the retinopathy itself - managed via optimizing systemic ART/HIV control. Not a treatable ocular inflammatory condition.

diseases.name: Pneumocystis jirovecii Choroiditis
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.13.
key_features: RARE complication of P. jirovecii pneumonia (which AIDS patients are at increased risk for)
Choroidal lesions: slightly elevated, plaque-like, yellow-white, with MINIMAL vitritis
FA: hypofluorescent early phase, hyperfluorescent late phase
Should prompt evaluation for DISSEMINATED P. jirovecii infection by infectious disease specialist

investigations.name: Infectious disease workup for disseminated infection

treatment.regimen: IV trimethoprim 20mg/kg/day + sulfamethoxazole 100mg/kg/day for 3 weeks, OR pentamidine 4mg/kg/day
treatment.outcome: Most yellow-white lesions disappear within 3-12 weeks post-treatment, leaving mild pigmentary changes. Vision USUALLY NOT AFFECTED - generally good visual prognosis unlike many other AIDS-associated choroidal infections.

diseases.name: Ocular Cryptococcosis
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.13.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (fungal - Cryptococcus neoformans, yeast found in soil/pigeon feces worldwide)
diseases.course: can progress to endophthalmitis if untreated
key_features: MOST COMMON cause of fungal MENINGITIS and the MOST FREQUENT fungal eye infection in AIDS patients (despite being uncommon overall)
Predilection for CNS; ocular infection may reach eye hematogenously OR via direct extension from optic nerve given frequent meningitis association
Ocular infection can occur MONTHS after meningitis onset, or RARELY before clinically apparent CNS disease - timing is variable relative to CNS involvement
Meningitis-associated ocular findings: optic nerve edema -> optic atrophy; also nystagmus, cranial nerve palsies (diplopia, ptosis, ophthalmoplegia)
MOST FREQUENT non-meningitis-related presentation: MULTIFOCAL CHOROIDITIS +/- granulomatous AC inflammation, variable vitritis, vascular sheathing, exudative RD, papilledema
Severe intraocular infection CAN progress to endophthalmitis even WITHOUT meningitis or apparent systemic disease - can be an isolated severe ocular presentation

investigations.name: India ink stain
investigations.note: Demonstrates the organism

investigations.name: C. neoformans culture (vitreous, chorioretinal biopsy, or CSF)

treatment.first_line: IV amphotericin B + oral flucytosine required to halt progression
treatment.alternative: Systemic and intravitreal voriconazole may be considered
treatment.prognosis: POOR visual recovery prognosis with optic nerve or macular involvement

diseases.name: Endogenous Candida Endophthalmitis
diseases.source: NEW entity, formalized as distinct from general endogenous_endophthalmitis given its own well-characterized clinical picture. AAO BCSC Ch.14.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (fungal - Candida species, MOST COMMON cause of endogenous fungal endophthalmitis)
key_features: Candida albicans most common (natural skin/mucous membrane/gut organism); non-albicans species (e.g. C. glabrata) also implicated
Hematogenous spread to choroid -> breaks through Bruch membrane -> subretinal abscess -> secondary retinal/vitreous involvement
Classic: MULTIPLE, BILATERAL, WHITE, well-circumscribed lesions <1mm diameter, postequatorial fundus, with overlying vitreous inflammation +/- vascular sheathing/intraretinal hemorrhage
Vitreous opacities can assume a 'STRING-OF-PEARLS' appearance

Endophthalmitis rate in candidemia patients is ACTUALLY LOW (<1% in a large systematic review with >1000 prospectively-identified candidemia patients using rigorous endophthalmitis definition) - don't overestimate baseline risk

Candidemia patients often have comorbidities (anemia, thrombocytopenia, hypertension) that can independently cause intraretinal hemorrhages/Roth spots/cotton-wool spots WITHOUT actual intraocular infection - don't over-diagnose endophthalmitis from nonspecific fundus findings alone in this population
diseases.screening_update_aao: IMPORTANT UPDATED GUIDANCE: AAO currently recommends ophthalmic consultation ONLY for candidemia patients with a CLINICAL RATIONALE (symptoms/signs concerning for endophthalmitis, OR intubated/unable-to-communicate-symptoms patients) - ROUTINE screening dilated exam for ALL candidemia patients is NO LONGER recommended (this supersedes an older practice of baseline dilated exam + 2-week monitoring for all candidemic patients).
diseases.differential_note: Differential: other fungal/bacterial infection, toxoplasmosis_retinitis (similar posterior pole lesions), pars_planitis (Candida snowball lesions can resemble it).

investigations.name: Fungal stain + Sabouraud agar culture + pan-fungal PCR + Candida-specific PCR on undiluted vitreous
Diagnostic +/- therapeutic vitrectomy
investigations.note: Especially for severe disease or when systemic infection not yet confirmed

treatment.chorioretinal_only_no_vitreous: Oral triazole: fluconazole OR voriconazole 200mg BID for 2-4 weeks, with vigilant monitoring for progression. Voriconazole often PREFERRED (good oral bioavailability, achieves therapeutic intravitreal levels, broad spectrum).
treatment.vitreous_involved: Intravitreal antifungal (amphotericin B 5-10ug/0.1mL OR voriconazole 100ug/0.1mL - cross-reference appendix_b_intravitreal_antimicrobial_reference_aao) usually WITH pars plana vitrectomy. AVOID long-acting corticosteroid injections.
treatment.vitrectomy_dual_role: Diagnostic (microbiologic/molecular sampling) AND therapeutic (debulk pathogen load, clear vitreous inflammation)
treatment.severe_systemic: IV amphotericin B +/- flucytosine. LIPOSOMAL lipid-complex formulations greatly reduce dose-limiting toxicity (renal/cardiac/neurologic) vs conventional amphotericin B.
treatment.echinocandins: Caspofungin (IV, active against Candida AND Aspergillus) used successfully in some cases though treatment failures reported; micafungin also available (US/Europe)
treatment.duration: Systemic antifungal treatment may be needed 6 WEEKS or LONGER

diseases.name: Endogenous Aspergillus Endophthalmitis
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.14.
diseases.etiology_group: infectious (fungal mold - Aspergillus species, esp. A. fumigatus and A. flavus)
key_features: RARE - human exposure to Aspergillus spores (from soil/decaying vegetation) is VERY COMMON but actual infection is rare, depending on organism virulence and host immunocompetence

Associated with DISSEMINATED aspergillosis: severe chronic pulmonary disease, cancer, organ transplant, endocarditis, severe immunocompromise, IV drug use - though ISOLATED cases in immunocompetent patients with NO apparent risk factors do occur
Disseminated infection: lungs MOST common site, EYE is the SECOND most common site
RAPID onset of pain + vision loss - faster/more aggressive than typical Candida presentation

Confluent YELLOWISH infiltrate in posterior pole, beginning in choroid/subretinal space; hypopyon can develop in SUBRETINAL or SUBHYALOIDAL space (unusual locations, distinct from typical anterior chamber hypopyon)
Retinal hemorrhages, retinal VASCULAR OCCLUSIONS, full-thickness retinal necrosis possible; healed macular lesions form central atrophic scar
diseases.differential_note: vs Candida: Aspergillus lesions are LARGER, MORE LIKELY hemorrhagic, and COMMONLY INVADE retinal/choroidal VESSELS causing broad ischemic infarction areas - a notably more destructive/vasculo-invasive pattern than Candida. Also differentiate from cmv_retinitis, toxoplasmosis_retinitis, Coccidioidomycosis, bacterial endophthalmitis.

investigations.name: Demonstration of dichotomously branching SEPTATE HYPHAE on vitreous specimen
investigations.note: Confirmatory finding

investigations.name: Search for coexisting systemic aspergillosis
investigations.note: Strong clue for ocular dissemination in high-risk patients. NOTE: Aspergillus organisms may be DIFFICULT TO CULTURE FROM BLOOD - a negative blood culture does NOT rule out disseminated disease.

treatment.approach: AGGRESSIVE - diagnostic + therapeutic pars plana vitrectomy COMBINED with intravitreal amphotericin B or voriconazole; intravitreal corticosteroids may be used ADJUNCTIVELY with these antifungals
treatment.systemic: Given most patients have disseminated aspergillosis: oral voriconazole, IV amphotericin B, or caspofungin often required. Other options: itraconazole, miconazole, fluconazole, ketoconazole.
treatment.comanagement: Systemic aspergillosis managed by infectious disease specialist

diseases.prognosis: POOR despite aggressive treatment - frequent MACULAR involvement means final VA usually <20/200. Disseminated aspergillosis carries a HIGH MORTALITY rate - this is as much a systemic emergency as an ocular one.

diseases.name: Diffuse Anterior Scleritis
diseases.source: NEW entity - genuinely new disease category (scleritis, distinct from uveitis). AAO BCSC Ch.7.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Anterior TO THE RECTI MUSCLES (scleral location descriptor) - NOT the same concept as intraocular anatomic_class used for true uveitis entities.
key_features: MOST COMMON and LEAST SEVERE type of scleritis - complication risk <10% in most affected individuals
Severe pain that may WORSEN with eye movement and RADIATE to face/jaw - a distinctive pain pattern worth specifically asking about
Scleral EDEMA + intense hyperemia -> characteristic VIOLACEOUS hue on external exam (best appreciated in daylight/room light with upper lid lifted, NOT just slit lamp)
Slit lamp: marked dilation of the DEEP episcleral plexus, displaced outward by scleral edema - contrast with episcleritis where only the SUPERFICIAL plexus is involved and there is NO scleral edema
Diffuse swelling/infiltration WITHOUT nodule formation (distinguishes from nodular_anterior_scleritis)
AC reaction occasionally seen
diseases.differential_note: Key exam distinguishing feature from episcleritis: phenylephrine blanching test shows PARTIAL/NO blanching in scleritis vs COMPLETE blanching in episcleritis (superficial plexus only).

investigations.name: Targeted systemic workup per suspected associated disease
investigations.note: See scleritis_classification_reference_aao.investigation_table_7_2 for disease-specific panels

treatment.first_line: Oral NSAIDs (see scleritis_classification_reference_aao.nsaid_dosing_table_7_3) for NO MORE THAN 2 WEEKS - not for long-term management
treatment.gi_protection: Consider H2-antagonist or PPI given gastroduodenal ulceration risk; monitor renal function
treatment.escalation: Systemic corticosteroids (1mg/kg/day up to 60-80mg/day, slow taper) if refractory to NSAIDs
treatment.topical_adjunct: Topical corticosteroids can help concurrent iridocyclitis (present in ~1/3 of SEVERE scleritis cases) to prevent synechiae/uveitic glaucoma/cataract - though topical therapy ALONE is usually INEFFECTIVE for the scleritis itself

diseases.prognosis: Good with treatment - most eyes maintain good visual function long-term.

diseases.name: Nodular Anterior Scleritis
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.7.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (immune-mediated), rule out infectious cause especially if necrosis present
diseases.course: can progress to necrotizing disease in up to 10% of patients
key_features: TENDER, typically IMMOBILE scleral NODULE + local/diffuse violaceous hue from engorged deep episcleral vessels
Progresses to NECROTIZING disease in up to 10% of cases, PARTICULARLY when an underlying systemic inflammatory condition is present
Necrotic change: AVASCULAR area in nodule CENTER (eventually with superficial ulceration), OR a new discrete lesion extending circumferentially
After resolution: increased scleral translucency, eventually thinning revealing the BLUISH hue of underlying uveal tissue
diseases.critical_note: Important to actively RULE OUT INFECTIOUS etiologies of nodular scleritis, ESPECIALLY when necrosis is present - infectious and immune-mediated nodular scleritis can look similar but require completely different treatment approaches (immunosuppression is dangerous if infection is the true cause).

investigations.name: Consider microbiologic workup if necrosis present
investigations.note: Cross-reference infectious_scleritis

treatment.note: Same general approach as diffuse_anterior_scleritis (NSAID first, then systemic steroid) - escalate more readily given the higher necrotizing-progression risk.

diseases.name: Necrotizing Scleritis (with inflammation)
diseases.source: NEW entity - the most severe/destructive scleritis category. AAO BCSC Ch.7.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Can extend posteriorly too, and the postsurgical/SINS subtype in particular can develop distant from the original surgical site.
diseases.granulomatous: variable by subtype (granulomatous subtype specifically is granulomatous; others may show granulomatous histology even when not the 'granulomatous' clinical subtype)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: 'more commonly affects older individuals' - lansia bucket, though exact cutoff not given numerically.
key_features: MOST SEVERE and DESTRUCTIVE scleritis type - highest vision-loss risk
Systemic disease association in 50-60% of cases (HIGHER than scleritis overall ~40%) - includes LIFE-THREATENING vasculitic diseases
Historical mortality up to 30% when associated with systemic inflammatory disease - IMPROVED SIGNIFICANTLY with modern biologic therapies (an important prognosis-over-time note)

3 clinical subtypes with inflammation: VASO-OCCLUSIVE (vessel wall destruction -> ischemia/nonperfusion, limbus/peripheral cornea often SPARED, usually infection or systemic disease-associated), GRANULOMATOUS (starts at limbus, extends to cornea+sclera, 'lumpy' appearance, STRONGLY associated with granulomatosis_with_polyangiitis and polyarteritis_nodosa specifically), and POSTSURGICAL/SINS (surgically induced necrotizing scleritis - see below)

Up to 15% of NONNECROTIZING scleritis cases can PROGRESS to necrotizing disease over time - if this occurs, it warrants FURTHER investigation for associated causes and therapy escalation, not just local treatment intensification

postsurgical_sins_detail.definition: Surgically Induced Necrotizing Scleritis - arises MONTHS to YEARS after scleral surgical trauma
associated_procedures: Pterygium excision WITH mitomycin C or beta radiation
postsurgical_sins_detail.critical_distinction: MUST exclude infection to distinguish SINS from postoperative INFECTIOUS scleritis - these require completely different management
postsurgical_sins_detail.location_note: Necrosis develops commonly AT the surgical site, but SOMETIMES more distant from the incision
postsurgical_sins_detail.pathogenesis: Many SINS patients have an underlying AUTOIMMUNE disease - the surgical scleral injury is hypothesized as a local TRIGGER for the inflammatory process, not a purely mechanical complication

diseases.differential_diagnosis_from_ch10_crossre: GPA (granulomatosis_with_polyangiitis) and polyarteritis_nodosa are the classic systemic vasculitides associated with the granulomatous necrotizing subtype - cross-reference those entities' full systemic detail already in this KB.

investigations.name: Full systemic vasculitis workup per Table 7-2
investigations.note: Given 50-60% systemic association rate, thorough workup is essential, not optional

investigations.name: Rule out infection (culture/biopsy) BEFORE immunosuppression
investigations.note: Critical safety step given high stakes of getting this wrong

treatment.systemic_steroid: 1mg/kg/day prednisone (up to 60-80mg/day), slow taper - but ONLY after infection excluded/treated
treatment.iv_pulse_option: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day x3 days, followed by high-dose oral prednisone (up to 60-80mg/day) for severe cases
treatment.imt: Antimetabolites (methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, azathioprine) as steroid-sparing/adjuvant for recurrent/refractory disease, ESPECIALLY necrotizing
treatment.biologics: TNF inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab) and anti-CD20 (rituximab) successfully used for refractory cases
treatment.alkylating_agents: Cyclophosphamide RESERVED for severe necrotizing involvement of sclera+cornea, especially with IMPENDING PERFORATION risk
treatment.vasculitis_specific: GPA- and PAN-associated scleritis TYPICALLY requires MORE AGGRESSIVE therapy with rituximab or cyclophosphamide specifically
treatment.surgical: Scleral grafting (tectonic) for impending perforation or to close a defect - cadaveric donor sclera CAN melt, so some prefer autogenous periosteum or donor cornea instead

diseases.prognosis: HIGH risk of permanent vision loss. Higher mortality given frequent association with life-threatening vasculitic disorders - proper timely systemic management is as important as ocular treatment.

diseases.name: Scleromalacia Perforans (necrotizing scleritis WITHOUT overt inflammation)
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.7.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (immune-mediated, granulomatous on histology despite lack of clinical inflammation)
diseases.age_group_note: AAO: 'often older adult women with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis'.
key_features: HISTORICAL MISNOMER ALERT: previously called 'scleritis WITHOUT inflammation' - this is INACCURATE, since histology DOES show inflammatory cell infiltration (necrotizing granulomatous response) - the inflammation is just clinically SILENT/asymptomatic, not truly absent
LACK of significant symptoms/signs of clinical scleral inflammation despite active histologic disease - patients often do NOT report pain
Progressive circumferential scleral destruction eventually leaving a STAPHYLOMA
Classic patient: OLDER women with LONG-STANDING rheumatoid arthritis
Yellowish/white necrotic plaques (sequestrum) on sclera/episclera of BOTH eyes, surrounded by mildly dilated episcleral vessels
Staphylomata covered by conjunctiva + thin translucent fibrous tissue layer
DESPITE the name 'perforans', these lesions do NOT usually perforate spontaneously - reassuring despite the alarming name

investigations.name: Rheumatoid arthritis workup if not already established

treatment.note: Not explicitly detailed as distinct from other necrotizing scleritis management in this excerpt - general necrotizing scleritis principles apply, informed by underlying RA control.

diseases.name: Posterior Scleritis
diseases.source: NEW entity - sight-threatening, easily missed given lack of anterior signs when isolated. AAO BCSC Ch.7.
diseases.anatomic_pattern_note: Defined as scleral inflammation POSTERIOR TO THE ORA SERRATA - a scleral-location descriptor, not the intraocular uveitis anatomic_class concept.
diseases.etiology_group: non-infectious (immune-mediated) predominantly, systemic association rate comparable to anterior scleritis
key_features: DIAGNOSTIC TRAP: may be DIFFICULT TO RECOGNIZE unless concomitant with anterior scleritis, because of LACK of visible anterior inflammatory signs when isolated - a high index of suspicion is needed
Severe deep BORING eye pain + tenderness on palpation - BUT in 30-40% of patients these symptoms are MILD OR ABSENT (don't require pain to consider the diagnosis)
Decreased vision often reported - depends on inflammation location/structures involved
Possible signs: choroidal detachment/folds, subretinal fluid, optic disc edema
Anterior rotation of pars plicata (ciliary body) can push the lens-iris diaphragm forward -> ANGLE CLOSURE + acute IOP elevation - an important secondary glaucoma mechanism to recognize
Extraocular muscle involvement (myositis) can occasionally cause diplopia
diseases.critical_diagnostic_sign: CLASSIC 'T-SIGN' on B-scan ultrasonography: accumulation of sub-Tenon fluid CONTIGUOUS TO THE OPTIC NERVE SHADOW creates a T-shaped appearance - this is the single most useful confirmatory imaging sign for posterior scleritis. B-scan also typically shows scleral thickening with sub-Tenon fluid accumulation.
diseases.differential_note: vs vkh_syndrome: posterior scleritis with exudative RD can resemble VKH - compare clinical features carefully (cross-reference vkh_syndrome differential_diagnosis_aao which already lists posterior scleritis as a VKH differential).

investigations.name: B-scan ultrasonography
investigations.note: Look for the T-sign; also assess choroid/ciliary body/retina/EOM/orbit involvement

investigations.name: FA and/or ICGA
investigations.note: FA: optic nerve staining, multiple pinpoint leakage, late-phase subretinal fluid pooling

investigations.name: CT/MRI with and without gadolinium
investigations.note: If orbital process is a concern

treatment.note: Same systemic treatment ladder as severe anterior scleritis (NSAID -> systemic steroid -> IMT/biologics as needed) - cross-reference necrotizing_scleritis.treatment for the escalation ladder, since posterior disease is generally managed with similar aggression to severe/necrotizing anterior disease given sight-threatening potential.

diseases.prognosis: HIGH risk of permanent vision loss, similar to necrotizing and infectious scleritis - one of the three 'high-risk' scleritis categories per AAO.

diseases.name: Infectious Scleritis
diseases.source: NEW entity. AAO BCSC Ch.7.
diseases.course: can occur after any ocular surgery, from RECENT to MANY YEARS prior
key_features: Wide range of causative organisms: PSEUDOMONAS (most common AFTER PTERYGIUM EXCISION specifically), Actinomyces, Nocardia, mycobacteria, fungi (Fusarium, Aspergillus), gram-positive cocci (Strep pneumoniae, Streptococcus species). HSV or VZV can cause CHRONIC scleritis too.

Can occur after ANY ocular surgery: pterygium surgery (ESPECIALLY with beta radiation or mitomycin C), scleral buckling, cataract surgery, pars plana vitrectomy - timing can be recent, months later, or even YEARS after the precipitating surgery
Can also follow penetrating injury contaminated by VEGETABLE OR ORGANIC MATTER
NODULAR and NECROTIZING scleral disease patterns are MORE COMMON in infectious vs noninfectious scleritis
If associated intraocular inflammation (sclerouveitis) is present, it tends to be DISPROPORTIONATELY MORE SUBSTANTIAL in infectious scleritis - a useful severity-mismatch clue
Sclera appears necrotic, thin, avascular, with inflammation AT THE EDGES, usually at a surgical/traumatic wound site. Mucopurulent discharge possible depending on organism.
diseases.critical_note: MUST BE DISTINGUISHED from noninfectious scleritis (including SINS - cross-reference necrotizing_scleritis.postsurgical_sins_detail) because AGGRESSIVE CORTICOSTEROID/IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE THERAPY GIVEN FOR AN UNIDENTIFIED INFECTIOUS ETIOLOGY CAN LEAD TO DEVASTATING VISUAL CONSEQUENCES - this is one of the highest-stakes differentiation points in the entire scleritis category.

investigations.name: Incisional scleral biopsy
investigations.note: Very helpful with high clinical suspicion; also valuable to exclude neoplastic conditions (conjunctival carcinoma, lymphoma) which can masquerade as or accompany scleral inflammation

treatment.approach: Systemic (and topical) antimicrobials + surgical debridement as needed
treatment.duration: Microorganisms may be DIFFICULT TO ERADICATE from the sclera - LONG-TERM antimicrobial treatment may be necessary
treatment.surgical: Scleral grafting if severe scleral thinning develops; debridement of necrotic tissue reduces organism load and improves medication penetration

diseases.prognosis: HIGH risk of permanent vision loss - one of the three 'high-risk' scleritis categories per AAO (along with posterior and necrotizing).

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: severe eye pain worsening with eye movement, radiating to face/jaw + violaceous hue + deep episcleral vessel dilation + PARTIAL/NO blanching with topical phenylephrine
quick_pattern_matchers.note: The phenylephrine blanching test (partial/no blanching) plus deep-plexus-not-superficial-plexus involvement distinguishes scleritis from episcleritis. High-grade INTRAOCULAR inflammation would suggest AAU/endophthalmitis instead of scleritis for a similarly non-blanching eye.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: deep boring eye pain (or possibly NO pain in 30-40% of cases) + decreased vision + choroidal folds/subretinal fluid/disc edema + NO visible anterior scleral inflammation
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Easily missed without anterior signs - actively obtain B-scan looking for the classic T-sign (sub-Tenon fluid contiguous with optic nerve shadow) whenever this is suspected.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Order B-scan ultrasonography to look for T-sign.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: scleritis + necrotic/avascular area developing at the site of PRIOR ocular surgery (cataract, strabismus, retinal, trabeculectomy, cryotherapy, pterygium with mitomycin/beta-radiation), occurring months to years later
quick_pattern_matchers.note: This is SINS (surgically induced necrotizing scleritis) - but infection MUST be excluded first before attributing to SINS, since postoperative infectious scleritis looks similar and requires opposite treatment (antimicrobials, not immunosuppression).

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: older woman + long-standing rheumatoid arthritis + bilateral yellowish/white necrotic scleral plaques + NO pain or overt inflammation reported
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Despite the alarming name, spontaneous perforation is actually UNCOMMON. The lack of symptoms does NOT mean lack of disease activity - histology shows active granulomatous inflammation despite clinically silent presentation.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: scleritis + recent pterygium excision with mitomycin C or beta radiation, OR scleral wound contaminated by vegetable/organic matter, OR disproportionately severe intraocular inflammation for the degree of visible scleral disease
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Nodular/necrotizing patterns and disproportionate sclerouveitis favor infectious over noninfectious scleritis. Getting this wrong (treating infection with immunosuppression) can be devastating.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Obtain scleral scraping/biopsy for microbiology before committing to aggressive immunosuppressive therapy.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: necrotizing scleritis + 'lumpy' limbal-starting granulomatous appearance + known or suspected systemic vasculitis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: The granulomatous necrotizing subtype is strongly linked to GPA and PAN specifically - cross-reference those entities for full systemic workup (c-ANCA/PR3 for GPA, hepatitis B serology for PAN).

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: chronic recurring anterior inflammation months after cataract surgery + whitish plaque visible between IOL and posterior capsule + responds to steroids but recurs on taper
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Do NOT perform Nd:YAG capsulotomy if this diagnosis is suspected - it can liberate sequestered organisms and worsen disease.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: chronic postoperative intraocular inflammation that WORSENS after corticosteroid therapy (topical/periocular/intraocular)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Worsening on steroids should automatically raise fungal suspicion over bacterial C. acnes.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Consider fungal stains/culture/PCR before further steroid escalation.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: systemic sepsis-like illness OR recent infection/surgery/diabetes/IVDU history + acute severe intraocular inflammation (anterior AND posterior) + especially if BOTH eyes affected
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Detailed history/ROS is essential since the source (e.g. osteomyelitis, sinusitis, pneumonia misdiagnosed as URI) can be difficult to identify by labs alone.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: liver abscess history (or unexplained sepsis in a patient from/in Asia) + endogenous-appearing endophthalmitis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Klebsiella-from-liver-abscess is THE dominant pattern for endogenous bacterial endophthalmitis in Asia specifically - actively search for occult liver abscess in this context.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: candidemia + multiple bilateral small (<1mm) white postequatorial chorioretinal lesions + string-of-pearls vitreous opacities
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Endophthalmitis rate in candidemia is actually LOW (<1%) - don't over-attribute nonspecific fundus findings (Roth spots, cotton-wool spots from comorbidities like anemia/thrombocytopenia) to true endophthalmitis.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: candidemia patient with NO ocular symptoms and able to communicate normally
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Per current AAO guidance, routine dilated ophthalmic screening is NO LONGER recommended for ALL candidemia patients - reserve ophthalmic consultation for those with a clinical rationale (symptoms/signs, or intubated/unable to report symptoms).

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: rapid-onset severe eye pain and vision loss + confluent yellowish posterior pole infiltrate + hypopyon in an unusual (subretinal/subhyaloidal) location + disseminated pulmonary/systemic risk factors
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Larger, more hemorrhagic, more vaso-invasive lesions than Candida - a distinctly more aggressive/destructive pattern. Blood cultures often falsely negative even with true dissemination.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: AIDS/HIV patient + cotton-wool spots + no progression on serial observation
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Key differentiator from early CMV retinitis is LACK of progression without treatment - if serial exams show progression, reconsider CMV retinitis instead.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: AIDS patient with CD4+ <50 + small white retinal infiltrate resembling cotton-wool spot + PROGRESSES on follow-up
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Progression despite the cotton-wool-spot-like appearance distinguishes this from hiv_retinopathy. CD4+ <50 warrants 3-monthly screening even if asymptomatic.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: AIDS patient + P. jirovecii pneumonia history + slightly elevated plaque-like yellow-white choroidal lesions + minimal vitritis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Generally good visual prognosis unlike most other AIDS-associated choroidal infections - reassuring once diagnosis confirmed.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: AIDS patient + multifocal choroiditis + history of or concurrent cryptococcal meningitis, OR optic nerve edema/atrophy with cranial nerve palsies
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Ocular involvement can precede OR follow meningitis by months - timing does not rule this out either way.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: AIDS patient + multifocal choroidal lesions found incidentally
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Multifocal choroiditis in AIDS is found in up to 10% of patients and should prompt an EXHAUSTIVE systemic workup regardless of which specific organism is suspected first - multiple simultaneous infectious agents are possible given profound immunosuppression.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: HIV/AIDS patient + vitritis WITHOUT visible chorioretinitis as an isolated finding
quick_pattern_matchers.note: This atypical presentation (vitritis without chorioretinitis) can be the FIRST manifestation of syphilis specifically in AIDS patients - don't require classic chorioretinal lesions to consider syphilis in this population.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: HIV/AIDS patient + bilateral large toxoplasma-like retinitis lesions WITHOUT an adjacent old scar
quick_pattern_matchers.note: AIDS-associated ocular toxoplasmosis often lacks the classic adjacent-scar pattern since it's more often newly acquired/disseminated rather than reactivated - larger lesions and up to 40% bilateral involvement are also atypical-but-expected in this population.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: HIV testing consideration: patient younger than 50 years presenting with facial/eyelid herpes zoster lesions
quick_pattern_matchers.action: AAO explicitly recommends considering HIV testing in this scenario - zoster in a younger patient can be a sentinel sign of underlying immunosuppression.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: recent febrile illness (fever/headache/myalgia/rash, possibly with bleeding tendency or low platelets) + subconjunctival petechial hemorrhage + sudden central vision loss/scotoma ~1 month later, imaging may show foveal avascular zone disruption with NORMAL-looking fundus exam
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Especially relevant in Indonesia given endemic Dengue - actively ask about recent febrile illness since patients may not volunteer a 'mild' fever. Imaging findings can be present even when the fundus exam looks unremarkable.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: fever + prominent arthralgia/polyarthropathy with stooped posture + anterior uveitis with ocular hypertension, OR posterior-pole retinitis with hemorrhage and MINIMAL vitritis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Distinguish retinitis from herpetic mimics by location (posterior pole vs peripheral) and vitritis grade (minimal vs high). Relevant in Indonesia given regional Aedes-borne co-circulation with Dengue.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: mosquito-borne febrile illness + conjunctivitis or anterior/posterior uveitis with numerous chorioretinal lesions or unilateral acute maculopathy, especially if pregnant or partner/family member pregnant
quick_pattern_matchers.note: All three arboviruses share the Aedes aegypti vector and can coinfect in endemic areas - consider all three together rather than assuming just one. Congenital Zika causes STRUCTURAL findings, not uveitis - if evaluating a newborn, look for microcephaly/structural changes rather than inflammation.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: multifocal chorioretinitis with lesions arranged in LINEAR ARRAYS following retinal nerve fiber layer + recent febrile/flu-like illness + conjunctival hyperemia
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Linear nerve-fiber-following arrangement of chorioretinal lesions is a distinctive pattern.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: macular or paramacular retinitis SPARING the fovea + recent contact with livestock/animal abortus + febrile illness
recurrent uveitis in a patient with known HTLV-1 endemic-region origin (e.g. Japan, Caribbean, parts of South America) + retinal vasculitis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: If HU appears refractory to treatment, actively consider ATL retinal infiltration or HAM/TSP retinal degeneration as alternative/concurrent diagnoses rather than assuming simply 'severe HU'.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: Ebola survivor + new or recurrent uveitis, even months after systemic recovery, especially unilateral hypertensive anterior uveitis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Virus can persist in ocular tissue (possibly RPE reservoir) long after systemic clearance.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: 'idiopathic' or birdshot-like posterior uveitis that FAILS to respond to standard corticosteroid/IMT therapy
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Consider PCR + local antibody analysis of aqueous for herpesviruses before further escalating immunosuppression - response to a switch to systemic ANTIVIRAL therapy is itself diagnostically supportive.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Trial systemic antiviral if viral workup positive or clinical suspicion remains high despite negative workup.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: translucent globular subretinal or intravitreal cyst with an undulating scolex visible on exam + history in/from Southeast Asia, Latin America, Africa, or India + undercooked pork consumption
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Directly relevant given Southeast Asia is an explicitly named endemic region. Do NOT attempt laser destruction or simple observation - plan for early surgical removal given risk of severe panuveitis on larval death.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: insidious unilateral vision loss + transient gray-white postequatorial lesions + moderate-severe vitritis + disc edema, especially in a child or young adult
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Actively search for a visible subretinal worm - this is both the strongest diagnostic evidence AND directly treatable via laser if found.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis with visible microfilariae swimming in the anterior chamber + punctate stromal keratitis + history of residence in sub-Saharan Africa or endemic Central/South American focus
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Before treating with ivermectin, screen for Loa loa coinfection given fatal encephalitic reaction risk in coinfected patients.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis with hyphema/hypopyon/posterior synechiae PRESENT + dendritiform corneal epithelial disease + reduced corneal sensation
quick_pattern_matchers.note: These features (hyphema/hypopyon/PS, dendritiform disease, reduced sensation) argue AGAINST cmv_anterior_uveitis, which typically lacks all of them.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis + ring-shaped KP clusters or scant small white-domed KP + corneal stromal immune ring + NO hyphema/hypopyon/PS + NO dendritiform disease or reduced corneal sensation
quick_pattern_matchers.note: This KP/corneal pattern combination is relatively specific for CMV over HSV/VZV.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: placoid yellowish-gray RPE-level lesion(s) + hyperautofluorescent on FAF + vitritis + sexually active adult
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinitis is essentially PATHOGNOMONIC for secondary syphilis when this imaging pattern is present.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: necrotizing retinitis resembling ARN/PORN BUT progressing MORE SLOWLY than typical + responds dramatically to empiric IV penicillin trial
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Treatment response itself can be diagnostically informative when syphilis serology is pending or ambiguous.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: infant/newborn OR older child with 'salt-and-pepper' bilateral retinopathy that is stable/non-progressive with normal-ish vision, OR girl with interstitial keratitis + deafness + notched teeth
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Congenital syphilis pattern - Hutchinson triad (interstitial keratitis + CN VIII deafness + Hutchinson teeth) is classic for late congenital disease.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: born outside the US (or in TB-endemic region) + choroidal granuloma OR serpiginous-like choroiditis OR necrotizing scleritis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Country of birth is the single most important US risk factor per AAO; in endemic regions like Indonesia this baseline probability is much higher, further favoring TB workup.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young healthy man 20-40yo + recurrent unilateral vitreous hemorrhage + peripheral retinal periphlebitis + eventual fellow-eye involvement
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Consider TB screening given hypothesized tuberculin-hypersensitivity link, though this is a distinct clinical entity from direct tubercular retinal disease.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: new-onset uveitis with NO tick-bite risk factors and NO systemic Lyme symptoms
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Do NOT order Lyme serology as a routine/reflexive screen in this scenario - per AAO, intraocular inflammation is not a common LD manifestation and screening without compatible history/symptoms is not indicated.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: systemic febrile illness + water/soil/animal exposure (agricultural, sewer, veterinary, fishery work, or swimming/rafting in endemic tropical/subtropical area) + delayed-onset anterior uveitis or panuveitis with retinal vasculitis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Ocular disease can present MONTHS after the acute systemic illness resolved - ask specifically about past febrile illness with relevant exposure, not just current symptoms. Highly relevant in tropical/subtropical settings.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: immunocompromised patient + unilateral choroidal/subretinal mass or abscess + MINIMAL vitritis (mimicking fungal endophthalmitis)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Consider Nocardia specifically when fungal cultures/workup are negative in a fungal-endophthalmitis-LIKE presentation in an immunocompromised host.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: child <10yo + cat exposure/scratch + regional lymphadenopathy + unilateral disc edema with macular star
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Classic neuroretinitis presentation. Disc edema/peripapillary serous RD can PRECEDE the macular star by 2-4 weeks - don't wait for the star to form before suspecting this diagnosis.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: middle-aged white man + migratory arthritis + diarrhea/malabsorption/weight loss + bilateral panuveitis with retinal vasculitis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Unusual granular/crystalline deposits on iris/capsule/IOL is a distinctive but uncommon associated sign. Untreated Whipple disease can be FATAL - urgent systemic workup needed, not just eye treatment.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: posterior segment finding (cotton-wool spots, retinal nonperfusion/infarction) in known SLE patient + NO significant vitritis or overt intraocular inflammation
quick_pattern_matchers.note: This is likely lupus RETINOPATHY (a vaso-occlusive, hypercoagulable-state process), NOT true uveitis - the mechanism is thrombotic, not inflammatory. True SLE-associated uveitis is rare.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: middle-aged man + retinal/choroidal vasculitis + spares the lungs + ANCA NEGATIVE + mononeuropathy multiplex
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Lung-sparing pattern + ANCA-negative status are key discriminators from granulomatosis_with_polyangiitis.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young woman + encephalopathy or cognitive change + branch retinal artery occlusion(s) + hearing loss/tinnitus + minimal vitreous cells
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Complete triad often absent at onset - any 1-2 of the 3 components with the others evolving over time should still raise suspicion. Boxcar arteriolar segmentation and Gass plaques (away from bifurcations, unlike Hollenhorst plaques) are supportive fundus signs.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: acute iritis + erythema nodosum + febrile arthropathy + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Classic Lofgren syndrome presentation - good prognosis, responds well to steroids.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: uveitis + parotitis + fever + facial nerve palsy
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Heerfordt syndrome ('uveoparotid fever') - a distinct acute sarcoidosis presentation.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: bilateral panuveitis after eye surgery/trauma + serous retinal detachments + Dalen-Fuchs nodules + NO history of penetrating trauma in the FELLOW eye
quick_pattern_matchers.note: By definition VKH requires NO trauma history at all (either eye); if there IS a trauma/surgery history in one eye and the fellow eye now shows similar findings, favor sympathetic_ophthalmia. The two are otherwise nearly immunogenetically identical.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: bilateral panuveitis + serous RD in cloverleaf pattern + headache/tinnitus/meningismus within past 4 weeks + no trauma history
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Matches SUN 2021 early-stage VKH classification option 2. Remember to exclude syphilis (treponemal test) and sarcoidosis (CXR/biopsy) - both are formal EXCLUSION criteria for VKH diagnosis, not just differentials.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: sunset-glow fundus OR (chronic anterior/panuveitis + vitiligo/poliosis/alopecia) + history of earlier VKH-compatible episode
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Matches SUN 2021 late-stage VKH classification criteria.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: sudden explosive posterior uveitis/panuveitis that spontaneously resolves within weeks + occlusive retinal vasculitis (chalky white retinitis) affecting both arteries and veins
quick_pattern_matchers.note: The ACUTE EXPLOSIVE then SELF-RESOLVING pattern (weeks, not months) is distinctive - contrast with most other panuveitis entities (VKH, sarcoid, SO) which tend toward chronic/smoldering courses.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young male 15-25yo + severe uveitis + delayed diagnosis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: AAO explicitly flags this demographic as having especially severe disease AND being prone to diagnostic delay - a dangerous combination worth actively screening for.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: family history of granulomatous disease + young child + panuveitis with multifocal choroiditis + skin/joint granulomatous disease
quick_pattern_matchers.note: NOD2 gene mutation confirmed as the cause (AAO). Overlaps clinically with pediatric sarcoidosis and JIA in this age group - family history is the key differentiator to actively ask about.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: chorioretinal lesions resembling birdshot BUT patient younger than typical BCR age AND HLA-A29 negative AND lesions smaller/more punched-out
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Key discriminators from birdshot_retinochoroidopathy: age, HLA-A29 status, lesion size/morphology.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young myopic woman + high-grade ANTERIOR chamber inflammation (unusual for a white dot syndrome) + panuveitis + lesions coalescing into subretinal fibrosis over time
quick_pattern_matchers.note: The HIGH-GRADE anterior involvement is atypical for most white dot syndromes (which are usually posterior-predominant with mild/absent anterior signs) - this is a distinguishing red flag for SFU specifically.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: placoid choroidal lesions + MULTIFOCAL and DISTINCTLY SEPARATE (not clustered from one origin point) + peripheral lesions predating posterior pole involvement
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Distinguishes from serpiginous_choroidopathy (single-origin, contiguous, peripapillary-starting pattern).

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: unilateral central metamorphopsia/scotoma + small dark spots with yellow halo in deep retina + honeycomb pattern on FA + mild/no vitritis + self-limited course
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Honeycomb FA pattern (central hypofluorescence + lacy hyperfluorescent rim) is distinctive.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: significant visual field loss or unusual photopsias (windmills/bubbles/sparkles) + CLINICALLY NORMAL-APPEARING FUNDUS on exam
quick_pattern_matchers.must_exclude: Optic neuritis, optic nerve compressive lesion, pituitary/intracranial tumor, autoimmune_retinopathy - these are more urgent/serious mimics that should be actively excluded, not assumed to be AZOOR by default.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young adult + acute unilateral central vision loss + turbid yellow macular subretinal fluid + recent hand-foot-mouth disease (self or child contact)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Do NOT confuse with acute_macular_neuroretinopathy (AMN) - similar name, different entity, different mechanism (AIM = subretinal fluid/RPE process; AMN = deep capillary plexus microvascular event, not even considered truly inflammatory anymore per AAO).

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: progressive bilateral vision loss + photopsias/scotomas/nyctalopia/photoaversion + NORMAL or near-normal fundus + ABSENT/minimal intraocular inflammation + abnormal ERG
quick_pattern_matchers.note: The near-total ABSENCE of visible inflammation despite real visual dysfunction is itself the key clue. Do not rely on serum anti-retinal antibody testing alone (poor correlation with phenotype, non-standardized across labs) - this is fundamentally a diagnosis of exclusion.
quick_pattern_matchers.must_exclude: Malignancy (for pAIR vs npAIR distinction), inherited retinal degeneration (retinitis_pigmentosa_masquerade), and any alternative uveitic diagnosis explaining the findings.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: suspected autoimmune retinopathy + known/suspected malignancy (especially small cell lung cancer) OR melanoma history
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Anti-recoverin antibodies support CAR (small cell lung cancer most common association); anti-TRPM1 antibodies support MAR. Absence of these antibodies does NOT rule out paraneoplastic AIR - age/sex-appropriate cancer screening may be more useful than chasing more antibody panels.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Treatment of underlying malignancy is the first treatment step, not local eye therapy.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: any white dot syndrome differential + patient has ANY CNS symptom (even isolated headache)
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Urgent neurologic evaluation with neuroimaging and CSF studies - rare but life-threatening cerebral vasculitis association with APMPPE specifically.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young man 20-40yo + acute unilateral (alternating) anterior uveitis + hypopyon/fibrin + high-grade AC cells
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Classic HLA-B27 AAU demographic and pattern per AAO Ch.8 explicit age range.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: psoriasis patient + anterior uveitis + insidious onset + bilateral + chronic course
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Psoriatic-arthritis-associated AAU pattern is INSIDIOUS/BILATERAL/CHRONIC - opposite pattern from classic acute-unilateral-alternating HLA-B27 AAU, even though both are in the spondyloarthritis family. If psoriasis WITHOUT arthritis: even older age of onset, longer duration, POSTERIOR segment involvement possible.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: child + oligoarticular JIA + ANA positive + girl + age <5 at JIA onset
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Highest-risk JIA subgroup for uveitis development per AAO Ch.8.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: child + JIA + enthesitis-related arthritis subtype + ACUTE painful red uveitis (not the usual silent JIA pattern)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: ERA-JIA is the exception to 'JIA uveitis is always silent' - it presents acutely, resembling HLA-B27 AAU pattern instead.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: postoperative or trauma-related anterior uveitis + INTACT lens capsule + mature/hypermature cataract + KP and synechiae ABSENT + refractile bodies in aqueous
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Absence of KP/synechiae is the key bedside differentiator from phacoantigenic uveitis (lens_induced_uveitis), which typically HAS KP/synechiae.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: postoperative or trauma anterior uveitis WITHIN 24 HOURS of capsule disruption + KP present + posterior synechiae common + elevated IOP
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Phacoantigenic uveitis (capsule disrupted, immune-mediated) - contrast with phacolytic_uveitis (capsule intact, mechanical).

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: floaters/vitritis + snowballs + snowbanks + age 5-15 OR 20-40 (bimodal) + no identifiable systemic cause after workup
quick_pattern_matchers.note: 85-90% of all intermediate uveitis is pars planitis - default/most-likely diagnosis for intermediate uveitis once specific causes (sarcoid, MS, syphilis, Lyme, toxocariasis, lymphoma) are excluded.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: new-onset vitreous cells + patient older than typical pars planitis age (>=50-60yo) + visual acuity better than expected for the amount of vitreous cells + macular edema ABSENT
quick_pattern_matchers.note: VA-better-than-expected + absent macular edema despite dense vitreous cells is a specific AAO-stated dissociation pattern favoring lymphoma over pars planitis.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Do not default to pars_planitis diagnosis in this age group without actively excluding lymphoma first.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: White woman 20-50yo + bilateral intermediate uveitis + MILD vitritis + periphlebitis + macular edema LESS prominent than typical
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Do NOT automatically order MRI/neuro workup for this demographic alone UNLESS neurologic symptoms are present - this pattern alone is a differential consideration, not an automatic imaging trigger.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: any 'idiopathic' intermediate uveitis about to start a TNF inhibitor
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Obtain brain MRI to screen for demyelinating disease BEFORE starting the TNF inhibitor, regardless of symptoms - TNF inhibitors are contraindicated in demyelinating disease and can exacerbate/unmask MS.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: adolescent/young adult + bilateral acute nongranulomatous anterior uveitis + fever/flank pain/systemic symptoms + abnormal urinalysis or creatinine
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Renal and ocular flares do NOT necessarily occur simultaneously - a quiet kidney does not rule out active TINU-related eye disease and vice versa.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: child + panuveitis with multifocal CHOROIDITIS (not just anterior) + skin granulomas + arthritis + family history of similar disease
quick_pattern_matchers.note: The posterior/choroidal component is what distinguishes this from typical anterior-only JIA-associated uveitis.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: new-onset uveitis temporally associated with starting a new medication (especially cidofovir, rifabutin, bisphosphonate, moxifloxacin, TNF-inhibitor, immune checkpoint inhibitor, or topical brimonidine)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Consider Naranjo Algorithm to formally grade causality. Discontinuing the agent may be diagnostic AND curative.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: cancer patient on immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy + new bilateral panuveitis resembling VKH + subretinal fluid on OCT
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-induced VKH-like panuveitis is a specific, increasingly recognized presentation - can recur with subsequent chemo cycles. Distinguish from true VKH by the temporal relationship to starting immunotherapy.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: long-term brimonidine use + red irritated eye + extensive granulomatous KP + low-grade anterior uveitis
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Classic brimonidine-associated uveitis presentation - consider stopping brimonidine before pursuing a full granulomatous-disease workup.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: age 50-70 + intermediate uveitis OR atypical posterior/panuveitis + partial/transient response to steroid
quick_pattern_matchers.note: TOP-PRIORITY masquerade check. Transient steroid response is a TRAP, not reassurance - VRL vitritis often improves temporarily on steroids.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Refer for vitreous biopsy / brain MRI evaluation regardless of neuro symptoms if this pattern present and case is otherwise atypical or refractory.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: multifocal cream/yellow choroidal lesions resembling sarcoidosis or birdshot + gradual painless vision loss + salmon-pink FIXED (non-mobile) episcleral/conjunctival mass
exudative retinal detachment resembling VKH + hematologic abnormality (anemia, abnormal CBC, bleeding tendency)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: VKH-mimicking exudative RD is a specific leukemia masquerade trap.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: bilateral panuveitis-like presentation resembling VKH + known or suspected systemic malignancy + rapid bilateral cataract + placoid pigmented AND nonpigmented iris/choroidal nodules
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Paraneoplastic VKH-mimic - search for occult malignancy is the diagnostic priority, not local eye treatment.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: child age 4-6 + white shifting pseudohypopyon + iris nodules + minimal/absent calcification on imaging
quick_pattern_matchers.note: White pseudohypopyon that SHIFTS with head position (vs yellow true inflammatory hypopyon) is the key distinguishing sign. Absence of calcification does NOT rule this out (diffuse infiltrating subtype often lacks it).
quick_pattern_matchers.must_exclude: Also cross-check toxocariasis_ocular - both are pediatric masquerades of granulomatous-appearing intraocular disease.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: infant under 1 year + spontaneous hyphema + reddish-yellow skin lesions
chronic anterior uveitis-like picture + PRESERVED good vision + peripheral fundus findings on scleral-depression exam (pigment demarcation lines, retinal breaks, subretinal fluid)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Relatively preserved vision despite apparent chronic uveitis is a clue this is NOT true uveitis. Elevated IOP can occur (Schwartz-Matsuo mechanism: photoreceptor outer segments clog trabecular meshwork).
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Always perform dilated fundus exam WITH SCLERAL DEPRESSION before labeling unexplained chronic anterior uveitis as idiopathic.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: man age >=65 + anterior chamber flare markedly out of proportion to cell count + anterior segment neovascularization
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Flare-cell dissociation (flare >> cells) is atypical for true uveitis and should prompt carotid workup.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: chronic unexplained unilateral uveitis + NO clear trauma history volunteered by patient
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Do not rely on the patient volunteering trauma history - it is often perceived as too mild to mention. Actively ask.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis + markedly increased IOP disproportionate to inflammation level, RECURRENT episodes, self-limited, NOT pseudophakic
quick_pattern_matchers.note: AAO Table 5-10 groups Posner-Schlossman/glaucomatocyclitic crisis with herpetic iritis as the shared first branch point when 'increased IOP' is the dominant associated factor. PCR of intraocular fluid is optional to help distinguish a viral cause.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis + increased IOP + PSEUDOPHAKIC patient (has IOL) + possible hyphema
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Pseudophakic status is the key differentiator from Posner-Schlossman/herpetic iritis on the same 'increased IOP' branch. Check IOL position/chafing.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: acute anterior chamber inflammation within days after cataract surgery / penetrating trauma / indwelling systemic lines
quick_pattern_matchers.note: This is ALSO the same clinical branch as infectious endophthalmitis (see endogenous_endophthalmitis) - the critical workup is B-scan for vitritis + vitreous culture to distinguish TASS (sterile, toxic) from true infection (needs antimicrobials) from chronic low-grade C. acnes infection (needs vitrectomy+capsulectomy+culture). Getting this wrong has serious consequences - steroids alone can be catastrophic if truly infectious.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Always rule out infection with B-scan/culture before treating as sterile TASS, regardless of how classic the toxic-reaction timing looks.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: chronic anterior OR atypical intermediate/posterior uveitis + patient older than 50 years + poor response to standard steroid therapy
quick_pattern_matchers.note: MASQUERADE SAFETY-NET - see vitreoretinal_lymphoma.critical_note. This should be actively surfaced and NOT suppressed by other well-matching infectious/autoimmune differentials when age >50 + atypical/refractory course are both present.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Do not let a good score elsewhere prevent this from being shown - masquerade exclusion should be a parallel check, not a competing rank.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: posterior uveitis + retinal vasculitis + malar rash + female + arthralgias
quick_pattern_matchers.note: SLE vasculitis is primarily ARTERITIS (Table 5-4) - contrast with sarcoidosis/MS/Behcet/birdshot which are primarily PHLEBITIS.
quick_pattern_matchers.source: AAO BCSC Ch.5, Table 5-10 and Table 5-4

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: posterior uveitis + retinal vasculitis + chronic sinusitis with hemorrhagic rhinorrhea + dyspnea + renal insufficiency + purpura
quick_pattern_matchers.note: c-ANCA (anti-proteinase 3) is the primary confirmatory test.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: intermediate uveitis + neurologic symptoms
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Vasculitis pattern (if present) is primarily PHLEBITIS, same category as sarcoidosis/Behcet - MRI brain+C-spine and LP for oligoclonal bands are the key confirmatory workup.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: febrile illness + flank or abdominal pain + anterior uveitis (any severity)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Check renal function/urinalysis/urine beta-2-microglobulin; HLA-DRB1*01:02 is the specific (though not required) HLA association.
quick_pattern_matchers.source: AAO BCSC Ch.5, Table 5-10 and Table 5-11

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: KP diffuse across ENTIRE cornea, extending beyond Arlt's-triangle/midline + fine/stellate morphology + no posterior synechiae + eye white
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Fuchs is the classic textbook example of KP distribution violating the Arlt's-triangle rule. Combine with heterochromia + absent PS to strengthen.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: KP diffuse across ENTIRE cornea, extending beyond Arlt's-triangle/midline + elevated IOP + sectoral/patchy iris atrophy (any of HSV/VZV/CMV pattern present)
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Diffuse/beyond-midline KP distribution is a group-level clue for VIRAL anterior uveitis as a whole (vs granulomatous non-viral). Use IOP intensity, injection level, flare/cell count, PS presence, and antiviral/steroid response to differentiate among the three - see the disease-specific quick_pattern_matchers above/below for that finer discrimination.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Do NOT treat this pattern alone as conclusive - always combine with at least one more discriminating feature (IOP behaviour, injection degree, PS presence/absence, treatment response) before ranking a specific virus.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: KP concentrated in classic inferior triangle (Arlt's triangle), mutton-fat/large, granulomatous
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Arlt's-triangle distribution is suggestive of granulomatous inflammation but is NOT etiology-specific by itself - it does not distinguish infectious (TB/syphilis) from non-infectious (sarcoid/VKH) granulomatous disease. Serology/CXR/IGRA still required to differentiate within this group.
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Never use Arlt's-triangle presence alone to rule OUT infectious causes (TB, syphilis) - always screen serology/IGRA regardless of a 'typical non-infectious-looking' granulomatous KP pattern.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + minimal ciliary injection + limited flare/few cells + poor response to steroid/aciclovir
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Consider Posner-Schlossman syndrome. Contrast with herpes_simplex_anterior_uveitis/vzv_anterior_uveitis which also raise IOP but usually with more prominent inflammation and a positive antiviral history/response.

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: unilateral recurrent anterior uveitis, SAME eye each time + elevated IOP + sectoral/patchy iris atrophy + past herpetic keratitis or cold sores
anterior uveitis 1-3 weeks after ipsilateral shingles rash + elevated IOP + sectoral iris atrophy + reduced corneal sensation
recent cataract surgery/trauma + elevated IOP + visible lens fragment + corneal oedema adjacent to fragment
elevated IOP noted, but patient currently ON topical/regional steroid therapy
quick_pattern_matchers.action: Consider IATROGENIC steroid-response IOP elevation before attributing to disease-specific mechanism - does not reliably discriminate between entities in this state

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: hypopyon + mobile + white eye
hypopyon + immobile + red painful eye
heterochromia + stellate KP + no PS + white eye
child + asymptomatic uveitis + arthritis
satellite lesion adjacent to old chorioretinal scar
HIV/immunocompromised + pizza-pie retinitis
immunocompromised + rapid necrosis + minimal inflammation
healthy adult + necrotizing retinitis + prominent panuveitis
leukocoria or strabismus in child with retinal granuloma
tinnitus/vertigo + bilateral granulomatous panuveitis + exudative RD
prior penetrating trauma + fellow eye now inflamed
serpiginous/geographic choroiditis pattern
quick_pattern_matchers.note: MUST rule out TB and syphilis before labeling idiopathic serpiginous choroidopathy

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: young myopic woman + MEWDS-like but recurrent/overlapping
wide field placoid posterior lesions + neuro symptoms
anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + minimal injection/flare/cells + PS rare + no response to aciclovir/steroid
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Non-response to treatment can itself be the diagnostic clue for CMV anterior uveitis

quick_pattern_matchers.pattern: anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + sectoral iris atrophy + history of ipsilateral HZO rash 1-3 weeks prior
anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + patchy/sectoral iris atrophy + recurrent SAME eye + history of HSV keratitis/cold sores
anterior uveitis + elevated IOP + recent cataract surgery + visible lens fragment
chronic unilateral anterior uveitis + heterochromia + stellate KP + NO PS + eye white + glaucoma present/developing
quick_pattern_matchers.note: Glaucoma in FUS develops in up to 60% of eyes, typically a LATE manifestation

iop_reference_table.purpose: Quick-reference summary of IOP behaviour across uveitis entities - useful as a scoring signal since tonometry is universally available even in resource-limited settings (unlike HLA typing, PCR, etc.)
iop_reference_table.general_principle: Kanski notes IOP in anterior uveitis 'may be reduced as the result of impairment of aqueous secretion by the ciliary epithelium, or elevated due to a variety of mechanisms, including the use of topical steroids.' So IOP direction alone is not pathognomonic, but PATTERN + CONTEXT is discriminative.

mechanism_detail_aao.low_iop_mechanism: With uveitic involvement of ciliary body/trabecular meshwork, IOP is often LOW, secondary to DECREASED AQUEOUS PRODUCTION or INCREASED UVEOSCLERAL OUTFLOW - i.e. the more common baseline tendency in active uveitis is actually toward LOWER IOP, not higher.
high_iop_mechanisms: Trabecular meshwork clogged by inflammatory cells/debris

Trabeculitis - the trabecular meshwork ITSELF is the site of inflammation - CHARACTERISTICALLY associated with HERPETIC uveitis (directly explains why herpes_simplex_anterior_uveitis, vzv_anterior_uveitis, and cmv_anterior_uveitis all show elevated IOP as a hallmark - this is the underlying mechanism, not just a coincidental association)
Pupillary block with iris bombe and secondary angle closure
mechanism_detail_aao.clinical_use: This mechanistic detail explains WHY our elevated-IOP quick_pattern_matchers for viral anterior uveitis are clinically sound (trabeculitis mechanism), and also clarifies that LOW IOP is actually the more common/expected finding in uncomplicated active anterior uveitis generally - so a case with LOW IOP does not particularly discriminate between entities, while ELEVATED IOP is the more informationally-rich finding to score on.

iop_reference_table.posner_schlossman_cross_reference: See new entity posner_schlossman_syndrome (glaucomatocyclitic crisis) - AAO Table 5-10 groups this with herpetic iritis under the SAME 'increased IOP' branch point of the anterior uveitis differential, alongside uveitis_glaucoma_hyphema_syndrome (pseudophakic-specific). All three should be considered together whenever IOP spike is the dominant presenting feature.

entities_with_IOP_ELEVATION_as_a_notable.strength: strong - very common, often with minimal other inflammatory signs; a hallmark feature
moderate - common finding alongside sectoral/patchy iris atrophy
moderate - common finding alongside sectoral iris atrophy, reduced corneal sensation
moderate-strong - frequently elevated, associated with corneal oedema near lens fragment
mentioned - IOP may be elevated with roseolae/secondary syphilis anterior uveitis
mentioned - elevated IOP may follow spill-over anterior uveitis
strong but CHRONIC/LATE - glaucoma develops in up to 60% of eyes over time, not necessarily an early/acute sign
complication-level - glaucoma is a common serious COMPLICATION of longstanding disease, not an early diagnostic clue
complication-level - glaucoma listed among typical complications
complication-level - glaucoma listed among ocular complications (with macular neovascularization, subretinal fibrosis, vitreous haemorrhage, cataract)
complication-level, END-STAGE only - glaucoma appears among end-stage disease complications (with optic/retinal atrophy, PS, cataract); NOT a feature of acute Behcet presentation

iop_reference_table.iatrogenic_caution: Topical/regional/intraocular steroid treatment itself commonly causes elevated IOP ('steroid responders') - this is a TREATMENT SIDE EFFECT, not a diagnostic clue, and must be distinguished from disease-related IOP elevation, especially in patients already on steroid therapy for their uveitis. Regional steroid injection carries up to 25% risk of refractory IOP elevation.
iop_reference_table.clinical_utility_note: Because tonometry is essentially universally available (unlike HLA typing, PCR, ICGA), 'elevated IOP present/absent' is a HIGH-VALUE, LOW-COST checkbox to include prominently in the clinical input form - it meaningfully narrows the viral anterior uveitis differential (HSV/VZV/CMV) even when advanced investigations are not accessible.

source_conflict_log.purpose: Explicit record of points where Kanski Ch.12 and AAO BCSC Section 9 (2025-2026) genuinely differ, rather than silently merging into a single hybrid recommendation neither source actually makes.

conflicts.kanski_position: Kanski Ch.12: 'Retinal vasculitis - arteritis as well as phlebitis, in contrast to pure venous involvement in sarcoidosis - can manifest with sheathing, perivascular haemorrhages and occlusion.' Explicitly states Behcet involves BOTH arteritis and phlebitis.
conflicts.aao_position: AAO BCSC Table 5-4 ('Diseases With Retinal Vasculitis') classifies Behcet disease under 'Primarily Phlebitis' column, separate from the 'Arteritis and Phlebitis' column (which lists ocular toxoplasmosis, relapsing polychondritis, GPA, Crohn disease, frosted branch angiitis instead).
conflicts.resolution: NOT resolved - both are shown to the user. This may reflect genuine disease heterogeneity (Behcet vasculitis severity/pattern varies by case) rather than a factual error in either source.

conflicts.topic: Behcet disease - TNF inhibitor treatment line
conflicts.kanski_position: Kanski Ch.12 (citing EULAR 2008 guidelines): systemic steroids + azathioprine (2.5mg/kg/day) recommended as INITIAL management for posterior uveitis in Behcet. Infliximab/adalimumab positioned as 'early' escalation (American Uveitis Society recommendation), implying second-line-but-prompt use. [CONFIRMED as reflecting the EULAR panel specifically]
conflicts.aao_position: AAO BCSC 2025-2026 (more recent): 'According to expert consensus, TNF inhibitors should be considered FIRST-LINE treatment for Behcet disease' - explicit, unambiguous first-line positioning, not escalation. [CONFIRMED as reflecting a specific AMERICAN expert panel, explicitly contrasted by AAO itself against the EULAR position]
conflicts.resolution: CONFIRMED via AAO BCSC Ch.10 (not speculation anymore): this is an explicit, acknowledged disagreement between an AMERICAN expert panel (TNF inhibitors infliximab/adalimumab as first-OR-second-line) and a EUROPEAN LEAGUE AGAINST RHEUMATISM panel (azathioprine+steroids first-line, cyclosporine/infliximab second-line). AAO itself presents BOTH positions side by side without picking one as superior. Kanski (UK-based textbook) aligns with the EULAR position. This is a genuine geographic/organizational difference in expert consensus, not outdated-vs-current guidance - both remain actively used depending on region/panel followed.

source_conflict_log.note_for_tool_ui: When a disease entity has an entry in this log, the UI should surface BOTH positions with clear source attribution, not present a single blended recommendation.

sun_official_reference.source: AAO BCSC Section 9 (2025-2026), Chapter 5, citing Jabs DA, Nussenblatt RB, Rosenbaum JT; SUN Working Group. Am J Ophthalmol. 2005;140(3):509-516.

anatomic_classification_official_rule.critical_correction: OFFICIAL SUN RULE (supersedes/clarifies our earlier informal anatomic_class_reference): If both anterior chamber AND vitreous inflammatory cells are present, but the amount of vitreous inflammation is MORE than expected for an isolated anterior uveitis, the correct classification is 'ANTERIOR AND INTERMEDIATE UVEITIS' - NOT 'panuveitis'. Panuveitis and posterior uveitis BOTH REQUIRE choroidal and/or retinal lesions to be present - structural complications alone (e.g. macular edema) or vitreous/anterior cells alone are NOT sufficient to classify as posterior uveitis or panuveitis.
anatomic_classification_official_rule.practical_implication_for_scoring: Our checkbox-based anatomic_class scoring (see anatomic_class_reference) should treat 'panuveitis' as requiring genuine simultaneous anterior+intermediate+posterior/choroidal-retinal involvement with NO single predominant site - not just 'multiple compartments checked'. A case with anterior uveitis + heavy vitritis but NO chorioretinal lesion should score toward 'anterior + intermediate' disease entities, NOT toward panuveitis entities like VKH/sympathetic ophthalmia/Behcet.

panuveitis.primary_site: anterior chamber + vitreous + retina/choroid, with NO predominant site

course.acute: episode characterized by sudden onset AND limited duration
course.recurrent: repeated episodes separated by periods of inactivity WITHOUT treatment >=3 months in duration
course.chronic: persistent uveitis with relapse in <3 months after discontinuing treatment

onset_duration_course_table_5_2.cross_check_vs_kanski: Matches Kanski's SUN-based definitions already in our reference.md - no conflict, this is the same official SUN table cited independently by both sources.

activity_terminology_table_5_3.worsening_activity: 2-step increase in inflammation level (e.g. AC cells, vitreous haze) OR increase from grade 3+ to 4+
activity_terminology_table_5_3.improved_activity: 2-step decrease in inflammation level OR decrease to grade 0
activity_terminology_table_5_3.remission: Inactive disease for >=3 months AFTER DISCONTINUING all treatment for eye disease

ac_cell_flare_grading_table_5_7.note: Official SUN grading, 1x1mm high-intensity slit beam at 45-60deg angle, dark room
ac_cell_flare_grading_table_5_7.cross_check_vs_kanski: Identical to Kanski Table 12.2/12.3 - both cite the same SUN source, no conflict.

vitreous_cell_haze_grading_table_5_8.note: MUST trial grading, 1x0.5mm high-intensity slit beam, retrolental space.
