NOTE: SUN system itself does NOT specify a vitreous cell grading system - this MUST-trial scale is a commonly used de facto standard, distinct from the NIH/SUN vitreous HAZE grading (0-4, based on posterior pole clarity via indirect ophthalmoscopy with 20D lens).

granulomatous_vs_nongranulomatous_clarif.important_caveat: AAO explicitly cautions: granulomatous vs nongranulomatous clinical appearance does NOT necessarily correlate with histologic appearance, and may be influenced by disease stage, antigen load at presentation, or treatment stage (e.g. after starting corticosteroids). A uveitis may present granulomatous and become nongranulomatous-appearing with chronicity/treatment. This means our granulomatous:true/false field should be treated as a MODERATE-confidence signal, not a hard classifier.
granulomatous_vs_nongranulomatous_clarif.variable_granulomatous_or_not: Viral anterior uveitis (HSV/VZV/CMV) may present with EITHER granulomatous or nongranulomatous appearance - already reflected in our herpes/vzv/cmv anterior uveitis entities as 'variable'.

retinal_vasculitis_type_reference_table_.note: IMPORTANT: retinal vasculitis (perivascular sheathing/leakage/occlusion on FA) is explicitly NOT a defining feature for anatomical classification of ANY uveitis type per SUN - it's a descriptive finding layered on top, not a classifier.
retinal_vasculitis_type_reference_table_.conflict_flag: See source_conflict_log - Behcet is 'Primarily Phlebitis' here per AAO, but Kanski describes Behcet as having BOTH arteritis and phlebitis.
retinal_vasculitis_type_reference_table_.scoring_use: Can be used as an additional discriminative signal: if user reports arterial-predominant vasculitis on FA, downweight sarcoidosis/MS/birdshot/Eales in favor of SLE/PAN/syphilis/ARN/IRVAN/Susac/Churg-Strauss; if venous-predominant, upweight sarcoidosis/MS/birdshot/Eales.

epidemiology_reference.global_blindness_burden: ~10% of blindness in US/Europe; up to 25% of blindness worldwide
epidemiology_reference.prevalence: 58-121 per 100,000 in the US; up to 730 per 100,000 in the developing world
epidemiology_reference.anatomic_distribution_base_rates: Anterior uveitis is most common (70-80% of cases), followed by panuveitis, posterior uveitis, then intermediate uveitis - USEFUL AS A BAYESIAN PRIOR for scoring: absent other strong signals, anterior uveitis diagnoses should carry a slight base-rate edge.
epidemiology_reference.age_sex: Peak incidence 20-60 years old; risk also increased >65yo per recent data; women slightly higher rates overall
epidemiology_reference.children_vs_adults: Uveitis prevalence is ~5-10 fold LOWER in children than adults overall (context for our age_group scoring - 'anak'/'remaja' should generally score lower base-rate across most entities except the specific pediatric-predominant ones we already tagged, e.g. JIA)
geographic_associations: Developing countries have higher rates of infectious uveitis, posterior uveitis, and panuveitis than industrialized nations

treatment_algorithm_general_aao.source: AAO BCSC Section 9 (2025-2026), Chapter 6: Therapy for Uveitis
treatment_algorithm_general_aao.core_principle: Infectious causes of uveitis MUST be investigated and excluded/treated BEFORE corticosteroids (especially periocular/intravitreal forms) are administered. This matches Kanski's TB/syphilis treatment-alongside-steroid caution, generalized as a universal rule.
seven_step_algorithm_noninfectious_disea: 2. Quiet the inflammation with some form of corticosteroid. If inflammation WORSENS on steroid, reconsider an infectious etiology.
3. Slowly taper corticosteroids off completely OR down to a medically safe dose (max 7.5mg/day oral prednisone, or a topical dose low enough to avoid ocular side effects)
4. If inflammation recurs on taper, escalate to a longer-acting/stronger option: topical -> periocular/intravitreal/systemic

5. When corticosteroids are maximized or cannot be continued, add systemic corticosteroid-sparing IMT. Antimetabolites have historically been first-line IMT; in certain cases a biologic (usually TNF inhibitor) or, less commonly, an alkylating agent may be used FIRST instead.

6. IMT takes time (weeks-months) to reach therapeutic effect and not every agent works for every patient/disease - may need to switch agents. When effective and tolerated, IMT is maintained for AT LEAST 1-3 years.
7. For certain diseases with systemic manifestations, IMT may be indicated as FIRST-LINE treatment from the start (not after steroid failure) - see early_imt_indication_list.

topical.general: First-line for anterior uveitis, given once daily to hourly depending on severity; ointment form available for nighttime use
topical.difluprednate_0.05%: Highly potent, deeper tissue penetration; 4 drops/day considered equivalent to 8+ drops/day of prednisolone acetate 1%. MORE frequent/severe ocular hypertension risk, especially in children.
topical.loteprednol_fluorometholone: LOWEST ocular hypertensive effect among topical options, but LESS effective than prednisolone acetate for more severe uveitis - efficacy/safety tradeoff

periocular.agents: Triamcinolone acetonide 40mg or methylprednisolone acetate 40-80mg (depot); dexamethasone/betamethasone (short-acting, subconjunctival) for limited-duration effect
techniques: Sub-Tenon (Nozik technique) - superotemporal or inferotemporal approach
periocular.caution: 'Saw-tooth decline' phenomenon: relapses before scheduled reinjection cause CUMULATIVE damage even though each injection produces visible improvement - can be missed without careful longitudinal analysis. Scheduled reinjection BEFORE effect wears off (not waiting for relapse) may improve long-term prognosis in chronic cases.

systemic_oral.drug_of_choice: Prednisone (NOT the blister-pack methylprednisolone taper - dose too low, duration too short for uveitis)
systemic_oral.starting_dose: 1 mg/kg/day, up to a MAXIMUM of 60 mg/day (doses above 60mg/day avoided - increased avascular bone necrosis risk)
systemic_oral.safe_maintenance_threshold: 7.5 mg/day or less is considered safe intermediate/long-term (though some data suggest increased cardiovascular risk even at low cumulative doses like 5mg/day over 20 years)
systemic_oral.imt_trigger: If prednisone CANNOT be tapered to <=7.5mg/day within 3 months, systemic IMT is typically initiated

iv_pulse.indication: Most aggressive immediate treatment for SEVERE noninfectious uveitis or scleritis
iv_pulse.protocol: Methylprednisolone 1 g/day infused over 1 hour, for 3 days, followed by gradual oral prednisone taper starting at 1mg/kg/day
iv_pulse.caution: Should ONLY be administered by a physician experienced with the approach - multiple adverse effects observed, some life-threatening
iv_pulse.cross_check_vs_kanski: Compatible with Kanski's VKH protocol (IV methylprednisolone 500-1000mg/day) - AAO's 1g/day falls within/matches that range, not a conflict, just AAO standardizing on the upper end as 'the' protocol.

prophylaxis_notes: NSAID + systemic corticosteroid combination increases gastric ulcer risk - best avoided; if risk factors present, add H2-antagonist or PPI
Calcium + vitamin D supplementation + DEXA bone density screening for long-term systemic steroid users
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole every-other-day as Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis when on prednisone >=20mg/day for >4 weeks, or on alkylating agents/prolonged high-dose steroid+IMT combinations

diseases: Necrotizing scleritis associated with systemic vasculitis
early_imt_indication_list_aao.cross_check_vs_our_kb: Consistent with what we already tagged from Kanski for vkh_syndrome, sympathetic_ophthalmia, behcet_uveitis, serpiginous_choroidopathy - good cross-source validation. NEW additions from this list not yet in our KB: mucous membrane pemphigoid (not a uveitis entity per se, external disease - out of scope for this KB), necrotizing scleritis with systemic vasculitis (relevant once we add scleritis chapter).

imt_general_principles.onset_of_action: Nonbiologic IMT (antimetabolites, T-cell inhibitors, alkylating agents) takes WEEKS TO MONTHS for effect - unlike biologics which act faster. Patients may need >1 agent trial to find effective/tolerated regimen - this refinement process may take 6-18 months.
imt_general_principles.duration_before_taper: Quiescent on IMT + lowest steroid dose for >=12 months (LONGER for pediatric patients) before considering slow IMT taper
pre_initiation_workup: Baseline objective disease activity measure for longitudinal monitoring
imt_general_principles.vaccination_timing: All routine vaccines/boosters ideally given 2 weeks before IMT start (4 weeks for LIVE vaccines). Also recommended: pneumococcal vaccine (regardless of age), inactivated zoster vaccine, annual inactivated influenza, COVID-19 vaccine (additional doses per evolving CDC guidance for immunosuppressed).
imt_general_principles.fertility_counseling: Required for all patients before IMT start. Methotrexate and mycophenolate mofetil have ABSOLUTE pregnancy contraindications - must be discontinued (both men and women) at least 3 months before attempting conception.
imt_general_principles.malignancy_data_reassurance: SITE cohort study (15,938 patients, 187,151 person-years): azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, cyclosporine, systemic corticosteroids, AND TNF inhibitors were NOT associated with increased overall or cancer-related mortality. Alkylating agents showed higher overall mortality but this lost significance after confounder adjustment. Some data suggest IMT (as a class) may increase NONMELANOMA skin cancer risk - sunscreen counseling advised.

tnf_inhibitor_specific_notes.expert_consensus_first_line_for_behcet: TNF inhibitors (specifically adalimumab, infliximab - the best-studied) should be considered FIRST-LINE treatment for Behcet disease per AAO expert consensus - see source_conflict_log for comparison with Kanski/EULAR-2008 framing.
best_studied_agents: Adalimumab - fully human antibody, FIRST FDA-APPROVED systemic medication for noninfectious uveitis (posterior segment)
Infliximab - mouse/human chimeric antibody, higher antidrug-antibody formation risk than adalimumab
tnf_inhibitor_specific_notes.not_recommended: Etanercept is explicitly stated as NOT effective for ocular inflammatory disease (cross-check: matches Kanski's JIA section which also says 'Etanercept should be avoided as it can result in severe worsening of inflammation' - CONFIRMED, no conflict, both sources agree)
tnf_inhibitor_specific_notes.adalimumab_dosing: Subcutaneous 80mg initial dose, then 40mg every other week starting 1 week after initial injection (may increase to weekly). Co-administration with methotrexate increases serum levels and reduces antidrug-antibody formation.
tnf_inhibitor_specific_notes.infliximab_dosing: IV 5-10mg/kg at weeks 0, 2, 6, then maintenance every 4-8 weeks. Up to 20mg/kg may be used in children. Low-dose methotrexate (5-7.5mg/week) co-administration reduces infliximab-induced lupus syndrome risk and antidrug-antibody formation.
tnf_inhibitor_specific_notes.contraindication: Relatively contraindicated in congestive heart failure. Latent TB must be excluded/treated (with infectious disease specialist oversight) before starting. Risk of CNS demyelination (unmasking MS), hepatitis B reactivation. Avoid live vaccines while on TNF inhibitors.

other_biologics.tocilizumab: Anti-IL-6; STOP-Uveitis trial showed improved VA/reduced vitreous haze; APTITUDE trial (JIA-uveitis refractory to TNF-inhibitor) did not meet primary outcome but several retrospective series show benefit for refractory uveitic macular edema specifically
other_biologics.rituximab: Anti-CD20 (B-cell depleting), IV every 6 months; useful for Behcet retinal vasculitis, GPA/RA-associated scleritis, mucous membrane pemphigoid, refractory JIA-uveitis (case series)
other_biologics.interferon_alfa_2a_2b: Subcutaneous; ~90% inflammation control in Behcet uveitis (European literature), ~60% in non-Behcet uveitis. Flu-like syndrome common (reducible with prophylactic acetaminophen); leukopenia/thrombocytopenia and DEPRESSION are notable adverse effects. Must discontinue other IMT before starting.
other_biologics.abatacept: T-cell costimulation inhibitor; mixed results in JIA-uveitis (14-49% sustained control across studies)

immunomodulatory_drug_reference_aao.note: Adult dosing shown. This is materially MORE PRECISE than what Kanski Ch.12 provides (Kanski lists drug classes/examples without this level of dosing detail) - treat as ENRICHMENT, not conflict.

methotrexate.pregnancy: HIGH RISK (spontaneous abortion, fetal abnormalities) - absolute contraindication

azathioprine.monitoring: Check TPMT enzyme activity BEFORE initiation (genotypic test - low/no activity in 0.3% of patients = azathioprine NOT recommended); then CBC/CMP monthly x3 months, then every 6-12 weeks
azathioprine.pregnancy: MINIMAL risk (preterm birth, IUGR) - relatively safer than other antimetabolites in pregnancy

cyclosporine.monitoring: CBC, CMP every 6-12 weeks; blood pressure
cyclosporine.pregnancy: Minimal risk (preterm birth, small for gestational age)

tacrolimus.monitoring: CBC, CMP every 6-12 weeks; tacrolimus trough levels
tacrolimus.pregnancy: Minimal risk (preterm birth, small for gestational age)

cyclophosphamide.monitoring: CBC and urinalysis weekly to monthly

adalimumab.dosing: SC 80mg day 1, 40mg day 8, then 40mg every other week; may increase to weekly
adalimumab.monitoring: Interval varies; ongoing monitoring for neuro/cardiovascular symptoms; anti-drug antibody testing for efficacy loss; annual TB test
adalimumab.fda_status: FIRST FDA-APPROVED systemic medication for noninfectious uveitis (posterior segment)

infliximab.dosing: IV 5-10 mg/kg induction at weeks 0, 2, 6, then maintenance every 4-8 weeks; may increase up to 20mg/kg in children
infliximab.note: Certolizumab (another TNF inhibitor, less-studied in uveitis) has LESS third-trimester transplacental transfer than other TNF inhibitors - relevant if pregnancy planning is a factor

appendix_a_review_of_systems_checklist_a.source: AAO BCSC Section 9 (2025-2026), Appendix A - Diagnostic Survey for Uveitis (adapted by AAO from Foster CS, Vitale AT. Diagnosis and Treatment of Uveitis. 2nd ed. 2012:123-128)
appendix_a_review_of_systems_checklist_a.purpose: A validated, structured patient self-administered questionnaire. Directly usable as the structured checklist backing our input_schema's currently free-text 'systemic_symptoms' and 'travel_or_exposure_history' fields - replaces vague free text with a concrete, comprehensive checklist.
family_history: Multiple sclerosis or other autoimmune disease
social_history: Raw meat or uncooked sausage consumption
Same-sex sexual relations or partner who engages in same-sex relations
personal_medical_history: Prior eye surgeries (including laser) with dates
appendix_a_review_of_systems_checklist_a.implementation_note: This checklist is comprehensive by design (it's a full uveitis-specific ROS, not just eye-focused). For our tool's Section C (Riwayat & Konteks), recommend presenting this as collapsible sub-groups matching the categories above, all still OPTIONAL per our core parsial-input design principle - this is a superset menu to pick from, not a mandatory form.

appendix_b_intravitreal_antimicrobial_re.source: AAO BCSC Section 9 (2025-2026), Appendix B - Antimicrobial Agents for Intraocular Injection
appendix_b_intravitreal_antimicrobial_re.cross_validation_note: Clindamycin 1mg/0.1mL for intravitreal use MATCHES Kanski's toxoplasmosis_retinitis treatment entry exactly (Kanski: 'Intravitreal therapy with clindamycin (1 mg) and dexamethasone (400 ug)') - independent cross-source confirmation, no conflict.

ceftazidime.coverage: Aerobic GNB (e.g. P. aeruginosa); gram-positive bacilli

clindamycin.coverage: Gram-positive cocci (staph, pneumococci); GPB (Bacillus spp); GNB (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium spp); Toxoplasma gondii

amphotericin_b.coverage: Yeasts, filamentous fungi (resistance reported in various Aspergillus species)

foscarnet.repeat_interval: Adjunct to systemic antiviral: 2-3x/week until retinitis stable, then weekly if needed

ganciclovir.repeat_interval: Adjunct to systemic antiviral: 2-3x/week until retinitis stable, then weekly if needed

surgical_management_notes_aao_ch16_parti.source: AAO BCSC Section 9 (2025-2026), Chapter 16 - PARTIALLY reviewed (practical highlights only; full chapter not yet systematically extracted - this chapter covers complication-specific surgical management, e.g. band keratopathy EDTA chelation, cataract surgery timing/technique, glaucoma surgery, uveitic macular edema management - available for a future extraction batch if useful)

cataract_surgery_timing_pearl.general_guideline: Achieve well-controlled uveitis with NO flare-ups for AT LEAST 3 MONTHS before elective cataract surgery in a uveitic eye - reduces risk of severe postoperative inflammation
cataract_surgery_timing_pearl.evidence_caveat: This 3-month guideline is based on retrospective case series and clinical experience only - NO prospective/controlled trial data confirm it as an exact threshold
exceptions: Uveitic disorders with good surgical prognosis (e.g. Fuchs uveitis syndrome - cross-reference our fuchs_uveitis_syndrome entity)
When posterior segment visualization is urgently needed (e.g. rhegmatogenous RD repair)
cataract_surgery_timing_pearl.perioperative_protocol: Oral corticosteroid 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day starting 3 days before surgery, tapered over following weeks; and/or intensive topical corticosteroid; sub-Tenon or intravitreal steroid also options
cataract_surgery_timing_pearl.infectious_uveitis_caveat: For Toxoplasma gondii or herpetic viral uveitis specifically, perioperative PROPHYLACTIC ANTIMICROBIAL therapy (not preoperative oral corticosteroid) is typically used to prevent surgically-induced recurrence - cross-reference toxoplasmosis_retinitis, herpes_simplex_anterior_uveitis, vzv_anterior_uveitis, cmv_anterior_uveitis entries.

surgical_management_notes_aao_ch16_parti.band_keratopathy: Calcium deposition in interpalpebral zone, most common in JIA-associated or undifferentiated chronic anterior uveitis (cross-reference jia_uveitis), can develop within MONTHS of uveitis onset. Treatment: epithelial debridement + disodium EDTA chelation (may recur, needing repeat treatment); photorefractive keratotomy is an alternative.

white_dot_syndrome_comparison_table_aao.source: AAO BCSC Ch.9, Table 9-1 'Selected White Dot Syndromes' - side-by-side comparison across 9 entities. Extremely high scoring value since age/sex/laterality/systemic-association are cheap, universally-collectable data points.

birdshot_chorioretinopathy.structural_complications: retinal vasculitis, disc edema, macular edema, CNV (6%)

serpiginous_choroiditis.structural_complications: CNV (25%), RPE mottling, scarring, loss of choriocapillaris

multifocal_choroiditis_panuveitis.structural_complications: optic disc edema, peripapillary pigment changes, macular edema (14-44%), macular subretinal fibrosis, CNV

punctate_inner_choroidopathy.structural_complications: CNV (17-40%), serous detachment over confluent lesions

subretinal_fibrosis_uveitis_syndrome.structural_complications: neurosensory retinal detachment, macular edema, CNV

noninfectious: Leukemic infiltration of the optic nerve - cross-reference ocular_manifestations_leukemia
neuroretinitis_differential_reference_aa.clinical_use: Macular star + disc edema (neuroretinitis pattern) has a BROAD differential spanning infectious, vascular, neoplastic, and idiopathic causes - Bartonella is well-known but should not be assumed by default without considering this full list, especially the noninfectious/vascular causes in older patients with vascular risk factors.

scleritis_classification_reference_aao.category_note: IMPORTANT: Scleritis is a DISTINCT diagnostic category from uveitis proper (inflammation of the SCLERA, not the uveal tract/intraocular compartments), though it shares MANY of the same systemic disease associations and is a critical differential for 'painful red eye'. Entities in this KB tagged disease_category='scleritis' should be considered ALONGSIDE true uveitis differentials when a patient presents with pain+redness, but are mechanistically/anatomically distinct - do not conflate scleritis anatomic descriptors (anterior/posterior scleritis = LOCATION ON THE SCLERA) with the uveitis anatomic_class framework (which describes INTRAOCULAR compartment).

anterior_scleritis: Necrotizing scleritis (with inflammation: granulomatous, vaso-occlusive, postsurgical/SINS; OR scleromalacia perforans without overt inflammation)
classification_table_7_1.posterior_scleritis: Scleral inflammation posterior to the ora serrata

epidemiology.no_geographic_racial_difference: Unlike many uveitis entities, scleritis shows NO geographic/racial variation in incidence/prevalence
epidemiology.referral_share: 0.1-2.6% of newly referred cases at tertiary centers
epidemiology.systemic_association_rate: ~40% of ALL scleritis cases associated with systemic disease; NECROTIZING scleritis specifically: 50-60% - substantially higher systemic-association rate than scleritis overall

scleritis_classification_reference_aao.key_exam_technique: PHENYLEPHRINE BLANCHING TEST: topical phenylephrine causes vessels to BLANCH in episcleritis (superficial plexus only) but shows PARTIAL OR NO blanching in scleritis (deep episcleral plexus, displaced outward by scleral edema). CAVEAT: non-blanching conjunctival injection can also occur in acute_anterior_uveitis or endophthalmitis, so this test alone is not scleritis-specific - the presence of HIGH-GRADE INTRAOCULAR inflammation helps distinguish those from true scleritis.

investigation_table_7_2.source: AAO Table 7-2 - Investigations for Noninfectious Inflammatory Conditions Associated With Scleritis
investigation_table_7_2.rheumatoid_arthritis: RF, anti-CCP, ESR, CRP, joint radiography, C3, C4
investigation_table_7_2.sle: ANA, CBC, urine sediment, C3, C4
investigation_table_7_2.jia: ANA, HLA-B27, RF, anti-CCP, joint radiography
investigation_table_7_2.reactive_arthritis: HLA-B27, ESR, CRP, joint radiography, cultures (urogenital/throat/stool/synovial fluid)
investigation_table_7_2.granulomatosis_with_polyangiitis: c-ANCA/anti-PR3, tissue biopsy, chest/sinus radiography, urine sediment, C3, C4
investigation_table_7_2.polyarteritis_nodosa: Hepatitis B serology, tissue biopsy, urine sediment
investigation_table_7_2.churg_strauss_egpa: CBC (eosinophilia), p-ANCA/anti-MPO, tissue biopsy, urine sediment, C3, C4
investigation_table_7_2.cogan_syndrome: Hearing test (NEW association not elsewhere in our KB)
investigation_table_7_2.giant_cell_arteritis: ESR, CRP, CBC, temporal artery biopsy (NEW association not elsewhere in our KB)
investigation_table_7_2.takayasu_arteritis: Cardiac catheterization (NEW association not elsewhere in our KB)
investigation_table_7_2.sarcoidosis: ACE (lysozyme if on ACE-inhibitor), CXR/CT, tuberculin skin test or IGRA, tissue biopsy

nsaid_dosing_table_7_3.source: AAO Table 7-3 - NSAID Dosage for Mild-Moderate Noninfectious Anterior Scleritis (NOT for long-term management)
